variants.text:
# AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation
## Measurement Uncertainty Standards, ASME V&V Guides, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework

Every claim an engineer makes about a system is only as credible as the framework used to make it. Professional experimentalists have spent decades building that framework already.

---

The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework introduced in Week 1's lecture is not itself a published standard. It is a course framework assembled from professional engineering standards, measurement guides, and validation references so that ASEN 3501 students have one clear structure for connecting physical experiments to mathematical models. We use it here because you are at the point in the curriculum where experimental methods and computational methods should start to feel connected rather than separate. The AMVF is our way of making those connections visible now, in a form that is broad enough for your current work but still grounded in the same professional foundations you will encounter later in discipline-specific standards, senior design, and capstone-level system work.

This reading is therefore not a list of documents you must master in Week 1. It is a map of the professional sources behind the framework so you know where the language comes from, why practicing engineers use it, and which references will become more important as the course progresses. As your work becomes more specialized, you will lean harder on the standards that fit your particular experimental domain. Right now, the goal is to see that the framework used in this class is not arbitrary. It is a distilled teaching version of a professional standards culture that you will keep encountering in different forms.

No single document governs every experiment in this course. **[ASME PTC 19.1](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/publications-information/performance-test-codes)**, the **[GUM](https://www.bipm.org/en/committees/jc/jcgm/publications)**, and [Coleman & Steele](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epub/10.1002/9781119417989) provide the primary basis for measurement-uncertainty terminology and reporting. ASME's computational-model V&V documents provide useful domain examples: **[ASME V&V 10](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/find-codes-standards/standard-for-verification-and-validation-in-computational-solid-mechanics)** anchors the solid-mechanics side of the conversation, while **[ASME V&V 20](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/find-codes-standards/standard-for-verification-and-validation-in-computational-fluid-dynamics-and-heat-transfer/2009)** addresses computational fluid dynamics and heat transfer. We use their intended-use, verification, validation, and model-credibility ideas where relevant, but we do not treat any one of them as a universal experimental procedure. When you cite a standard, annotate what it contributes to your specific experiment and what remains outside its scope.

![A two-column diagram showing the V&V framework structure. On the left, a "Mathematical Model" branch descends through equation derivation and numerical solution, terminating at "Verification." On the right, a "Physical Model" branch descends through experiment design, data collection, and uncertainty quantification, terminating at "Experimental Outcomes." A horizontal bridge labeled "Validation" connects the two branches at the bottom, with a bidirectional arrow labeled "Quantitative Comparison" spanning the gap. The overall structure mirrors the AMVF flowchart. A label in the lower corner reads "ASME V&V 10-2006." Clean black-on-white engineering diagram style, minimal shading, clear label typography.](../images/E1_W1_R1_image1.png)
*This image makes the course framework visible as two linked branches so you can see exactly where verification stops, where experimental evidence begins, and where validation has to bridge the two.

Beyond V&V 10-2006, practicing aerospace experimentalists draw on a cluster of related documents. The **[AIAA measurement standards](https://aiaa.org/publications/standards/)**, produced by the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, address measurement methodology specific to aerospace applications: wind tunnel testing, flight measurements, and propulsion characterization. These standards specify how uncertainty should be treated in measurement-intensive aerospace environments and will surface explicitly when E2's more complex experimental setups are introduced. Measurement standards aren't generic. Different engineering domains have their own standards that define acceptable practice, and knowing which standard applies to your experiment is part of experimental design.

For uncertainty analysis specifically, the **[ASME PTC (Performance Test Codes) standards](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/publications-information/performance-test-codes)** provide procedural requirements for measurement uncertainty in engineering performance testing. These standards require that every reported result carry explicit uncertainty bounds derived by methods consistent with the standard's prescribed framework. If you're ever asked whether your measurement result is credible in a professional or regulatory context, the answer lives in whether your uncertainty analysis conforms to a recognized standard.

![A layered pyramid diagram with three tiers. The top tier is labeled "Primary Standards" and shows ASME V&V 10-2006 and AIAA measurement standards as formal document icons. The middle tier is labeled "Reference Texts" and shows Coleman & Steele as a textbook icon alongside the ISO GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) as a second document icon. The bottom tier is labeled "Practitioner Tools" and shows icons for lab documentation, MATLAB, and data recording. Arrows point downward from each tier to the tier below, indicating that the upper layers define the rules that lower-layer practice must follow. Flat-design illustration style, CU Boulder gold and black color palette.](../images/E1_W1_R1_image2.png)
*This image shows the hierarchy behind the AMVF so the standards, reference texts, and day-to-day lab tools read as one connected professional stack rather than a random list of sources.

The reference that bridges all of these to classroom practice is **[Coleman & Steele](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epub/10.1002/9781119417989)**, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers* (4th edition). This book is the accessible bridge between the formal standards and the methods you'll use in this course, beginning in Week 2, when partial derivative error propagation and Monte Carlo simulation are introduced. Relevant chapters will be assigned as the course progresses; having the book as a reference from Week 1 means you can follow the lecture directly into its theoretical foundations whenever you want to go deeper.

This is easy to miss on first encounter: the AMVF is a course synthesis, while standards are domain- and purpose-specific sources of accepted terminology and practice. In E3 you will select and justify references that fit your proposed experiment. A citation is useful only when you can say what the source establishes, why it applies, and what it does not answer.

**Reference Links**
- [ASME PTC 19.1 and Performance Test Codes](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/publications-information/performance-test-codes)
- [JCGM GUM publications](https://www.bipm.org/en/committees/jc/jcgm/publications)
- [Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epub/10.1002/9781119417989)
- [ASME V&V 10](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/find-codes-standards/standard-for-verification-and-validation-in-computational-solid-mechanics)
- [ASME V&V 20](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/find-codes-standards/standard-for-verification-and-validation-in-computational-fluid-dynamics-and-heat-transfer/2009)
- [AIAA standards overview](https://aiaa.org/publications/standards/)

---

**The Takeaway:** Use Coleman & Steele, PTC 19.1, and the GUM for measurement-uncertainty practice; use V&V 10 or V&V 20 when their computational domain and intended-use guidance are relevant. In every case, explain the connection rather than citing a standard mechanically.

learning_goals: Locate measurement within the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework (AMVF) and identify the role of each node — CLO 1, CLO 3
Distinguish what a sensor measures from what it infers, and explain how installation effects and bias affect sensor output — CLO 1
Formulate a testable hypothesis and identify independent and dependent variables for their assigned canned experiment — CLO 1
Map a simple experimental workflow to the AMVF flowchart and annotate the diagram with their own design decisions — CLO 3

- [ASME V&V 10](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/find-codes-standards/standard-for-verification-and-validation-in-computational-solid-mechanics)
- [ASME V&V 20](https://www.asme.org/codes-standards/find-codes-standards/standard-for-verification-and-validation-in-computational-fluid-dynamics-and-heat-transfer/2009)
- [AIAA standards overview](https://aiaa.org/publications/standards/)

---

**The Takeaway:** Use Coleman & Steele, PTC 19.1, and the GUM for measurement-uncertainty practice; use V&V 10 or V&V 20 when their computational domain and intended-use guidance are relevant. In every case, explain the connection rather than citing a standard mechanically.

variants.text:
# Technical Writing in Engineering
## Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say

The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping, without hedging or over-explaining, and letting the evidence speak for itself.

---

You have been writing for years. You know how to tell a story, argue a point, describe something you observed. Technical writing uses none of those skills in quite the same way. It's a different genre with different rules, and the rules exist for a specific reason: engineering writing isn't read for enjoyment. It's read by someone who needs to make a decision, verify a result, reproduce a measurement, or check your work. Every sentence exists to support that purpose, or it should not exist at all.

This reading gives you the practical framework for engineering writing that you'll apply throughout this course, starting with the Lab 1 brief report this week.

---

## The Reader You Are Writing For

Before writing a single sentence, ask yourself: *who reads this, and what do they need from it?*

In professional engineering, your reader is a technical person who has never seen your specific work before. They are competent; they understand the physics, the methods, the vocabulary. They are not, however, clairvoyant. They cannot infer what you measured from how you describe your setup. They cannot reconstruct your uncertainty calculation from a result with no error bars. They cannot evaluate your conclusion without knowing how you got there.

**Write for the skeptical expert, not the sympathetic friend.** The sympathetic friend fills in gaps; the skeptical expert points at them. The goal is a document that survives the skeptical expert: one where every claim is supported, every result is reproducible, and no reader has to guess at anything essential.

A second audience worth naming explicitly: the non-engineer. Several assignments in this course ask you to explain a technical result to someone without an engineering background. This is not dumbing down. It's a different technical skill. Plain-language communication requires you to identify the *essential idea* underneath the technical apparatus and express it without jargon. If you cannot do that, it is a signal that your own understanding is not yet complete.

![A two-panel illustration. Left panel, labeled "The Sympathetic Friend," shows a reader leaning forward, nodding, filling in a thought bubble with a complete version of an incomplete sentence: the written text trails off mid-sentence but the reader's thought bubble finishes it generously. Right panel, labeled "The Skeptical Expert," shows a reader with a red pen, question marks in their thought bubble, circling the same incomplete sentence and writing "Insufficient detail — how was this measured?" Clean editorial illustration style, black and white with one accent color.](../images/E1_W2_R2_image1.png)
*This image captures the audience shift the reading is asking for: your draft cannot rely on a friendly reader who fills in missing logic for you.

### Worked Example: From Engineering Form to Plain Language

Here is the same result written two ways.

**Engineering form (for the skeptical expert):**
"The measured electrical power dissipated in a 5 Ω resistor with a 12 V input is 25.7 ± 1.3 W at 95% confidence, derived using partial derivative uncertainty propagation from voltage and current measurements with a ±0.2 V voltmeter and ±0.05 A ammeter."

This version is correct and complete. Every number is supported. Every bound is traceable to a method. A colleague can reproduce the result.

**Plain-language version (for the non-engineer):**
> "We measured how much electrical power a small resistor used when connected to a 12-volt source. It used about 26 watts, roughly the power used by a dim incandescent bulb. Because the meters are only specified to be accurate within a certain range, the actual value is probably somewhere between about 24 and 27 watts."

**What changed and why:**
- *Uncertainty expressed as consequence, not as a statistical bound.* "±1.3 W at 95% confidence" is meaningless to a non-engineer; "between about 24 and 27 watts" states the practical consequence in plain language.
- *Physical result grounded in an everyday reference.* "25.7 W" is a number; "roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses" is a physical anchor that makes the magnitude interpretable.
- *Technical method translated.* The non-engineer reader does not need the phrase "Type B uncertainty." They need to know that the range comes from documented limits on the measuring instruments, not from a guess.

The skeptical expert version and the plain-language version contain the same core result. Writing both isn't redundant. It's evidence that you understand the result well enough to translate it. Several deliverables in this course require both: the Lab 1 brief report, the Phase 1 report (Section 9), and the Phase 3 presentation.

---

## The Architecture of a Technical Document

Every technical document (lab report, memo, technical note) is a narrative. Not a story with plot and character, but a logical argument that has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Readers orient by structure. A document without clear structure wastes the reader's time.

The standard structure for an engineering report maps directly onto the logical arc of a technical argument:

**Introduction** — Three things, in this order: (1) what this document is about and why it was written; (2) what new information it contains that the reader did not have before; (3) why the results matter. The introduction is not background for its own sake; it is a contract with the reader about what they are about to receive.

**Body** — The evidence: your method, your measurements, your analysis. Sufficient detail that someone else could reproduce your results. This is where your figures, tables, and equations live, each one integrated into the narrative, not appended to it.

**Conclusion** — Not a summary of the introduction. A conclusion states: (1) what was found; (2) what that finding implies; (3) what remains uncertain or unresolved; (4) what comes next. Conclusions that merely restate the introduction have done nothing.

If a section of your report does not serve one of these three purposes, it probably should not be there.

---

## Figures: The Core Unit of Technical Communication

Most technical writing errors are not in the prose. They are in the figures and in the prose that surrounds them. A figure in an engineering report is not a visual aid. It is an argument. It exists to support a specific claim, and every element of the figure must serve that claim.

When you produce any figure in this course, the following elements are required, not optional:

- **Labeled axes with units.** "Force (N)" not "Force." "Time (s)" not "seconds." No axis is unlabeled, no unit is omitted.
- **A caption that identifies and explains.** The caption must name every plotted element and explain what the figure shows. "Figure 2. Measured power vs. voltage for all three configurations" is a label. "Figure 2. Measured power as a function of applied voltage for the P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R configurations. Error bars represent ±1σ from partial-derivative uncertainty propagation. The three configurations converge at low voltages but diverge above 5V, consistent with resistance variation as a function of temperature." That is a caption.
- **Reference in the text.** Every figure is referenced before it appears. "Figure 2 shows..." or "as shown in Figure 2..." Never let a figure float in the document without a textual anchor that explains why it is there.
- **Conclusions drawn explicitly.** Do not leave the reader to extract the point. After referencing the figure, state what it shows, what it implies, and whether any anomalies require explanation.

![Two versions of the same MATLAB-style scatter plot with a best-fit line. Top version, labeled "Insufficient," has unlabeled axes ("x" and "y"), no units, a title that says "Data," and no caption below. Bottom version, labeled "Sufficient," has the same data but with fully labeled axes ("Applied Voltage (V)" and "Measured Power (W)"), a legend, error bars on data points, a figure number, and a two-sentence caption that names the trend and identifies what the error bars represent. Same data, entirely different level of communication. Clean technical diagram style.](../images/E1_W2_R2_image2.png)
*This image shows how much explanatory work a figure has to do before it can stand on its own in a technical report.

---

## The Language of Technical Writing

Engineering writing has a distinctive voice, and it is not stuffy or passive by accident. The conventions exist because precision matters more than personality.

**No contractions.** "Do not" not "don't." "Cannot" not "can't." This isn't pedantry; contractions signal informality and undermine technical credibility in a professional document.

**Define before you use.** Every acronym is spelled out on first use: "National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)." Every non-obvious symbol is defined. Assume the reader can look up a word; do not assume they will know what your variable names mean.

**Be specific.** Vague claims invite skepticism. "The measurement was close to the predicted value" tells the reader nothing useful. "The measured power (4.32 W) fell within the 95% confidence interval of the propagated uncertainty estimate (4.18 to 4.51 W)" makes a falsifiable claim. Every quantitative result should be stated with its uncertainty in engineering form: *estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level).*

**Active voice, lean sentences.** "The apparatus was connected, and three sets of data were collected" is cleaner than "I plugged in the apparatus and successfully collected three sets of data." The passive voice is appropriate when the actor is irrelevant or unknown, not as a default. The goal is directness, not impersonality.

**Remove what does not earn its place.** Technical writing is edited ruthlessly. Words like "very," "basically," "actually," "in order to," "it should be noted that," and "as previously mentioned" add length without adding meaning. The revision process is not about polishing prose. It's about cutting everything that does not serve the reader's need to understand your result.

---

## Numbers, Equations, and Units

Numbers and equations follow conventions that are enforced uniformly in professional engineering writing, and they will be enforced in this course.

- **Space between number and unit.** Write `9.81 m/s²` not `9.81m/s²`. Write `2.4 mm` not `2.4mm`. No exceptions.
- **Consistent units.** Do not switch between metric and imperial mid-document. If your data is in N, your results are in N.
- **Variables are italicized; numbers and units are not.** The variable *P* represents power; the value 4.32 W is a measurement. Only variables are italicized.
- **Inline units may remain plain text.** Use standard SI-style prose such as `4.32 W`, `9.81 m/s²`, and `t = 7 s`; do not invent a second bracketed-unit convention for ordinary sentences. For a compact reference, see the [NIST unit-writing checklist](https://physics.nist.gov/cuu/Units/checklist.html).
- **Equations are part of the text.** Introduce every equation in a sentence: "Power dissipated in the resistor is given by..." Equations aren't standalone objects; they are statements that the surrounding prose must set up and interpret.
- **Every result carries uncertainty.** A number without uncertainty bounds is not an engineering result; it is a reading. State method, confidence level, and bounds every time.

---

## The Revision Mindset

Good technical writing isn't written; it's revised. The document you produce in the first pass is a draft that organizes your thinking, not a product that communicates it. Every professional communicator, engineers included, treats revision as the actual work.

A practical three-pass approach: **First pass**, check structure. Does the document have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion that form a complete logical argument? **Second pass**, check claims. Is every quantitative statement supported? Is every figure correctly referenced and captioned? Is every conclusion drawn explicitly from evidence? **Third pass**, check language. Are there contractions? Vague terms? Redundant words? Sentences that could be cut in half without losing meaning?

This course's use of AI for drafting does not change this framework. It intensifies it. An AI-generated draft may have perfect grammar and confident tone while being subtly wrong about the physics, imprecise about the uncertainty, or logically disconnected in its conclusions. **Your job isn't to produce the draft; it is to know what right looks like so you can recognize when the draft is wrong.** The revision mindset is the professional skill this course is building.

![A horizontal flow diagram with three labeled stages. Stage 1, "Draft," shows a rough document icon with a thought bubble that says "organized my ideas." Stage 2, "Revision," shows a document with red ink marks, strikethroughs, and margin notes — labels point to specific types of edits: "structural," "claims," "language." Stage 3, "Final," shows a clean document icon with a checkmark. An arrow beneath all three stages reads: "The revision IS the work." Flat illustration style, clean typography, CU Boulder gold accent color on the arrow.](../images/E1_W2_R2_image3.png)
*This image reinforces the reading's main claim that revision is not cosmetic cleanup but the actual process of turning early thinking into usable engineering communication.

---

## A Checklist for Every Deliverable

Before submitting any written work in this course, confirm:

- [ ] Every figure has labeled axes with units, a complete caption, and an explicit reference in the text
- [ ] Every quantitative result is stated in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level)
- [ ] The introduction states purpose, new information, and significance
- [ ] The conclusion states findings, implications, uncertainties, and next steps, not a restatement of the introduction
- [ ] No contractions appear anywhere in the document
- [ ] Every acronym is defined on first use; every symbol is defined before use
- [ ] There is a space between every number and its unit
- [ ] Units are consistent throughout
- [ ] Every claim that needs evidence has it
- [ ] The document has been read aloud at least once; sentences that are hard to say aloud are hard to read

---

**The Takeaway:** Technical writing is not a soft skill bolted onto engineering. It is the mechanism by which your results become claims that others can evaluate, reproduce, and build on. A result that cannot be communicated clearly is a result that does not yet exist as engineering knowledge.

---

*CLO Alignment: CLO 5 — Communicate experimental results effectively using standard technical writing conventions*
*Assigned: E1, Week 2 | Companion deliverable: Lab 1 Brief Report*
*Sources: Write More Good Club Technical Writing Checklist (UTEP/Rumpf Lab); Technical Writer's Checklist, TSC 2007; ASEN 2003 Lab Report Guide (Scheeres/Park, CU Boulder)*

learning_goals: Locate measurement within the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework (AMVF) and identify the role of each node — CLO 1, CLO 3
Distinguish what a sensor measures from what it infers, and explain how installation effects and bias affect sensor output — CLO 1
Formulate a testable hypothesis and identify independent and dependent variables for their assigned canned experiment — CLO 1
Map a simple experimental workflow to the AMVF flowchart and annotate the diagram with their own design decisions — CLO 3

activity_meta.title: The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework
activity_meta.purpose: Introduces the AMVF as the organizing framework for the entire course — mathematical vs. physical modeling branches, verification vs. validation, outcomes vs. results, and uncertainty as a mandatory element of any engineering claim. Students see where Phase I sits in the flowchart and why the left-to-right logic of the arc is not arbitrary. The AMVF is also positioned as a gateway to professional engineering standards and handbooks that practicing engineers use to structure experimental work. **Test engineer framing:** This lecture should open by naming the professional identity this course builds toward — the test engineer. Test engineers design tests, characterize systems, and validate models. Every activity in ASEN 3501 is test engineer training: the AMVF is the framework test engineers use, uncertainty is the language test engineers speak, and the ability to argue for a result numerically and with bounded confidence is the output test engineers produce. This identity stake should be planted here and echoed at the opening of each lab segment.

activity_meta.title: Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design
activity_meta.purpose: Covers what is measured vs. what is inferred, the transducer chain, bias vs. precision, installation effects, calibration basics, and confounding factors — establishing that measurement is model realization, not data collection. The second half of this lecture introduces formal experiment design vocabulary: hypothesis formulation, independent and dependent variables, control conditions, and confounding factor identification. Students practice framing a physical question as an engineering hypothesis before any hardware is involved.

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 1 — Design Session (Canned Experiment Introduction)
activity_meta.purpose: Students are introduced to their assigned canned experiment and complete the formal design phase before any data is collected. This is the required pre-lab work for the Tier 1 Phase 1 lab. Required elements regardless of which canned experiment is assigned: (1) a formal hypothesis — one sentence, falsifiable, with brief physical reasoning; (2) identification of independent and dependent variables, control conditions, and at least one confounding factor; (3) an annotated AMVF diagram mapping the planned experimental workflow to framework nodes; (4) acknowledgment that the experiment will use at least two sensors to measure the same physical quantity; (5) a plan to use experimental results to inform and improve the predictive model (not just compare). Students bring this pre-lab work to the W2 lab session where data collection begins.

activity_meta.title: AMVF Reference, ASME V&V 10-2006 & Engineering Standards as Professional Resources
activity_meta.purpose: A curated set of pointers to engineering standards and professional handbooks that ground the AMVF in professional practice. The AMVF used in this course is not itself a published standard; it is a junior-year teaching synthesis built from the same professional foundations students will keep encountering in computational methods, later discipline-specific work, and capstone design. ASME V&V 10 is named explicitly as the primary solid-mechanics standards anchor for the AMVF conversation in this course, with ASME V&V 20 serving as the companion fluid/heat-transfer reference. Additional pointers include AIAA measurement standards, ASME PTC measurement uncertainty standards, and relevant instrumentation handbooks. Students are not expected to read all of these in depth. The purpose is to establish that such resources exist, that professional experimentalists use them, and that students should explain what a cited standard contributes to an experiment rather than citing it mechanically.

activity_meta.title: Viewing — AI Literacy Introduction
activity_meta.purpose: Students watch the instructor's existing AI literacy video content (Coursera-sourced). The core message: AI usage is expected and encouraged — it is the reality of professional engineering practice, and course workload expectations assume AI augmentation. The counterbalance is authentic assessment: in-person oral reviews, engineering critique of AI-generated content, and in-class formative assessments are the primary mechanisms for demonstrating real understanding. Creation is now cheap — the valuable skill is critical evaluation of what is good, what is not, what is missing, and how to make it better. This framing echoes through every assessment in the course. **Supplemental resource:** Students who want structured external AI training are encouraged to explore the Anthropic SkillJar platform (https://anthropic.skilljar.com/) and the MathWorks training offerings — these provide free, self-paced AI and MATLAB learning tracks that complement the course material.

activity_meta.title: GitHub Basics for Course Documentation
activity_meta.purpose: An optional tutorial-style introduction to GitHub for portfolio documentation, now housed in the E0 supplemental collection rather than the required E1 Week 1 reading sequence. Students who need setup support can use it before publishing Tier 1 work, but the course should treat GitHub mechanics as supportive infrastructure rather than a core Week 1 learning objective.

activity_meta.title: Technical Writing in Engineering
activity_meta.purpose: A practical framework for engineering writing applied directly to the Lab 1 brief report. Covers the discipline of claim-then-evidence structure, avoiding hedging and padding, and writing for a reader who needs to verify or reproduce results, not a reader seeking narrative. Students apply this framework immediately in the Lab 1 deliverable due in Week 2. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week Students leave Week 1 knowing what they are measuring, why the AMVF structures the course, and what experiment they have been assigned and designed — but they do not yet know how to characterize the variation in their measurements or how to use their data to improve a model. Week 2 introduces the statistical and computational apparatus needed to move from "we measured X" to "our estimate of X is Y ± Z with 95% confidence," and begins data collection on the Tier 1 canned experiment.

modules.label: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2)
modules.headline: E1 Week 2: Uncertainty Foundations: Power, Monte Carlo & MATLAB
learning_goals: Distinguish variability, measurement error, and uncertainty, and explain why the distinction matters for engineering claims — CLO 2
Apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty in a derived quantity — CLO 2
Explain how different mathematical representations of the same physical quantity yield different sensitivities to measurement variation — CLO 2
Run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to model uncertainty in a derived quantity and interpret the resulting distribution — CLO 2
Use experimental results to inform and update a predictive model — not merely compare to it — and articulate what the data revealed about model limitations — CLO 3

Write a structured lab report with required sections: measurement procedure, error source classification, uncertainty propagation, confidence interval, MATLAB figures with captions, and a plain-language explanation accessible to a non-technical audience — CLO 5
modules.summary: Measurement produces variation. Week 2 gives students the mathematical and computational apparatus to characterize and communicate that variation rigorously. The central activity uses electrical power — expressed as P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R — to explore uncertainty propagation from multiple angles: the standard partial derivative method, how different equation forms yield different sensitivities to measuremen...

lectures.title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty
Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data.

================================================================================

slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same
(Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.")
Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester.

================================================================================

slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise
(Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.")
Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions.

================================================================================

slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random
(Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.)
Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected.

================================================================================

slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim
(Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed arrow spanning ±0.4 N. To the right: "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (expanded uncertainty, k=2, Taylor-series method)." Below the bar, two evidence boxes feed into combined standard uncertainty: "Type A — data-driven: repeated observations" and "Type B — information-driven: datasheets, certificates, prior information, judgment." A final note reads: "Type A/Type B describe evaluation methods, not random/systematic effects. Always name the interval and its basis.")
Talking Point: Uncertainty helps convert a raw measurement into an engineering claim, but the symbol after the plus-or-minus sign must be identified. Type A and Type B tell us how an uncertainty was evaluated. Type A is data-driven, using statistics from repeated observations. Type B is information-driven, using sources such as datasheets, calibration certificates, manuals, prior information, or engineering judgment. These labels do not mean random versus systematic; either evaluation method may be used to characterize different effects. Coleman and Steele define a standard uncertainty as an estimate of a standard deviation; standard uncertainties have no percentage confidence or coverage by themselves. We combine components on that common basis to obtain a combined standard uncertainty, u-c. If the result is then multiplied by a stated coverage factor, we report expanded uncertainty, U equals k times u-c. The coverage factor k is a multiplier, not a confidence level by itself, so k equals 2 does not automatically create a 95 percent frequentist confidence interval. A frequentist confidence interval and a Monte Carlo coverage interval are different constructions and must keep their own names. Our course convention is simple: state the estimate, units, interval type, method, and coverage basis every time.

================================================================================

slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions
(Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.)
Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size.

================================================================================

slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean
(Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.")
Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards.

================================================================================

slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility
(Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.")
Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result.

================================================================================

slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice
(Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.")
Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks.

================================================================================

slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows
(Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.)
Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be.

================================================================================

slide 11: Evaluator Moment — What Is Missing from This Conclusion?
(Image: A bar graph showing three side-by-side bars on a white background. Blue bars labeled "Measured Power" at three voltage levels (3V, 6V, 9V) with values approximately 1.8 W, 7.1 W, 16.4 W. Orange bars labeled "Model Prediction (P = V²/R)" at the same voltage levels. The bars appear close in height. No error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is missing?")
Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this figure and caption. [Pause — allow student responses.] The conclusion is not defensible. Without uncertainty bounds on the bars, you cannot determine whether the visual agreement is meaningful or coincidental. The gap between each blue and orange bar might exceed the measurement uncertainty — in which case you have a discrepancy requiring explanation. Or the gap might be negligible — in which case agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty intervals on every bar, the conclusion has no engineering basis. A professional report that made this claim would be returned immediately for revision.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes

- Three-way distinction (variability / measurement error / uncertainty) is the organizing spine of the lecture, as specified in the blueprint Purpose statement.
- Confidence interval interpretation (slide 7) uses the correct frequentist framing — students need this for the W3 comparison work.
- Repeatability vs. reproducibility (slide 8) references ASME and ISO standards, consistent with the professional standards thread from Week 1.
- Slide 10 explicitly connects this lecture forward to W3, W4, and W5 — AMVF continuity maintained without forcing it.
- Monte Carlo content deferred to W2 L2 per blueprint structure.
- Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — missing uncertainty in bar graph claim).
- V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 9): Slide 5 talking point — added sentence attributing experimental uncertainty requirements to ASME PTC 19.1, distinguishing from ASME V&V 10-2006 which is the computational modeling standard.
- V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 6 talking point — added t-distribution note for N < 30 with MATLAB reference. Slide 7 talking point — added one sentence on Bayesian credible interval as an alternative framework, noting course uses frequentist CI per ASME/ISO standards.

The coverage factor k is a multiplier, not a confidence level by itself, so k equals 2 does not automatically create a 95 percent frequentist confidence interval. A frequentist confidence interval and a Monte Carlo coverage interval are different constructions and must keep their own names. Our course convention is simple: state the estimate, units, interval type, method, and coverage basis every time.

================================================================================

slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions
(Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.)
Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size.

================================================================================

slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean
(Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.")
Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards.

================================================================================

slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility
(Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.")
Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result.

================================================================================

slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice
(Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.")
Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks.

================================================================================

slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows
(Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.)
Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be.

================================================================================

slide 11: Evaluator Moment — What Is Missing from This Conclusion?
(Image: A bar graph showing three side-by-side bars on a white background. Blue bars labeled "Measured Power" at three voltage levels (3V, 6V, 9V) with values approximately 1.8 W, 7.1 W, 16.4 W. Orange bars labeled "Model Prediction (P = V²/R)" at the same voltage levels. The bars appear close in height. No error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is missing?")
Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this figure and caption. [Pause — allow student responses.] The conclusion is not defensible. Without uncertainty bounds on the bars, you cannot determine whether the visual agreement is meaningful or coincidental. The gap between each blue and orange bar might exceed the measurement uncertainty — in which case you have a discrepancy requiring explanation. Or the gap might be negligible — in which case agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty intervals on every bar, the conclusion has no engineering basis. A professional report that made this claim would be returned immediately for revision.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
