lectures.title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities
(Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.")
Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively.

================================================================================

slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method
(Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.)
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point.

================================================================================

slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms
(Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.")
Talking Point: Power equals IV, V-squared over R, and I-squared R are algebraically equivalent for an ideal resistor, but the lab implements them as different measurement models. Each uses a different combination of measured V, I, and R values, and those measurements may be taken sequentially, share instrument effects, load the circuit, or reflect different resistor temperatures. The partial derivatives describe sensitivity within each model; algebra alone does not create independent evidence. In Lab 1, identify these limitations conceptually and do not treat the three estimates as independent.

================================================================================

slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest
(Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.")
Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly.

================================================================================

slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break
(Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.")
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding.

================================================================================

slide 7: Manufacturer Bounds and Monte Carlo Inputs
(Image: A uniform distribution centered at measured value x with endpoints x−a and x+a. A bracket labels a as the manufacturer half-width used for Monte Carlo sampling. Below it, an arrow shows the conversion u_B = a/√3, labeled "standard uncertainty used in Taylor-series propagation." A side note reads: "Use a Gaussian only when a source supplies σ or a defensible coverage basis; do not invent σ from an unlabeled tolerance.")
Talking Point: Keep the manufacturer limit and the standard uncertainty as different quantities. If a datasheet gives a symmetric tolerance plus or minus a, and that is all you know, represent the Type B input as uniform over the original bounds x minus a to x plus a. Those original bounds go into the Monte Carlo sampler. The standard uncertainty of that same uniform input is u-B equals a divided by square root of three, and that standard uncertainty goes into the Taylor-series propagation. Sampling only from x minus u-B to x plus u-B would make the simulated input too narrow. A Gaussian input is appropriate only when a manufacturer, calibration certificate, or explicit assumption supplies a defensible standard deviation or coverage basis. Do not invent sigma from an unlabeled tolerance. Compare the Monte Carlo output standard deviation with the Taylor combined standard uncertainty, and label percentile bounds as coverage intervals.

================================================================================

slide 8: Comparing the Three Measurement Models — A Diagnostic, Not a Truth Region
(Image: Three output distributions labeled by the P measurement model. Shared-input arrows connect them. A note over the overlap reads "internal consistency clue" and a warning reads "overlap does not locate the true value." A checklist names loading, non-simultaneous readings, drift, self-heating, and shared-input covariance.)
Talking Point: Compare the three output distributions as a diagnostic. Substantial agreement can support internal consistency under the stated assumptions, but overlap does not prove where the true value lies, and the estimates are not independent because they share measurements and instrument effects. If they diverge, inspect the measurement models: meter loading, sequential timing, supply drift, resistor self-heating, and the Type B input assumptions. Covariance is an awareness item here; you are not required to calculate it in this first exposure.

================================================================================

slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework
(Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.")
Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward.

================================================================================

slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example
(Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.")
Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for.

================================================================================

slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose?
(Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.")
Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes

- P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction.
- Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint.

lectures.summary: Introduces first-order partial derivative error propagation using electrical power as the working example: P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R. The forms use different measured inputs for the same physical quantity, so the measurement models have different sensitivities and dependencies. Students see how justified input models produce different uncertainty estimates in the derived output. Monte Carlo simulation is introduced as a computational complement to the partial derivative method. Every result must carry an estimate plus a clearly labeled standard uncertainty, expanded uncertainty, confidence interval, prediction interval, or Monte Carlo coverage interval, as appropriate. **Graph presentation framework:** This lecture should include a dedicated segment on how to present a graph — a sequential checklist that students will apply to every MATLAB figure they produce in the course: (1) motivation — why does this graph exist, what question does it answer; (2) axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale; (3) data source — where did the data come from, how was it collected; (4) uncertainties — bounds shown and labeled with their basis and coverage; (5) trend — what pattern the data shows; (6) model comparison — how the trend compares to the predictive model; (7) discrepancy — what are possible causes for any gap between data and model; (8) system-level implication — what does this graph tell you about the overall system. This framework should also be provided as a required handout/reading in E1 W3, and students should be held to it in every subsequent deliverable.
lectures.purpose: Introduces first-order partial derivative error propagation using electrical power as the working example: P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R. The forms use different measured inputs for the same physical quantity, so the measurement models have different sensitivities and dependencies. Students see how justified input models produce different uncertainty estimates in the derived output. Monte Carlo simulation is introduced as a computational complement to the partial derivative method. Every result must carry an estimate plus a clearly labeled standard uncertainty, expanded uncertainty, confidence interval, prediction interval, or Monte Carlo coverage interval, as appropriate. **Graph presentation framework:** This lecture should include a dedicated segment on how to present a graph — a sequential checklist that students will apply to every MATLAB figure they produce in the course: (1) motivation — why does this graph exist, what question does it answer; (2) axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale; (3) data source — where did the data come from, how was it collected; (4) uncertainties — bounds shown and labeled with their basis and coverage; (5) trend — what pattern the data shows; (6) model comparison — how the trend compares to the predictive model; (7) discrepancy — what are possible causes for any gap between data and model; (8) system-level implication — what does this graph tell you about the overall system. This framework should also be provided as a required handout/reading in E1 W3, and students should be held to it in every subsequent deliverable.
learning_goals: Distinguish variability, measurement error, and uncertainty, and explain why the distinction matters for engineering claims — CLO 2
Apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty in a derived quantity — CLO 2
Explain how different mathematical representations of the same physical quantity yield different sensitivities to measurement variation — CLO 2
Run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to model uncertainty in a derived quantity and interpret the resulting distribution — CLO 2
lectures.body_markdown:
E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2
Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities
(Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.")
Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively.

================================================================================

slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method
(Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.)
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point.

================================================================================

slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms
(Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.")
Talking Point: Power equals IV, V-squared over R, and I-squared R are algebraically equivalent for an ideal resistor, but the lab implements them as different measurement models. Each uses a different combination of measured V, I, and R values, and those measurements may be taken sequentially, share instrument effects, load the circuit, or reflect different resistor temperatures. The partial derivatives describe sensitivity within each model; algebra alone does not create independent evidence. In Lab 1, identify these limitations conceptually and do not treat the three estimates as independent.

================================================================================

slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest
(Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.")
Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly.

================================================================================

slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break
(Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.")
Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding.

================================================================================

slide 7: Manufacturer Bounds and Monte Carlo Inputs
(Image: A uniform distribution centered at measured value x with endpoints x−a and x+a. A bracket labels a as the manufacturer half-width used for Monte Carlo sampling. Below it, an arrow shows the conversion u_B = a/√3, labeled "standard uncertainty used in Taylor-series propagation." A side note reads: "Use a Gaussian only when a source supplies σ or a defensible coverage basis; do not invent σ from an unlabeled tolerance.")
Talking Point: Keep the manufacturer limit and the standard uncertainty as different quantities. If a datasheet gives a symmetric tolerance plus or minus a, and that is all you know, represent the Type B input as uniform over the original bounds x minus a to x plus a. Those original bounds go into the Monte Carlo sampler. The standard uncertainty of that same uniform input is u-B equals a divided by square root of three, and that standard uncertainty goes into the Taylor-series propagation. Sampling only from x minus u-B to x plus u-B would make the simulated input too narrow. A Gaussian input is appropriate only when a manufacturer, calibration certificate, or explicit assumption supplies a defensible standard deviation or coverage basis. Do not invent sigma from an unlabeled tolerance. Compare the Monte Carlo output standard deviation with the Taylor combined standard uncertainty, and label percentile bounds as coverage intervals.

================================================================================

slide 8: Comparing the Three Measurement Models — A Diagnostic, Not a Truth Region
(Image: Three output distributions labeled by the P measurement model. Shared-input arrows connect them. A note over the overlap reads "internal consistency clue" and a warning reads "overlap does not locate the true value." A checklist names loading, non-simultaneous readings, drift, self-heating, and shared-input covariance.)
Talking Point: Compare the three output distributions as a diagnostic. Substantial agreement can support internal consistency under the stated assumptions, but overlap does not prove where the true value lies, and the estimates are not independent because they share measurements and instrument effects. If they diverge, inspect the measurement models: meter loading, sequential timing, supply drift, resistor self-heating, and the Type B input assumptions. Covariance is an awareness item here; you are not required to calculate it in this first exposure.

================================================================================

slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework
(Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.")
Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward.

================================================================================

slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example
(Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.")
Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for.

================================================================================

slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose?
(Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.")
Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for.

================================================================================

slide 12: Closure
Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14).
- Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a major new element in Iteration 5 not present in the prior version. Blueprint specifies this framework should be in W2 L2 and provided as a reading in W3. V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Slides 9-10 retained per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS; motivating transition sentence added to slide 9 talking point: "Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive."
- P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction.
- Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint.
- Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method, overlap region as key output — per blueprint.
- "Where would you spend your money/budget" framing embedded in slide 5 sensitivity matrix.
- Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — formula choice based on sensor uncertainties).
- V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 3 talking point — added worked partial derivative calculation step: ∂P/∂V = 2V/R for P = V²/R, evaluated at V = 12 V, R = 4.8 Ω, showing sensitivity coefficient of 5 W/V.

variants.text:
# Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition
## A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously

The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution, not a number, your entire relationship with experimental data changes.

---

This reading introduces a reference text you'll use repeatedly throughout this course: *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers* by Hugh W. Coleman and W. Glenn Steele, Fourth Edition. It's not a cover-to-cover assignment. It's a field guide: different chapters address different problems you'll encounter, and this reading orients you to the book's structure so you can find what you need when you need it.

The [Coleman & Steele OER](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epub/10.1002/9781119417989) is available online as the course reference copy.

Coleman & Steele belongs to a tradition of engineering texts that treat measurement uncertainty not as an afterthought (the error bar you add before submitting a figure) but as the primary product of an experiment. The core thesis of the book matches the core thesis of this course: the goal of an experiment is not to produce a number, it is to produce a defensible claim. A defensible claim requires not just a result but a quantified bound on how uncertain you are about that result, derived by methods that a critical reader can evaluate and reproduce.

![A two-panel illustration. Left panel shows a single point on a number line labeled "reported value: 9.81 m/s²" — clean, falsely precise, with no additional information. Right panel shows a bell curve centered on 9.81 m/s² with shaded tails labeled "±0.04 m/s² (95% CI, partial derivative propagation)" — the same measurement expressed as a distribution with explicit confidence bounds and method noted. A caption beneath both panels reads: "One of these is an observation. The other is an engineering claim." Black-on-white illustration, textbook diagram style with clean typography.](../images/E1_W2_R1_image1.png)
*This image makes the central Week 2 distinction concrete: a reported number becomes an engineering claim only when you say how uncertain it is and how that uncertainty was established.

**Part I of Coleman & Steele** (Chapters 1–4) is the section most relevant to E1. Chapter 1 establishes the conceptual framework: the three-way distinction between **elemental error sources** (individual contributors to uncertainty), **random errors** (scatter that reduces with repeated measurements), and **systematic errors** (bias that does not reduce with repetition). This structure and its vocabulary are exactly what Week 2's lectures develop. If a concept from lecture feels imprecise, Chapter 1 is the first place to check; it provides the careful definitions and worked examples that lecture time does not always allow.

Chapter 3 is where the **partial derivative propagation method** is fully derived. If you have seen the formula for propagating uncertainty through a derived quantity (summing the squared products of partial derivatives and input uncertainties in a root-sum-of-squares combination), that formula is the central result of this chapter. Coleman & Steele derive it carefully, identify the assumptions it requires (first-order Taylor series expansion, uncorrelated error sources), and work through engineering examples of the same form you'll encounter in Lab 1. The three power equations (P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R) are precisely the kind of derived-quantity example this chapter addresses. Different equation forms for the same physical quantity yield different partial derivatives and therefore different sensitivities to the same input errors. Chapter 3 is where that result is grounded in theory.

**Chapter 3** is also where Coleman & Steele introduce the Monte Carlo Method for propagating uncertainty through a result determined from multiple measured variables. Where the Taylor Series Method gives you an analytical estimate of output uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulation draws many samples from the input distributions and propagates them through the full equation, revealing the output distribution directly. The two methods agree when the assumptions of the Taylor-series approximation hold well; they diverge when the output function is highly nonlinear or when input distributions are non-Gaussian. Week 2 develops both methods in the context of Lab 1. Chapter 3 is the main reference for that side-by-side propagation comparison; Chapter 4 then shows how Taylor-series uncertainty analysis is used during experiment planning.

![A side-by-side diagram comparing two uncertainty propagation methods. Left side, labeled "Partial Derivative Method": a mathematical expression showing the RSS combination of (∂f/∂x₁·u₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂·u₂)² with an arrow to a single bell curve labeled "Predicted output distribution (analytical)." Right side, labeled "Monte Carlo Simulation": an arrow from "Input distributions (sampled N=10,000 times)" flowing into a function block labeled "f(x₁, x₂, ...)" and out to a histogram of simulated outputs labeled "Empirical output distribution." A bracket below both sides reads "Both methods estimate the same quantity — check them against each other." Clean technical diagram, MATLAB-inspired plot style, CU Boulder gold accent on axis labels.](../images/E1_W2_R1_image2.png)
*This image lets you compare the two propagation paths side by side so you can remember that the mathematics and the simulation are supposed to answer the same engineering question.

**Part II** of Coleman & Steele addresses validation, comparing experimental outcomes to model predictions within uncertainty bounds. This section becomes more relevant in E2 and E3, but the same reference you use for uncertainty propagation in Week 2 also develops the conceptual framework for the quantitative comparison methods you'll use in Week 3 and beyond. The book carries you through the full measurement-to-validation pipeline.

The relationship between Coleman & Steele and the ASME standards from Week 1 is deliberate. The book's methodology is consistent with ASME PTC 19.1 (the measurement uncertainty standard for performance testing) and with the uncertainty quantification framework assumed by ASME V&V 10-2006. The international reference for measurement uncertainty is the **ISO GUM** (*Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement*). Coleman & Steele's methods are consistent with the GUM framework, making the book an accessible entry point into what is otherwise a dense international standard. Applying Coleman & Steele's methods in your lab reports means applying an approach that satisfies recognized professional standards, which matters when engineering results are submitted for design decisions, regulatory review, or publication.

---

**The Takeaway:** Coleman & Steele is the methodological backbone behind every uncertainty propagation exercise in this course. Keep it as a reference, not a reading, and use it to ground your understanding in the professional standards that this course's framework is built on. Chapters 1 and 3 are your Week 2 anchors; Chapter 4 supports Monte Carlo; Part II becomes relevant in Week 3 and beyond.

Having this reference from Week 2 connects the course's uncertainty framework to professional engineering practice and to the ASME V&V standards cited in Week 1.

Coleman & Steele derive it carefully, identify the assumptions it requires (first-order Taylor series expansion, uncorrelated error sources), and work through engineering examples of the same form you'll encounter in Lab 1. The three power equations (P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R) are precisely the kind of derived-quantity example this chapter addresses. Different equation forms for the same physical quantity yield different partial derivatives and therefore different sensitivities to the same input errors. Chapter 3 is where that result is grounded in theory.

**Chapter 3** is also where Coleman & Steele introduce the Monte Carlo Method for propagating uncertainty through a result determined from multiple measured variables. Where the Taylor Series Method gives you an analytical estimate of output uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulation draws many samples from the input distributions and propagates them through the full equation, revealing the output distribution directly. The two methods agree when the assumptions of the Taylor-series approximation hold well; they diverge when the output function is highly nonlinear or when input distributions are non-Gaussian. Week 2 develops both methods in the context of Lab 1. Chapter 3 is the main reference for that side-by-side propagation comparison; Chapter 4 then shows how Taylor-series uncertainty analysis is used during experiment planning.

![A side-by-side diagram comparing two uncertainty propagation methods. Left side, labeled "Partial Derivative Method": a mathematical expression showing the RSS combination of (∂f/∂x₁·u₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂·u₂)² with an arrow to a single bell curve labeled "Predicted output distribution (analytical)." Right side, labeled "Monte Carlo Simulation": an arrow from "Input distributions (sampled N=10,000 times)" flowing into a function block labeled "f(x₁, x₂, ...)" and out to a histogram of simulated outputs labeled "Empirical output distribution." A bracket below both sides reads "Both methods estimate the same quantity — check them against each other." Clean technical diagram, MATLAB-inspired plot style, CU Boulder gold accent on axis labels.](../images/E1_W2_R1_image2.png)
*This image lets you compare the two propagation paths side by side so you can remember that the mathematics and the simulation are supposed to answer the same engineering question.

**Part II** of Coleman & Steele addresses validation, comparing experimental outcomes to model predictions within uncertainty bounds. This section becomes more relevant in E2 and E3, but the same reference you use for uncertainty propagation in Week 2 also develops the conceptual framework for the quantitative comparison methods you'll use in Week 3 and beyond. The book carries you through the full measurement-to-validation pipeline.

The relationship between Coleman & Steele and the ASME standards from Week 1 is deliberate. The book's methodology is consistent with ASME PTC 19.1 (the measurement uncertainty standard for performance testing) and with the uncertainty quantification framework assumed by ASME V&V 10-2006. The international reference for measurement uncertainty is the **ISO GUM** (*Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement*). Coleman & Steele's methods are consistent with the GUM framework, making the book an accessible entry point into what is otherwise a dense international standard. Applying Coleman & Steele's methods in your lab reports means applying an approach that satisfies recognized professional standards, which matters when engineering results are submitted for design decisions, regulatory review, or publication.

---

**The Takeaway:** Coleman & Steele is the methodological backbone behind every uncertainty propagation exercise in this course. Keep it as a reference, not a reading, and use it to ground your understanding in the professional standards that this course's framework is built on. Chapters 1 and 3 are your Week 2 anchors; Chapter 4 supports Monte Carlo; Part II becomes relevant in Week 3 and beyond.

readings.title: Course Convention for Reporting Uncertainty and Intervals

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# Course Convention for Reporting Uncertainty and Intervals
## A Coleman & Steele–aligned reference for every ASEN 3501 result

The symbol after the ± sign is never self-explanatory; your reader must know what quantity it represents and how you obtained it.

---

This course follows the terminology used by Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*, 4th edition, with the GUM distinction between standard and expanded uncertainty. Use this page whenever you prepare a calculation, table, plot, caption, report, or presentation.

**Sample standard deviation, `s`** — the observed spread of individual repeated measurements. Report it when the variability of individual observations matters.

**Standard error, `s/√n`** — the estimated standard deviation of the sample mean when the observations support that calculation. It describes precision of the estimated mean, not the spread of individual observations.

**Standard uncertainty, `u_i`** — an elemental uncertainty expressed as an estimated standard deviation. Coleman & Steele distinguish **random standard uncertainty**, associated with effects that vary during the measurement period, from **systematic standard uncertainty**, associated with effects that remain invariant during that period. A Type A or Type B label describes *how the uncertainty was evaluated*, not whether the underlying effect is random or systematic.

**Type A evaluation** — evaluation by statistical analysis of observations. **Type B evaluation** — evaluation using other evidence, such as a calibration certificate, manufacturer limit, resolution, prior data, or engineering judgment. A manufacturer limit is not automatically a standard uncertainty. State the assumed probability model or coverage statement used to convert the limit. For example, a symmetric hard bound `±a` modeled as uniform gives `u=a/√3`; a certificate value `U` with coverage factor `k` gives `u=U/k`.

**Combined standard uncertainty, `u_c`** — the standard uncertainty of a result after propagating all relevant elemental standard uncertainties through the data-reduction equation. Combine components only after putting them on a common standard-uncertainty basis. Include covariance terms when there is evidence that inputs share an error source; in introductory work, identify plausible shared sources even when a covariance estimate is not available.

**Expanded uncertainty, `U = k u_c`** — an interval half-width formed from the combined standard uncertainty and a stated coverage factor. The course default for a final Taylor-series-method result is `estimate ± U`, with `k=2`, when that approximation is justified. Write the basis explicitly: for example, `P = 25.7 ± 1.2 W (expanded uncertainty, k=2, Taylor-series method)`. Do not call every `k=2` result a “95% confidence interval.”

**Confidence interval** — a frequentist interval produced by a statistical procedure for a parameter such as a population mean or a difference of means. A 95% confidence procedure produces intervals that contain the fixed parameter in 95% of repeated applications under its assumptions. It is not a 95% probability statement about the realized interval.

**Prediction interval** — an interval for a future observation or outcome. It is usually wider than a confidence interval for the mean because it includes observation-to-observation variability as well as uncertainty in the estimated mean.

**Monte Carlo coverage interval** — an interval containing a stated percentage of the simulated output distribution. Report the input distributions, dependence assumptions, sample count, and interval rule. For example: `central 95% Monte Carlo coverage interval, 2.5th–97.5th percentiles`. Do not relabel it as a frequentist confidence interval.

For plots, replace the generic phrase **error bars** with the quantity actually shown: `±1 standard uncertainty`, `expanded uncertainty, k=2`, `95% confidence interval for the mean`, or `central 95% Monte Carlo coverage interval`. For comparisons, build an interval for the *difference* when statistical inference is needed, then compare that interval with a pre-specified engineering acceptance or equivalence region. Overlap of two separate intervals is only a visual clue, not the decision rule.

---

**The Takeaway:** Reduce every component to a standard-uncertainty basis before propagation, distinguish statistical confidence from uncertainty coverage, and label every interval with its method and meaning.
