================================================================================

slide 2: Predicting What Right Looks Like
(Image: A simple force-displacement graph for a spring-mass system. X-axis: "Displacement (mm)." Y-axis: "Force (N)." A solid straight diagonal line labeled "Expected model response: F = kx (linear)." Two uncertainty bands flank the line — one for measurement uncertainty (gray shading) and one for model parameter uncertainty (lighter outer shading). Annotations: "Slope = k (spring constant)" and "Expected scatter: within these bounds for any individual measurement." A caption: "Before touching hardware: commit to what the expected data signature looks like — the slope, the linearity, the magnitude, and the noise floor.")
Talking Point: One of the habits I want you to develop before the end of this course is this: before you run an experiment, commit to what the right result looks like — specifically. Not "force increases with displacement," but "force increases linearly with displacement at a slope equal to k, with scatter bounded by the propagated uncertainty from my sensor spec." The sketch on this slide is what that commitment looks like. You have drawn the expected data signature, including the expected noise floor, before any hardware has been touched. This sketch is worth something — it is a commitment that can be evaluated after the fact. And it has a corollary that is just as important: if you can describe what right looks like, you should also be able to describe what wrong looks like.

================================================================================

slide 3: Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures
(Image: Three side-by-side force-displacement plots for the same spring-mass system. Plot A (left): "Expected — scatter around a straight line. Scatter is symmetric, consistent with measurement noise." Dots scattered around the model line within the uncertainty band. Plot B (center): "Anomalous — hysteresis loop. Loading and unloading traces follow different paths, forming a closed loop." A label box reads: "Hysteresis: output depends on history — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated. This is not noise — it is a systematic pattern that the linear model cannot explain. Evidence of damping or friction not in the model." Plot C (right): "Anomalous — nonlinearity at large displacement. Slope increases at large displacement." Labeled: "Model assumes constant k. Data shows k is displacement-dependent at large deflection — geometric nonlinearity or material yielding." Caption: "You can predict which anomalous signatures are possible — if you understand your model's assumptions.")
Talking Point: Here is the key insight of this lecture. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure — failure that is not measurement noise, but a sign that the model is fundamentally wrong for these conditions. For a linear spring model, the expected signature is scatter around a straight line within the propagated uncertainty. But there are specific signatures that would tell you the model is wrong. Hysteresis — different loading and unloading paths — is evidence of energy dissipation: the model does not include damping or friction, so it cannot produce a loop. Hysteresis is a specific term worth knowing: it describes a system whose output depends not just on the current input, but on the history of how it got there — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated along the way. Nonlinearity at large displacement is evidence of material or geometric effects the model treats as negligible. These anomalous signatures are predictable in advance if you understand what assumptions your model is making. Commit to them before you run the experiment. Then, if you see them in your data, you will know exactly what physical effect they represent — and you will not mistake them for measurement noise.

================================================================================

slide 4: Scatter vs. Anomaly — The Diagnostic Distinction
(Image: Two plots shown side by side, both using the same force-displacement setup. Left plot: "Measurement Noise — scatter." Multiple data points scatter around the model line, some above and some below, with no systematic pattern. The scatter is bounded by the propagated uncertainty band (gray shading). Label: "This is consistent with random measurement error. No model revision required." Right plot: "Model-Form Error — hysteresis." The data forms a distinct loading and unloading loop that is clearly outside the uncertainty band and clearly systematic. Label: "This is NOT measurement noise. The uncertainty band does not explain this pattern. The model is missing an energy dissipation term." A central annotation: "The distinguishing feature is structure — systematic patterns are model-form errors. Random scatter within the uncertainty band is measurement noise.")
Talking Point: The distinction between scatter and anomaly is the practical form of the calibration-versus-validation distinction. Scatter that is bounded by your propagated uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise — random, symmetric, not systematic. It tells you your measurement process is working as expected. A pattern that is systematic — like hysteresis, or a trending residual, or data that falls consistently above or below the model across a range of conditions — is not measurement noise. It cannot be explained by your sensor spec. It is evidence that the model is missing a physical effect. The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis: data within it is consistent with your model being adequate. Data outside it, in a systematic way, is evidence that the model form is wrong. Commit to this diagnostic before the experiment, and you will recognize a model-form failure immediately when you see it in your data.

================================================================================

slide 5: Connecting to Phase 2 — Your Job Before Data Is Released
(Image: A labeled timeline for the Tier 1 assignment. Three phases shown horizontally: "Phase 1: Forward Analysis" (Weeks 1–4, grayed out), "Phase 2: Reverse Prediction" (Week 5, highlighted in gold), "Phase 3: Synthesis" (Week 5, lighter shade). Under Phase 2, two sub-items: "(1) Before data release: Prediction Memo — commit to expected data signature, uncertainty estimate, and at least one anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure." "(2) After data release: compare prediction to measured outcome, classify discrepancy type (noise vs. model-form)." A callout box: "The risk assessment section of the Prediction Memo asks you to name what wrong looks like — not just what right looks like. That is what today's lecture is preparing you for.")
Talking Point: I want to connect this lecture explicitly to what is coming in Week 5. In Tier 1 Phase 2, you will be given the experiment packet for an experiment you did not run — theory primer, sensor specs, physical setup description — and before any data is released, you will submit a Prediction Memo. One of the required sections of that memo is a risk assessment: you must identify at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation, as distinct from measurement noise. That is exactly the skill this lecture is building. For the spring-mass experiment, the risk assessment might say: if the force-displacement data shows hysteresis, the model is missing an energy dissipation term; if the data shows nonlinearity at large displacement, the linear stiffness assumption breaks down. You are committing, before seeing any data, to what wrong looks like — and why. That commitment is what makes your subsequent interpretation defensible.

================================================================================

slide 6: The Arc of This Concept — Where It Appears Again
(Image: A horizontal course arc diagram with five labeled milestones. Milestone 1 (Week 3): "Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced. Anomalous data signatures introduced." Milestone 2 (Week 4): "Calibration uncertainty propagated into integration report." Milestone 3 (Week 5): "Phase 2 Prediction Memo — risk assessment section names anomalous signatures." Milestone 4 (E2 Week 8): "Distinction deepened in complex aerospace systems — residual analysis and discrepancy as diagnostic." Milestone 5 (E3 Week 11): "Calibration and validation as separate sections of a formal V&V plan." A curved arc spanning all five milestones labeled "Increasing depth and stakes." The current milestone is highlighted in gold.)
Talking Point: I want to give you the arc. Today is the seed — you have the distinction conceptually. In Week 4, calibration becomes procedurally concrete when you run a calibration exercise in the lab and propagate calibration uncertainty into your integration report. In Week 5, the Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires you to use the anomalous-signature framework in your risk assessment. In E2 Week 8, we return to this distinction in the context of more complex aerospace systems, where residual analysis formalizes the diagnostic approach we introduced today. And in E3, when you write a V&V plan, calibration activities and validation activities appear as explicitly separate sections with separate purposes and separate evidence requirements. The concept does not disappear after this week — it gets heavier and more consequential at every stage. Build the habit now.

================================================================================

slide 7: Closure
The extension of the calibration-versus-validation distinction — committing before an experiment to what both the expected and the anomalous results look like — is the discipline that transforms experiment design from observation to hypothesis testing. When you know what wrong looks like before you start, you will recognize it when you see it, and you will be able to name what it means physically. The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis. Data outside it, in a systematic way, is your evidence of model-form failure. Pre-committing to the specific signature — hysteresis, nonlinearity, a consistent offset, an amplitude-dependent period — is what makes the Phase 2 Prediction Memo's risk assessment section more than a formality. That is the skill you need for every validation activity in the rest of this course.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- Created by V&V Executor (2026-05-19) as part of the split of original E1_W3_L2.
- Content source: original E1_W3_L2 slides 6-10 (Predicting What Right Looks Like; Predicting What Wrong Looks Like; Scatter vs. Anomaly; Connecting to Phase 2; The Arc). Now renumbered as slides 2-6. New title slide (slide 1) and new closure (slide 7) written for this lecture.
- Per instructor annotation on CONSOLIDATE_E1_20260518.md: "Split this lecture into two lectures. So Week 3 has three lectures. W3 L2 and W3 L3 can be on the shorter side of a typical lecture."
- Slide 3 (Anomalous Data Signatures) updated from original W3 L2 slide 7: added explicit definition of "hysteresis" in the talking point and image description ("output depends on history — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated"), per not-consolidated approved item: E1_W3_L2 slide 7 — define hysteresis before spring-mass example.
- Slide count: 7 (title + 5 content + closure). Intentionally shorter per instructor instruction.
- Blueprint updated: E1_week_by_week.md revised to include this lecture as W3 L3.
- The original W3 L2 Agent Note about the course arc diagram (slide 10) is preserved here as slide 6 — the arc diagram remains with the anomalous data content rather than being moved to W4 L1.

readings.title: How to Present a Graph

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# How to Present a Graph
## Eight Questions Every Engineering Figure Must Answer

A graph that requires a verbal explanation has already failed. A figure in an engineering report must stand alone, answering every question a critical reader could raise without requiring the author's interpretation.

---

Engineering figures are the primary medium for arguing from data. A well-constructed figure makes a claim, shows the evidence for that claim, and gives the reader the tools to evaluate both, without assistance from the surrounding text. A poorly constructed figure forces the reader to work to extract meaning, often arriving at the wrong conclusion or no conclusion at all. The gap between these two outcomes is not artistic talent; it is a checklist.

This mindset matters beyond ASEN 3501. In capstone and in professional engineering work, a figure often has to defend a design decision before the author is in the room to explain it.

The following eight-question framework provides a sequential checklist for evaluating any engineering figure you produce, or any figure you encounter in someone else's report. Apply these questions in order: each one checks a necessary element of a complete engineering argument from data. A figure that passes all eight can stand alone. A figure that fails any one of them is incomplete.

![An annotated engineering graph showing a damped oscillation response (amplitude vs. time) from a hypothetical mass-spring-damper experiment. The figure has eight numbered callout arrows pointing to specific visual elements: (1) points to the figure caption text, which contains the motivation; (2) points to the x-axis label "Time (s)" and y-axis label "Amplitude (m)" with units visible; (3) points to the legend entry "MATLAB Mobile accelerometer — 100 Hz sampling"; (4) points to error bars shown on the data points; (5) points to an annotation showing the exponential decay trend envelope; (6) points to a dashed line labeled "Undamped model prediction"; (7) points to a text annotation reading "Residual at t=2s exceeds uncertainty band — possible energy dissipation not in model"; (8) points to the figure caption's closing sentence: "Result implies measured damping ratio ζ=0.12±0.01." Clean MATLAB-style formatting with black data points, a dashed model line in gray, and gold error bars.](../images/E1_W3_R1_image1.png)
*This image gives you a fully worked example of what a figure looks like when it carries evidence, interpretation, and model comparison all at once.

---

**1. Motivation** — Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer? The figure caption or title should state this explicitly in one sentence. A graph without a stated purpose forces the reader to guess, and readers guess wrong. *Example: "Figure 3. Measured vs. predicted oscillation amplitude — evaluating whether the undamped model agrees with measured decay within propagated uncertainty bounds."* If you cannot write this sentence, the graph does not belong in the report yet.

**2. Axes** — What is plotted on each axis, with units, at an appropriate scale? Axis labels without units are incomplete. Scale choices that compress variation make disagreement invisible; scale choices that amplify noise make agreement look worse than it is. Both axes should span the physically meaningful range of the quantity, not the range of convenience.

**3. Data source** — Where did the data come from, and how was it collected? The reader should be able to identify the sensor, the acquisition method, and the conditions under which the data was taken without reading the body of the report. This information belongs in the caption or the legend. If your figure uses data from two different sensors or collection sessions, both must be identified.

**4. Uncertainties** — Are error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and explicitly labeled? A data point without uncertainty bounds isn't an engineering claim; it is a raw observation. Uncertainty bounds must appear on the figure, be derived by the method described in the report, and be labeled with their basis (for example: "95% CI, partial derivative propagation from manufacturer spec"). Unless otherwise specified, error bars in this course represent ±1 standard uncertainty (k=1), not a 95% confidence interval; if you use a different coverage factor, state it explicitly. A figure with no error bars says only "here is a number." A figure with labeled uncertainty bounds says "here is a defensible claim."

**5. Trend** — What pattern does the data show? Is the relationship linear, exponential, oscillatory, saturating? The trend is the signal your measurement is characterizing. State it explicitly, either in the caption or as an annotated line on the figure. A reader should be able to identify the pattern from the figure alone, without searching the prose.

**6. Model comparison** — How does the measured trend compare to the predictive model? The model should appear on the same figure as the data, as a line, band, or point estimate, with its own label. Placing model and data on the same axes makes the comparison visible and makes the argument spatial rather than verbal. A figure that shows only data, with the model discussed in the text, requires the reader to perform the comparison in their head. Do not outsource the argument.

**7. Discrepancy** — If there is a meaningful gap between measured data and model prediction, what are the possible causes? A gap that falls within the uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise; note that briefly when it matters to the claim. A gap with systematic structure (increasing with the independent variable, or consistently signed) suggests a model-form limitation; name the physical effect that is likely missing. If the gap is localized to a specific part of the figure, mark that region clearly, then explain it in the caption or surrounding text. Long explanations inside the plot usually make the figure harder to read.

**8. System-level implication** — What does this graph tell you about the overall system? The figure is evidence for a conclusion about the system under study. State that conclusion. A figure that ends at "the data shows X" without connecting X to a claim about the system has done half the work. The closing sentence of the caption should complete the argument: *"Result implies that the measured damping ratio (ζ = 0.12 ± 0.01) is 20% higher than the undamped model predicts, suggesting an aerodynamic drag contribution not captured by the model."*

![A vertical checklist diagram with eight rows. Each row contains a checkbox on the left and a label on the right. The labels read, top to bottom: (1) Motivation stated in caption, (2) Axes labeled with units and appropriate scale, (3) Data source identified in legend or caption, (4) Uncertainty bounds shown and labeled, (5) Trend named explicitly, (6) Model plotted on same axes as data, (7) Discrepancy annotated with physical interpretation, (8) System-level conclusion stated. The title above the checklist reads "Figure Completeness Checklist — Apply to Every MATLAB Figure." Clean sans-serif typography, black text on white, CU Boulder gold left border on the checklist box.](../images/E1_W3_R1_image2.png)
*This image converts the example above into a repeatable review tool you can apply to your own figures before anyone else has to.

---

**Building a figure from scratch:** Before opening MATLAB, start by answering question 1: what question does this figure answer? If you cannot state the question, you do not have a figure yet; you have a data-plotting exercise. Once the question is stated, the remaining seven elements follow naturally: the question determines what belongs on the axes, what comparison is needed, and what the closing sentence of the caption must say. This sequence (question first, figure second) is the difference between a figure that makes an argument and one that merely displays data.

**The Takeaway:** Apply this eight-question checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course. It is the minimum standard for an engineering argument from data. The quality of your figures against this checklist will be evaluated in every subsequent deliverable, beginning with the comparative analysis memo due this week.

learning_goals: Apply a named validation metric to compare a measured outcome to a model prediction within defined uncertainty bounds — CLO 2, CLO 3
Explain the distinction between calibration and validation and articulate why conflating them undermines experimental credibility — CLO 3
Evaluate agreement between two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors or methods — CLO 1, CLO 2
Identify at least one model-form limitation from residual inspection and articulate its implication — CLO 3

readings.title: Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work
## Why the ability to evaluate a report matters as much as the ability to write one

The most important thing a reviewer can tell you is not what you got wrong. It's what you assumed was obvious but left out.

---

Engineers spend years learning to produce: to design experiments, collect data, propagate uncertainty, and write the result. Far less time is spent learning the complementary skill: reading another engineer's work and determining, precisely and charitably, whether the argument holds. Yet engineering critique is not a peripheral academic exercise. It is the mechanism by which engineering knowledge is verified before it is trusted. Every technical report that crosses a program manager's desk, every analysis that feeds a design decision, passes through someone's judgment of: does this argument hold, and what is missing?

![A split two-panel illustration. Left panel: a single engineer at a desk producing a report, shown as a clean document with labeled sections. Right panel: two engineers at a table with the same report between them, one annotating with a red pen while the other gestures at a specific section. A caption below both panels: "Producing a report and evaluating one require different cognitive modes — and both must be learned."](../images/E1_W3_R2_image1.png)
*This image makes the role shift visible so you can treat peer review as a different engineering task from drafting, not just a second pass at your own writing.

**Engineering critique** is the practice of evaluating another person's engineering argument, not for aesthetic quality or writing style, but for structural validity: are the claims correct, are they supported by the evidence shown, does the work meet the requirements of the assignment, and what structured feedback would help the author improve it? The four-part framework below is the structure that makes this evaluation rigorous rather than impressionistic. You are committing to it before you read, for exactly the same reason you commit to a validation metric before you compute: naming your evaluation criteria in advance prevents you from finding what you hoped to find rather than what is actually there.

**Part 1 — What is correct and well-supported.** Every critique must begin here. Identifying what is done well is not a formality or a softener; it's a calibration exercise. If you cannot point to specific evidence that supports a claim, you have not evaluated it; you have reacted to it. A claim is well-supported when the calculation is shown, the intermediate steps are traceable, and the conclusion follows from the numbers. "The uncertainty analysis is complete" is not a valid observation unless you can cite the specific propagation steps and confirm they are present.

**Part 2 — What is incorrect or unsupported.** A claim is incorrect when the calculation is wrong or the conclusion contradicts the data shown. A claim is unsupported when the conclusion is present but the calculation that would support it is absent. These are different failure modes and require different responses. An incorrect claim needs correction; an unsupported claim needs the missing work to be shown. A common unsupported claim in validation work: "The measurement agrees with the model," stated without showing the numerical comparison at all. Agreement is a conclusion that requires an argument, not just an assertion.

**Part 3 — What is missing.** This is the category most evaluators underuse, and it is where the deepest value in a critique lies. Missing elements are structural absences: the required calculation is not there, the confidence level is not stated, the validation metric is not pre-committed, the residual analysis is not shown. Unlike an incorrect claim, a missing element cannot be corrected by revising what is written; it requires adding work that was never done. Identifying missing elements requires knowing the assignment requirements well enough to notice their absence. This is why the course requires pre-committed criteria in every deliverable: both for the student writing and for the student evaluating.

**Part 4 — What specific improvements would most strengthen the report.** Critique that ends at diagnosis is incomplete. The fourth part of a defensible evaluation asks: given the errors and absences identified, what specific actions would most improve this work? Not "add more analysis," but "state the validation metric and acceptance threshold before presenting the numerical comparison, and show the intermediate calculation steps." The test of a specific improvement is whether the author could act on it without asking a clarifying question. Vague feedback is not useful feedback; it is a signal that the reviewer stopped before finishing the evaluation.

![A checklist on a clean white background, styled as a formal report review form. Sections: "Claims" (sub-items: Correct / Incorrect / Unsupported), "Required Elements" (sub-items: Present / Missing), "Evidence Quality" (sub-items: Specific / Vague / Absent), and "Improvements" (sub-items: Actionable / Specific / Tied to requirements). Each sub-item has an empty checkbox. A caption: "A critique is only as specific as the criteria you brought to it."](../images/E1_W3_R2_image2.png)
*This image shows the level of structure a useful critique needs so feedback stays tied to evidence and requirements rather than drifting into vague impressions.

**Peer evaluation is engineering critique of a live presentation.** The Week 3 exercise asks you to evaluate a written report; the Phase 3 lightning talk asks you to evaluate a peer's spoken presentation. The same four-part structure applies, with one adaptation: the evidence you cite must be specific to what was said, shown, or omitted in the talk, not a general impression. "The uncertainty analysis was unclear" is not an observation; "the presenter stated a final result without showing how the sensor spec uncertainty was propagated into the derived quantity, which the rubric requires" is one. Vague praise ("the talk was well-organized") does not meet the standard; specific praise does ("the risk assessment named two physically motivated anomalous patterns before describing the outcome, which is the required structure"). The grading standard for a peer evaluation is the same as for any other deliverable: a claim without specific evidence is not a defensible claim.

---

**The Takeaway:** Engineering critique is a skill, and like all skills it must be practiced against defined criteria. The engineering critique exercise in Week 3 is your first graded practice, and the same four-part structure will appear in every peer evaluation and segment debrief through Phase III.

readings.body_markdown:
# Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work
## Why the ability to evaluate a report matters as much as the ability to write one

The most important thing a reviewer can tell you is not what you got wrong. It's what you assumed was obvious but left out.

---

Engineers spend years learning to produce: to design experiments, collect data, propagate uncertainty, and write the result. Far less time is spent learning the complementary skill: reading another engineer's work and determining, precisely and charitably, whether the argument holds. Yet engineering critique is not a peripheral academic exercise. It is the mechanism by which engineering knowledge is verified before it is trusted. Every technical report that crosses a program manager's desk, every analysis that feeds a design decision, passes through someone's judgment of: does this argument hold, and what is missing?

![A split two-panel illustration. Left panel: a single engineer at a desk producing a report, shown as a clean document with labeled sections. Right panel: two engineers at a table with the same report between them, one annotating with a red pen while the other gestures at a specific section. A caption below both panels: "Producing a report and evaluating one require different cognitive modes — and both must be learned."](../images/E1_W3_R2_image1.png)
*This image makes the role shift visible so you can treat peer review as a different engineering task from drafting, not just a second pass at your own writing.

**Engineering critique** is the practice of evaluating another person's engineering argument, not for aesthetic quality or writing style, but for structural validity: are the claims correct, are they supported by the evidence shown, does the work meet the requirements of the assignment, and what structured feedback would help the author improve it? The four-part framework below is the structure that makes this evaluation rigorous rather than impressionistic. You are committing to it before you read, for exactly the same reason you commit to a validation metric before you compute: naming your evaluation criteria in advance prevents you from finding what you hoped to find rather than what is actually there.

**Part 1 — What is correct and well-supported.** Every critique must begin here. Identifying what is done well is not a formality or a softener; it's a calibration exercise. If you cannot point to specific evidence that supports a claim, you have not evaluated it; you have reacted to it. A claim is well-supported when the calculation is shown, the intermediate steps are traceable, and the conclusion follows from the numbers. "The uncertainty analysis is complete" is not a valid observation unless you can cite the specific propagation steps and confirm they are present.

**Part 2 — What is incorrect or unsupported.** A claim is incorrect when the calculation is wrong or the conclusion contradicts the data shown. A claim is unsupported when the conclusion is present but the calculation that would support it is absent. These are different failure modes and require different responses. An incorrect claim needs correction; an unsupported claim needs the missing work to be shown. A common unsupported claim in validation work: "The measurement agrees with the model," stated without showing the numerical comparison at all. Agreement is a conclusion that requires an argument, not just an assertion.

**Part 3 — What is missing.** This is the category most evaluators underuse, and it is where the deepest value in a critique lies. Missing elements are structural absences: the required calculation is not there, the confidence level is not stated, the validation metric is not pre-committed, the residual analysis is not shown. Unlike an incorrect claim, a missing element cannot be corrected by revising what is written; it requires adding work that was never done. Identifying missing elements requires knowing the assignment requirements well enough to notice their absence. This is why the course requires pre-committed criteria in every deliverable: both for the student writing and for the student evaluating.

**Part 4 — What specific improvements would most strengthen the report.** Critique that ends at diagnosis is incomplete. The fourth part of a defensible evaluation asks: given the errors and absences identified, what specific actions would most improve this work? Not "add more analysis," but "state the validation metric and acceptance threshold before presenting the numerical comparison, and show the intermediate calculation steps." The test of a specific improvement is whether the author could act on it without asking a clarifying question. Vague feedback is not useful feedback; it is a signal that the reviewer stopped before finishing the evaluation.

![A checklist on a clean white background, styled as a formal report review form. Sections: "Claims" (sub-items: Correct / Incorrect / Unsupported), "Required Elements" (sub-items: Present / Missing), "Evidence Quality" (sub-items: Specific / Vague / Absent), and "Improvements" (sub-items: Actionable / Specific / Tied to requirements). Each sub-item has an empty checkbox. A caption: "A critique is only as specific as the criteria you brought to it."](../images/E1_W3_R2_image2.png)
*This image shows the level of structure a useful critique needs so feedback stays tied to evidence and requirements rather than drifting into vague impressions.

**Peer evaluation is engineering critique of a live presentation.** The Week 3 exercise asks you to evaluate a written report; the Phase 3 lightning talk asks you to evaluate a peer's spoken presentation. The same four-part structure applies, with one adaptation: the evidence you cite must be specific to what was said, shown, or omitted in the talk, not a general impression. "The uncertainty analysis was unclear" is not an observation; "the presenter stated a final result without showing how the sensor spec uncertainty was propagated into the derived quantity, which the rubric requires" is one. Vague praise ("the talk was well-organized") does not meet the standard; specific praise does ("the risk assessment named two physically motivated anomalous patterns before describing the outcome, which is the required structure"). The grading standard for a peer evaluation is the same as for any other deliverable: a claim without specific evidence is not a defensible claim.

---

**The Takeaway:** Engineering critique is a skill, and like all skills it must be practiced against defined criteria. Name the four questions before you read or listen, apply them specifically, and your structured feedback becomes the kind of evidence-based argument the course requires of every deliverable. The engineering critique exercise in Week 3 is your first graded practice, and the same four-part structure will appear in every peer evaluation and segment debrief through Phase III.

activity_meta.title: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics
activity_meta.purpose: Establishes that visual inspection of data is insufficient for engineering claims — agreement must be argued numerically using a defined validation metric. Covers candidate metrics (e.g., relative difference within uncertainty bounds, normalized agreement index), the concept of acceptable agreement defined before measurement, and the logical structure of a quantitative comparison argument. Students practice identifying what comparison metric is appropriate for different types of data and modeling claims. **Margin and requirements on plots:** A brief segment should cover how to show design margins and acceptance criteria on data plots — annotating the figure with a line or band representing the requirement, so that the plot itself communicates whether the result passes or fails. This is not a deep dive into systems design or design V&V; it is a practical data presentation skill that connects this course to the broader curriculum without overstepping the course scope.

activity_meta.title: Calibration vs. Validation — A Distinction That Matters
activity_meta.purpose: Introduces the calibration-vs-validation distinction at the conceptual level: calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference (it is a measurement activity), while validation tests whether a model makes accurate predictions (it is a modeling activity). Conflating the two is a common and consequential error. This distinction is introduced in Week 3, deepened in E2 Week 8, and operationalized in the E3 V&V plan. Students encounter a case study where a "calibrated" result was incorrectly treated as a "validated" model. This lecture covers the conceptual distinction and the error pattern only (slides 2–5 in the lecture outline). Anomalous data prediction content continues in Lecture 3. See `lectures/E1_week_3/E1_W3_L2_presentation_outline.txt`. *(Note: shortened by V&V Executor 2026-05-19 per instructor approval — anomalous data content moved to W3 L3.)*

activity_meta.title: Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures
activity_meta.purpose: Extends the calibration-vs-validation distinction into predictive discipline: if you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict not just the expected data signature but the specific anomalous patterns that would indicate model-form failure. Covers the spring-mass hysteresis example (loading vs. unloading differ because energy is dissipated — linear model cannot produce this), scatter vs. systematic anomaly as a diagnostic distinction (uncertainty band as null hypothesis), and connects directly to the Tier 1 Phase 2 Prediction Memo risk assessment section. Students leave with the skill of naming, before touching hardware, what "wrong" would look like and what physical effect it would indicate. Closes with the arc diagram connecting W3 to W5 Phase 2, E2 W8, and E3 V&V plan. See `lectures/E1_week_3/E1_W3_L3_presentation_outline.txt`. *(New lecture, V&V Executor 2026-05-19, created from content of original W3 L2 slides 6–10.)*

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 1 — Data Collection (Week 2 of 3) + Multi-Sensor Comparison
activity_meta.purpose: Continued Tier 1 Phase 1 data collection on the canned experiment. This session emphasizes the multi-sensor comparison requirement: students must be measuring the same primary quantity with at least two different sensor types or methods, and this week's lab work should produce enough data from both sensors to perform the comparison required in the Phase 1 report. Students also practice the system response sketch exercise (15 minutes, in-class) before collecting data: each student independently sketches the expected output — a hand-drawn plot of the primary measured quantity vs. time or vs. the independent variable — with labeled axes, units, and approximate uncertainty bounds. Students commit to specific predicted values and ranges before the session's data collection begins. After collection, they overlay the committed sketch on the measured data and note: (1) where the behavior matched and where it diverged, and (2) if there is a systematic divergence beyond the uncertainty band, what physical effect the sketch's underlying model might be missing. These notes feed directly into the Phase 1 report's model comparison and experimentally informed model sections.

activity_meta.title: Comparative Analysis Memo
activity_meta.purpose: A short written deliverable (1–2 pages plus figures) structured as an engineering memo, drawing on the multi-sensor data collected in lab this week or the prior week. Required: validation metric named and justified before the comparison is performed, metric applied numerically with all intermediate steps shown, conclusion stated within uncertainty bounds, at least one model-form limitation identified and its implication discussed, MATLAB figures with captions that state the conclusion. Students are evaluated both on the quality of their own analysis and on their ability to identify what is missing or incorrect in their argument — the memo should include an explicit self-critique section.

activity_meta.title: In-Class Exercise — Engineering Critique of a Sample Lab Report
activity_meta.purpose: Students are provided a complete lab report — AI-generated or instructor-selected — and are assessed on the quality of their critique. The evaluation structure: (1) what is correct and well-supported in this report, (2) what is incorrect or unsupported, (3) what is missing that should be present, (4) what specific improvements would most strengthen the report. This is the first explicit instance of the course's primary assessment mechanism: students are graded on critique quality, not on producing their own content. The same rubric used here recurs in E2 Week 9 and E3 Weeks 14–15. The sample report should be calibrated to contain genuine errors and gaps — not be obviously wrong throughout — so that students must discriminate carefully.

activity_meta.title: How to Present a Graph
activity_meta.purpose: A short handout capturing the sequential graph presentation framework introduced in E1 W2 Lecture 2. The checklist: (1) motivation — why does this graph exist; (2) axes — what is plotted, units, scale; (3) data source — where the data came from; (4) uncertainties — error bars or bounds shown and labeled; (5) trend — what pattern the data shows; (6) model comparison — how the trend compares to the predictive model; (7) discrepancy — possible causes for any gap; (8) system-level implication — what the graph tells you about the overall system. Students should apply this framework to every MATLAB figure they produce in the course. This reading reinforces the framework introduced in W2 and gives students a durable reference they can use in all subsequent deliverables.

activity_meta.title: Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work
activity_meta.purpose: Introduces and elaborates the four-part critique structure that students use in the W3 in-class engineering critique exercise and in all subsequent peer evaluations (E2 W9, E3 W14–15): (1) what is correct and well-supported; (2) what is incorrect or unsupported; (3) what is missing that should be present; (4) what specific improvements would most strengthen the report. The reading frames engineering critique as a technical skill in its own right — not a softer version of writing — and explains why specificity in all four parts is required. Connects the pre-commitment principle from W3 L1 (name the criteria before you read, for the same reason you commit to a metric before you compute) to the critique context. Seeds the critique skill that recurs as a graded mechanism throughout E2 and E3. See `readings/E1_week_3/E1_W3_R2_reading.md`. *(New reading, V&V Executor 2026-05-19 per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS — item 8.)* ## 3. Bridge to Next Week Students have now walked the individual steps of the Phase I pipeline and practiced evaluating the quality of another's work. Week 4 completes Tier 1 Phase 1 data collection and asks students to execute the full pipeline — from hypothesis to AMVF annotation — in the Phase 1 report. **AMVF reminder (W3):** The AMVF is the organizing spine of the holistic curriculum — experimental methods is the physical modeling branch that connects mathematical models to the real world. Students should be reminded at this point that each experiment they design and execute is not an isolated activity: the question is always "what does this experiment tell you about the system, and why does it matter to the project?" This connection to senior projects and the broader curriculum should be made explicit here and reinforced in E2 W6 and E3 W11.

lectures.title: From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
E1_W4_L1 — From Model to Experiment: Reading the Equations to Design the Test
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 4, Lecture 1
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14
Revised by V&V Executor — 2026-05-19 (added 3-slide opening sequence: Phase 1 report orientation, calibration uncertainty propagation, predict-from-specs procedure)
Revised by Item Review Executor — 2026-07-20 (removed alternate-sensor framing from the predict-from-specs requirement)

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test
Talking Point: You have spent three weeks learning to execute the physical modeling branch of the AMVF — how to measure, how to propagate uncertainty, how to argue for agreement. This week I want to change the direction of the question. Instead of asking "given this measurement, what does it tell us?" I want to ask "given this model, what does it tell us about how to design the experiment?" Because the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document. If you can read a model's equations and extract the sensitivity structure, you will make better instrumentation choices, select more informative test conditions, and write more defensible reports. That is what this lecture is about. But before we get into the design logic, I want to orient you to the Phase 1 report — the deliverable due at the end of this week — because this lecture connects directly to several of its required sections, and one of them you will practice in today's lab session.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Phase 1 Report — A 10-Section Integrated Argument
(Image: A structured numbered list of all 10 required sections of the Phase 1 report, displayed in two columns. Left column (sections 1–5): (1) Experimental Design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors; (2) AMVF Annotation — which pipeline steps you executed and what each established; (3) Measurement Record — calibrated values with documented uncertainty; (4) Multi-Sensor Comparison — do independent measurement approaches agree within mutual uncertainty bounds?; (5) Uncertainty Propagation — partial derivative analysis, dominant error source ranking. Right column (sections 6–10): (6) Model Comparison — validation metric and residual structure classification; (7) Experimentally Informed Model — how would you update the model given the comparison?; (8) MATLAB Figures — required plots; (9) Plain-Language Summary — the result stated for a non-engineer; (10) Predict-from-Specs Exercise — uncertainty prediction from the supplied lab sensor specifications, executed in today's lab session. Sections 5, 6, and 10 are highlighted in gold. A gold banner: "These 10 sections are not a checklist — they are a single argument. Each section depends on the ones before it.")
Talking Point: The Phase 1 report has 10 required sections. I want you to see them as a single integrated argument, not a to-do list, because each section depends on the analysis in the sections before it. Section 1 is your experimental design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors. Section 2 annotates your workflow against the AMVF — where each step sits in the verification and validation pipeline. Section 3 is your measurement record — calibrated data values with their documented uncertainties. Section 4 is your multi-sensor comparison — do your independent measurement approaches agree within their mutual uncertainty bounds? Section 5 is uncertainty propagation — partial derivative sensitivity analysis leading to a ranked list of error sources. Section 6 is model comparison — does the data agree with the prediction, and what does the residual structure tell you? Section 7 is the experimentally informed model — given the comparison, how would you update the model? Section 8 is your MATLAB figures. Section 9 is your plain-language summary — the same result, stated for a non-engineer. And Section 10 is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, which you will execute today during the lab session using the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ information for your experiment's primary sensor. Sections 1 through 9 form a single analytical thread from setup to conclusion. Section 10 is a separate procedure that previews Phase 2 — I am going to explain it explicitly in a moment.

================================================================================

slide 3: Calibration Uncertainty Propagation — A Worked Step
(Image: A four-step workflow diagram labeled "How Calibration Uncertainty Enters Your Total Error Budget." Step 1: "Record sensor output at known reference values" — a small table showing reference input values (e.g., known masses) in one column and measured sensor outputs in a second column, with the label "known standards → recorded sensor response." Step 2: "Fit calibration curve" — a scatter plot of the reference data with a linear fit line through it, slope and intercept labeled. Step 3: "Compute calibration residuals" — a residual plot showing (measured output − curve prediction) at each reference point, with the spread of residuals annotated as "calibration uncertainty u_cal." Step 4: "Propagate as one term in the quadrature sum" — the full quadrature propagation equation displayed: u_total = √[(∂Q/∂x₁ · u₁)² + (∂Q/∂x₂ · u₂)² + u_cal²], with u_cal labeled in gold as "calibration uncertainty enters here." A gold annotation: "Calibration uncertainty is not separate from your error budget — it enters through the same propagation machinery you have been using since Week 2." A caption below: "For the centripetal force experiment: the calibration residuals of your force transducer are the u_cal term. They propagate exactly like any other input uncertainty.")
Talking Point: One required component of your Phase 1 report — Section 5 — is a calibration uncertainty term in your total error budget. I want to make the procedure explicit because it is not always obvious how calibration connects to propagation. Here is the four-step chain. Step 1: record the sensor output at a set of known reference values. For a force transducer, these might be known dead-weight standards. For a temperature sensor, a precision bath. These are the calibration data points. Step 2: fit a calibration curve to those reference points — typically a linear regression, giving you a slope and an intercept. This curve is how you convert sensor voltage or count to physical units. Step 3: compute the calibration residuals — the difference between the curve's prediction and the actual measured output at each reference point. The spread of those residuals is your calibration uncertainty, u_cal. Step 4: propagate u_cal through the partial derivative framework as one additive term inside the quadrature sum, alongside your random measurement uncertainty and any systematic instrument uncertainty from the spec sheet. It enters through the same machinery you have been using since Week 2 — it is not a separate accounting system. For your Tier 1 experiment, the calibration documentation for each sensor you used tells you whether calibration was performed recently and with what precision. That documentation feeds directly into this step. If calibration records are not available, that absence is itself a reportable uncertainty.

================================================================================

slide 4: Predict-from-Specs — A Labeled Procedure
(Image: A three-step labeled workflow box titled "Predict-from-Specs — Section 10 Procedure." Step 1: "Read the supplied lab sensor specifications" — icon of a one-page document labeled "From your experiment-specific lab materials." Step 2: "Propagate specs forward" — the uncertainty propagation equation with the sensor's specified accuracy values substituted in; the output is labeled "predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for this sensor configuration." Step 3: "Sketch the prediction" — a hand-drawn-style sketch of the primary derived quantity plotted against the independent variable, with a shaded uncertainty band. Required annotation labels point to: x-axis (independent variable name and units), y-axis (derived quantity name and units), shaded band (±u at 95% CI), and a legend entry for the prediction curve. A gold annotation: "This is Phase 2 in miniature — predict, without data, what the designated sensor configuration should produce." A note at the bottom: "No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs.")
Talking Point: Section 10 of the Phase 1 report is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, and I want to name it explicitly as a procedure so you know exactly what to do during today's lab session. Here are the three steps. Step 1: return to the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ details for the primary sensor identified in your experiment-specific lab materials. Step 2: using those specifications, propagate the stated sensor accuracies through the partial derivative framework from Section 5 before you rely on any measured data. The output is a predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for that designated sensor configuration. Step 3: sketch that prediction — a plot of the derived quantity versus the independent variable with labeled axes and an uncertainty band shown as a shaded region or error bars. I want the sketch to include labeled axes with units, the uncertainty bound quantified numerically, and a clear indication that this is a prediction, not a measurement. No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs. This procedure is Phase 2 in miniature — you are predicting, from specifications alone, what the selected sensor configuration should produce. Today is your first practice run at it.

================================================================================

slide 5: The Model as a Design Document
(Image: A simple schematic showing a mathematical model equation on the left — for example, a damped oscillation equation "x(t) = A·e^(-ζωₙt)·cos(ωd·t + φ)" — with labeled parameters: A (initial amplitude), ζ (damping ratio), ωₙ (natural frequency), ωd (damped frequency). On the right, a test article diagram showing a spring-mass-damper on a shaker platform with accelerometers attached. Bidirectional arrows labeled with three questions: "What must I measure?" pointing from equation to hardware; "How precisely?" pointing from equation to hardware; "Under what conditions?" pointing from equation to hardware. A gold banner across the bottom: "Every term in the model is an experimental design requirement — if you know how to read it.")
Talking Point: The connection I want you to see is this: every term in a physical model is a measurement requirement in disguise. Look at the damped oscillation equation. It has four parameters — amplitude, damping ratio, natural frequency, and phase. Each of those parameters needs to be either measured, inferred, or held constant in the experiment. The natural frequency can be estimated from the mass and stiffness — but both mass and stiffness must be measured, and if either has uncertainty, that uncertainty appears in the natural frequency estimate. The damping ratio requires the decay envelope to be resolved — which means your time resolution must be fine enough to track the amplitude decrease over several cycles. The model tells you all of this before you touch hardware — if you know how to read it. The model is the first instrumentation document. Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — whether it is centripetal force, pendulum period, or electrical power — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor.

================================================================================

slide 6: The P=IV Lesson Generalized
(Image: A two-panel figure. Left panel shows the P=IV power equation example from Week 2 with the sensitivity breakdown: "P = I²R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error because current appears squared." Annotation: "You learned this pattern in Week 2." Right panel generalizes: a generic derived quantity equation Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) with a table showing three columns: "Input," "Partial Derivative ∂Q/∂xᵢ," and "Sensitivity Magnitude |∂Q/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ|." The table has three filled rows with placeholder values showing one input dominating the sensitivity. A gold arrow connects the two panels with the label "Same logic — any model, any derived quantity." Caption: "The power equation was the training problem. Now you apply the same logic to your actual experiment.")
Talking Point: You learned this pattern in Week 2 with the power equations. P equals I-squared times R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error, because current appears squared and the partial derivative amplifies its contribution. That was not a special property of the power equation — it is a property of any derived quantity that depends on measured inputs. For any model Q equals f of x₁, x₂, and so on, the partial derivatives are the sensitivity coefficients, and the product of a large sensitivity coefficient and a large input uncertainty is the signature of a dominant error source. The power equation was the training example. Your Tier 1 canned experiment is the real problem. Right now, you should be taking the governing equations from your experiment — whatever physical model connects your inputs to your derived output — and computing the partial derivatives to see which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget. That calculation should have been in your Phase 1 report. If it was not, it will be.

================================================================================

slide 7: Worked Example — An Unbalanced Wheel (Tier 1 Pool)
(Image: A diagram of a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass attached at radius r from the center. The disk rotates at angular velocity ω, and the centripetal force on the eccentric mass is labeled F_c = m·r·ω². Below the diagram, a partial derivative sensitivity analysis table. Column 1: "Parameter." Column 2: "Partial Derivative of F_c." Column 3: "Value (for m = 0.05 kg, r = 0.08 m, ω = 120 rad/s, typical sensor spec uncertainties)." Rows: m — ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²; r — ∂F_c/∂r = m·ω²; ω — ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. The sensitivity table shows that angular velocity dominates — the factor of 2m·r·ω, evaluated at 120 rad/s, is much larger than the others. A gold box: "Dominant uncertainty source: angular velocity measurement." This example is from the Tier 1 pool and is distinct from any experiment a student in the room is running.)
Talking Point: Let me use an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that is not your assigned experiment — to walk through this analysis concretely. Consider a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass at radius r. The centripetal force is F_c equals m times r times omega squared. Taking partial derivatives: the sensitivity to mass is r times omega squared, to radius is m times omega squared, and to angular velocity is 2 times m times r times omega. At typical lab conditions — 120 radians per second, for example — the angular velocity term is much larger than the others. And there is an additional amplification: angular velocity appears squared in the model, so a 2% uncertainty in omega contributes 4% to the uncertainty in force. This immediately tells you three things: first, angular velocity measurement is the highest-priority sensor selection. Second, if your tachometer has poor precision, improve it before improving the mass or ruler. Third, the most informative test conditions are those where omega variation is detectable and measurable, not just those where force is conveniently large.

================================================================================

slide 8: Four Questions the Model Answers
(Image: A clean four-question framework formatted as a 2x2 grid of boxes. Box 1 (top-left): "Which input dominates the uncertainty budget?" — icon of a ranked bar chart. Box 2 (top-right): "What sensor spec do I need?" — icon of a sensor with a tolerance specification label. Box 3 (bottom-left): "What test conditions are most informative?" — icon of a plot with a region highlighted. Box 4 (bottom-right): "What anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong?" — icon of a plot with a systematic deviation from the expected curve. A gold border surrounds all four boxes with the label: "Read these from the partial derivative structure — before you touch hardware.")
Talking Point: Every physical model, when you apply partial derivative sensitivity analysis, answers four questions that directly inform your experimental design. First: which input dominates the uncertainty budget? The answer tells you which sensor needs the tightest spec. Second: what sensor precision do I need? The answer comes from working backward from the acceptable output uncertainty — what maximum input uncertainty is tolerable given the sensitivity coefficients? Third: what test conditions are most informative? Models are often most sensitive to the parameter of interest — the one you are trying to estimate — at specific values of the independent variable. Testing there maximizes the information you extract. Fourth: what anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong? If the model assumes linearity, the anomalous signature is curvature or hysteresis. If the model ignores a coupling term, the anomalous signature is a dependence on a variable you held constant. Commit to all four answers before you run the experiment.

================================================================================

slide 9: Working Backward — What Precision Do I Need?
(Image: A worked example diagram. Top: "Acceptable output uncertainty goal: δF_c < 5% at 95% CI." Below: the F_c = m·r·ω² equation with partial derivative terms shown. A table computes: "Given δm from a lab scale (±0.1 g), δr from a caliper (±0.5 mm), what δω is acceptable?" Using the quadrature sum requirement and the dominant sensitivity coefficient for ω, the calculation shows that δω must be less than approximately 0.8 rad/s to meet the 5% goal. Below the table: "This means your tachometer or speed controller must have precision better than 0.8 rad/s at 120 rad/s — about 0.67% of full scale." A gold annotation: "Working backward from an output requirement to an input spec is called an accuracy requirement — it is the sensor selection criterion.")
Talking Point: Here is the reverse problem, and it is the one that shows up constantly in actual test design. Suppose I have a requirement: the uncertainty in my centripetal force estimate must be less than 5% at 95% confidence. I can compute, from the partial derivative structure, what that requirement implies for each of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the angular velocity term. Inverting the propagation equation tells me the maximum allowable angular velocity uncertainty — say, 0.8 radians per second at 120 radians per second — which translates to a tachometer precision requirement of less than 0.67% of full scale. Now I can go to an instrument catalog with a specific requirement, not just a vague desire for a "good" tachometer. This is what sensor selection looks like when it is grounded in the model rather than in habit or convenience.

================================================================================

slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative?
(Image: A sensitivity versus independent variable plot. X-axis: "Angular Velocity ω (rad/s)" ranging from 0 to 200. Y-axis: "Sensitivity of F_c to mass uncertainty ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²." The curve rises steeply with ω, labeled "Sensitivity increases as ω²." Three vertical dashed lines mark: "Low ω region — small sensitivity, hard to distinguish F_c from noise." "Mid ω region — moderate sensitivity, informative but not extreme." "High ω region — large sensitivity, best signal-to-noise for model comparison, but hardware constraints apply." A shaded green band marks the "most informative region" around mid-to-high ω. A note: "The model tells you where the comparison is most discriminating — test there.")
Talking Point: The model also tells you where to run the experiment for maximum information. In the centripetal force example, the force depends on omega squared — which means the sensitivity of the comparison between model and data is much higher at large omega. At low rotation rates, the forces are small and hard to measure above the noise floor. At high rotation rates, the forces are large and the comparison between model and data is highly discriminating — small disagreements are detectable. Of course, hardware constraints — bearing loads, motor speed limits, safety margins — set the upper bound. But within those constraints, the model tells you that testing at moderate-to-high omega gives you the most informative comparison. This is not just intuition — it comes directly from the partial derivative structure. Testing at conditions where the model is most sensitive to the quantity of interest is how you design a meaningful experiment rather than one that could agree or disagree for no informative reason.

================================================================================

slide 11: What Anomalous Behavior Would Tell Me the Model Is Wrong?
(Image: Three side-by-side plots, each showing the centripetal force model prediction (dashed line, F_c = m·r·ω²) against possible data patterns. Plot A: "Expected — scatter around the model curve within the uncertainty band." Dots scatter around the curve within gray bounds. Label: "Consistent with measurement noise — model is adequate." Plot B: "Anomalous — nonlinear deviation at high ω." Data follows the model at low ω but falls below it at high ω. Label: "Possible aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass — not in the model. Model is missing a velocity-dependent force." Plot C: "Anomalous — constant offset across all ω." Data runs parallel to the model but consistently below. Label: "Possible systematic bias in mass or radius measurement — calibration issue, not model-form error." Caption: "Anomalous signatures are predictable from model assumptions — commit to them before the experiment.")
Talking Point: From the model, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would tell you the model is wrong. For the centripetal force model, if the data shows scatter within the uncertainty band, the model is adequate for this experiment. But if the data shows a systematic deviation that grows with omega — falling below the model at high rotation rates — that is a signature of an additional velocity-dependent force, like aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass, that the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega values, that is not a model-form error — it is a systematic measurement bias in mass or radius, which is a calibration issue, not a physics issue. These three patterns are distinguishable, and you can predict which ones are physically plausible before running a single trial. That predictive commitment is what I want to see in the Phase 1 report's model comparison section.

================================================================================

slide 12: The Phase 1 Report — Connecting This Lecture to the Deliverable
(Image: A structured outline of the Phase 1 report's required sections, with specific sections highlighted. The full list: (1) Experimental design, (2) AMVF annotation, (3) Measurement record, (4) Multi-sensor comparison, (5) Uncertainty propagation, (6) Model comparison, (7) Experimentally informed model, (8) MATLAB figures, (9) Plain-language summary, (10) Predict-from-specs exercise. Sections 5 and 6 are highlighted in gold with annotations: Section 5 — "Dominant error source ranking: use partial derivative sensitivity analysis — which input contributes most to output uncertainty?" Section 6 — "Model comparison: validation metric + residual analysis — is disagreement random scatter or structured pattern?" A note: "Sections 5 and 6 are where this lecture lives.")
Talking Point: Let me connect this lecture to what you are producing for the Phase 1 report, which is due at the end of this week. Section five — uncertainty propagation — requires dominant error source ranking. That ranking comes from the partial derivative sensitivity analysis you now know how to do: compute the sensitivity coefficients, multiply by the input uncertainties, and rank the contributions. The dominant source is the one with the largest product. Section six — model comparison — requires not just a validation metric but also a classification of the residual structure. Is the disagreement between your data and your model random scatter within the uncertainty band, or is it structured — showing a trend, a curvature, a consistent offset? That classification comes from examining the residuals the way we discussed in Week 3 Lecture 2. Sections five and six are where everything in this lecture appears in your deliverable. Use the analysis, not just the vocabulary.

================================================================================

slide 13: The Model Is the First Instrumentation Document
(Image: A two-column comparison. Left column header: "Intuitive Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "I'll use the sensor we have in the lab. I'll measure everything I can reach. I'll run as many trials as time allows." A red annotation: "Decisions not connected to model structure — no basis for evaluating if they are sufficient." Right column header: "Model-Driven Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "Angular velocity measurement is the dominant source: spec the tachometer first. Test at high omega where comparison is discriminating. Take enough angular velocity readings to reduce the dominant uncertainty to below 1%." A green annotation: "Decisions derived from partial derivative analysis — directly connected to output uncertainty requirement." A gold banner: "The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you touch hardware.")
Talking Point: I want to close with a contrast that I hope makes the central point of this lecture vivid. The left column is how instrumentation decisions get made when they are not connected to the model — you use what's available, measure what's accessible, and run as many trials as time allows. These are not bad choices necessarily, but they have no principled basis. You do not know if they are sufficient. The right column is what model-driven instrumentation design looks like — specific sensor choices with specific precision requirements derived from specific sensitivity coefficients, test conditions selected because the model is most discriminating there, trial count determined by the output uncertainty requirement. The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you write a sensor list. Read it before you design the test matrix. Read it before you finalize your lab setup. If you do, your instrumentation choices become defensible, your comparison becomes meaningful, and your report becomes an engineering argument rather than a data summary.

================================================================================

slide 14: Evaluator Moment — Find the Error in This Ranking
(Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's Phase 1 report excerpt: "Uncertainty propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total uncertainty. This is because our lab scale has the largest absolute uncertainty of all three inputs at ±0.5 kg. Therefore, improving the scale would most reduce total uncertainty in F_c." A bold prompt below: "Is this ranking correct? If not, what is the error in the reasoning?")
Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause.] The ranking is wrong. The student identified the input with the largest absolute uncertainty but forgot to multiply by the sensitivity coefficient. Dominant error source is determined by the product |∂F_c/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity coefficient is orders of magnitude larger than ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω². Even if the tachometer has a smaller absolute uncertainty than the scale, multiplying by the large sensitivity coefficient may still give a larger contribution to total force uncertainty. The correct procedure is to compute all three products and rank them. The student skipped the sensitivity analysis and went straight to comparing raw uncertainties — which is exactly the error the partial derivative method is designed to prevent. If you are nodding right now, you understand why this lecture exists.

================================================================================

slide 15: Closure
The physical modeling branch of the AMVF is not a passive receiver of whatever data a measurement produces — it is an active design space that the mathematical model constrains from the beginning. The partial derivative sensitivity analysis you have practiced since Week 2 is not just a bookkeeping tool for uncertainty propagation — it is a design tool that tells you which sensors matter most, what precision they need, where to run the experiment for maximum information, and what anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure. The Phase 1 report asks you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline — from hypothesis through sensor selection through calibration through comparison — as a single traceable workflow grounded in the model you were given. That traceable workflow is what test engineering looks like. That is what this week asks you to demonstrate.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes

- This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W4 Lecture 1: "From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test" (CLO 1, 2, 3).

- Worked example uses centripetal force for an unbalanced wheel — an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that is distinct from what students in the room would be running (per blueprint instruction). Not a pendulum. Not P=IV (those are already used).
- Four-question framework (slide 8) captures the blueprint's stated scope: which quantity dominates, sensor selection implications, most informative test conditions, anomalous behavior signature.
- Backward propagation (sensor spec from output requirement) is in slide 9 — a practical skill that supports the Phase 1 report's sensor selection rationale section.

- V&V Executor revision (2026-05-19): Added slides 2, 3, 4 as an opening sequence per approved action item [HIGH] W4 L1 — Three critical content gaps. Slides 2–11 (original) renumbered to 5–14. New slides cover: (a) Phase 1 report as 10-section integrated argument; (b) calibration uncertainty propagation 4-step procedure using centripetal force example; (c) predict-from-specs workflow as a labeled 3-step procedure for Section 10 using the supplied lab sensor specifications.
- No E2 or E3 concepts introduced. W3 L2 calibration vs. validation distinction referenced but not re-taught.

lectures.summary: Establishes the connection between a physical model and the experimental design required to test it — and the connection between that design and the uncertainty budget. The lecture opens with a direct question: given a model equation, what does it tell you about what to measure, how precisely, and under what conditions? The answer lives in the partial derivatives. Each term in an error propagation expression is a sensitivity — it quantifies how much the derived output changes per unit of error in that input. A large sensitivity on a poorly characterized input is the dominant error source; a small sensitivity on a noisy input may be negligible. This is exactly the P=IV lesson from Lab 1, generalized: P=I²R is more sensitive to current than to resistance, so a current-measurement error has greater consequence than the same proportional error in resistance. That logic applies to any model. **Worked example (model-driven design):** Walk through a simple dynamics model from the Tier 1 experiment pool — e.g., an angular velocity expression for an unbalanced wheel or a damped oscillation equation — and use the partial derivatives to identify: (1) which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget, (2) what that implies about sensor selection (which input needs the tightest spec?), (3) what range of the independent variable would make the model comparison most informative (where is the model most sensitive to the parameter being estimated?), and (4) what anomalous behavior would look like if a model assumption breaks down. The key message: the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document for the experiment itself. Students who read their model before they set up the hardware make better instrumentation choices and write more defensible reports.
