learning_goals: Read a physical model's equations to identify what must be measured, how precisely, and under what conditions — connecting model structure to experimental design requirements — CLO 1, CLO 3
Rank the contributions of multiple error sources in a measurement chain and articulate where improving one input would most reduce total uncertainty in the derived output — CLO 2

Execute a complete Phase I experimental pipeline — hypothesis, sensor selection, installation, MATLAB-based data collection, uncertainty propagation, and quantitative model comparison — as a single traceable workflow — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3
Write explicit sensor selection rationale connected to accuracy requirements and intended use — CLO 1
lectures.body_markdown:
E1_W4_L1 — From Model to Experiment: Reading the Equations to Design the Test
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 4, Lecture 1
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14
Revised by V&V Executor — 2026-05-19 (added 3-slide opening sequence: Phase 1 report orientation, calibration uncertainty propagation, predict-from-specs procedure)
Revised by Item Review Executor — 2026-07-20 (removed alternate-sensor framing from the predict-from-specs requirement)

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test
Talking Point: You have spent three weeks learning to execute the physical modeling branch of the AMVF — how to measure, how to propagate uncertainty, how to argue for agreement. This week I want to change the direction of the question. Instead of asking "given this measurement, what does it tell us?" I want to ask "given this model, what does it tell us about how to design the experiment?" Because the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document. If you can read a model's equations and extract the sensitivity structure, you will make better instrumentation choices, select more informative test conditions, and write more defensible reports. That is what this lecture is about. But before we get into the design logic, I want to orient you to the Phase 1 report — the deliverable due at the end of this week — because this lecture connects directly to several of its required sections, and one of them you will practice in today's lab session.

================================================================================

slide 2: The Phase 1 Report — A 10-Section Integrated Argument
(Image: A structured numbered list of all 10 required sections of the Phase 1 report, displayed in two columns. Left column (sections 1–5): (1) Experimental Design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors; (2) AMVF Annotation — which pipeline steps you executed and what each established; (3) Measurement Record — calibrated values with documented uncertainty; (4) Multi-Sensor Comparison — do independent measurement approaches agree within mutual uncertainty bounds?; (5) Uncertainty Propagation — partial derivative analysis, dominant error source ranking. Right column (sections 6–10): (6) Model Comparison — validation metric and residual structure classification; (7) Experimentally Informed Model — how would you update the model given the comparison?; (8) MATLAB Figures — required plots; (9) Plain-Language Summary — the result stated for a non-engineer; (10) Predict-from-Specs Exercise — uncertainty prediction from the supplied lab sensor specifications, executed in today's lab session. Sections 5, 6, and 10 are highlighted in gold. A gold banner: "These 10 sections are not a checklist — they are a single argument. Each section depends on the ones before it.")
Talking Point: The Phase 1 report has 10 required sections. I want you to see them as a single integrated argument, not a to-do list, because each section depends on the analysis in the sections before it. Section 1 is your experimental design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors. Section 2 annotates your workflow against the AMVF — where each step sits in the verification and validation pipeline. Section 3 is your measurement record — calibrated data values with their documented uncertainties. Section 4 is your multi-sensor comparison — do your independent measurement approaches agree within their mutual uncertainty bounds? Section 5 is uncertainty propagation — partial derivative sensitivity analysis leading to a ranked list of error sources. Section 6 is model comparison — does the data agree with the prediction, and what does the residual structure tell you? Section 7 is the experimentally informed model — given the comparison, how would you update the model? Section 8 is your MATLAB figures. Section 9 is your plain-language summary — the same result, stated for a non-engineer. And Section 10 is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, which you will execute today during the lab session using the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ information for your experiment's primary sensor. Sections 1 through 9 form a single analytical thread from setup to conclusion. Section 10 is a separate procedure that previews Phase 2 — I am going to explain it explicitly in a moment.

================================================================================

slide 3: Calibration Uncertainty Propagation — A Worked Step
(Image: A four-step workflow diagram labeled "How Calibration Uncertainty Enters Your Total Error Budget." Step 1: "Record sensor output at known reference values" — a small table showing reference input values (e.g., known masses) in one column and measured sensor outputs in a second column, with the label "known standards → recorded sensor response." Step 2: "Fit calibration curve" — a scatter plot of the reference data with a linear fit line through it, slope and intercept labeled. Step 3: "Compute calibration residuals" — a residual plot showing (measured output − curve prediction) at each reference point, with the spread of residuals annotated as "calibration uncertainty u_cal." Step 4: "Propagate as one term in the quadrature sum" — the full quadrature propagation equation displayed: u_total = √[(∂Q/∂x₁ · u₁)² + (∂Q/∂x₂ · u₂)² + u_cal²], with u_cal labeled in gold as "calibration uncertainty enters here." A gold annotation: "Calibration uncertainty is not separate from your error budget — it enters through the same propagation machinery you have been using since Week 2." A caption below: "For the centripetal force experiment: the calibration residuals of your force transducer are the u_cal term. They propagate exactly like any other input uncertainty.")
Talking Point: One required component of your Phase 1 report — Section 5 — is a calibration uncertainty term in your total error budget. I want to make the procedure explicit because it is not always obvious how calibration connects to propagation. Here is the four-step chain. Step 1: record the sensor output at a set of known reference values. For a force transducer, these might be known dead-weight standards. For a temperature sensor, a precision bath. These are the calibration data points. Step 2: fit a calibration curve to those reference points — typically a linear regression, giving you a slope and an intercept. This curve is how you convert sensor voltage or count to physical units. Step 3: compute the calibration residuals — the difference between the curve's prediction and the actual measured output at each reference point. The spread of those residuals is your calibration uncertainty, u_cal. Step 4: propagate u_cal through the partial derivative framework as one additive term inside the quadrature sum, alongside your random measurement uncertainty and any systematic instrument uncertainty from the spec sheet. It enters through the same machinery you have been using since Week 2 — it is not a separate accounting system. For your Tier 1 experiment, the calibration documentation for each sensor you used tells you whether calibration was performed recently and with what precision. That documentation feeds directly into this step. If calibration records are not available, that absence is itself a reportable uncertainty.

================================================================================

slide 4: Predict-from-Specs — A Labeled Procedure
(Image: A three-step labeled workflow box titled "Predict-from-Specs — Section 10 Procedure." Step 1: "Read the supplied lab sensor specifications" — icon of a one-page document labeled "From your experiment-specific lab materials." Step 2: "Propagate specs forward" — the uncertainty propagation equation with the sensor's specified accuracy values substituted in; the output is labeled "predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for this sensor configuration." Step 3: "Sketch the prediction" — a hand-drawn-style sketch of the primary derived quantity plotted against the independent variable, with a shaded uncertainty band. Required annotation labels point to: x-axis (independent variable name and units), y-axis (derived quantity name and units), shaded band (±u at 95% CI), and a legend entry for the prediction curve. A gold annotation: "This is Phase 2 in miniature — predict, without data, what the designated sensor configuration should produce." A note at the bottom: "No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs.")
Talking Point: Section 10 of the Phase 1 report is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, and I want to name it explicitly as a procedure so you know exactly what to do during today's lab session. Here are the three steps. Step 1: return to the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ details for the primary sensor identified in your experiment-specific lab materials. Step 2: using those specifications, propagate the stated sensor accuracies through the partial derivative framework from Section 5 before you rely on any measured data. The output is a predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for that designated sensor configuration. Step 3: sketch that prediction — a plot of the derived quantity versus the independent variable with labeled axes and an uncertainty band shown as a shaded region or error bars. I want the sketch to include labeled axes with units, the uncertainty bound quantified numerically, and a clear indication that this is a prediction, not a measurement. No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs. This procedure is Phase 2 in miniature — you are predicting, from specifications alone, what the selected sensor configuration should produce. Today is your first practice run at it.

================================================================================

slide 5: The Model as a Design Document
(Image: A simple schematic showing a mathematical model equation on the left — for example, a damped oscillation equation "x(t) = A·e^(-ζωₙt)·cos(ωd·t + φ)" — with labeled parameters: A (initial amplitude), ζ (damping ratio), ωₙ (natural frequency), ωd (damped frequency). On the right, a test article diagram showing a spring-mass-damper on a shaker platform with accelerometers attached. Bidirectional arrows labeled with three questions: "What must I measure?" pointing from equation to hardware; "How precisely?" pointing from equation to hardware; "Under what conditions?" pointing from equation to hardware. A gold banner across the bottom: "Every term in the model is an experimental design requirement — if you know how to read it.")
Talking Point: The connection I want you to see is this: every term in a physical model is a measurement requirement in disguise. Look at the damped oscillation equation. It has four parameters — amplitude, damping ratio, natural frequency, and phase. Each of those parameters needs to be either measured, inferred, or held constant in the experiment. The natural frequency can be estimated from the mass and stiffness — but both mass and stiffness must be measured, and if either has uncertainty, that uncertainty appears in the natural frequency estimate. The damping ratio requires the decay envelope to be resolved — which means your time resolution must be fine enough to track the amplitude decrease over several cycles. The model tells you all of this before you touch hardware — if you know how to read it. The model is the first instrumentation document. Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — whether it is centripetal force, pendulum period, or electrical power — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor.

================================================================================

slide 6: The P=IV Lesson Generalized
(Image: A two-panel figure. Left panel shows the P=IV power equation example from Week 2 with the sensitivity breakdown: "P = I²R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error because current appears squared." Annotation: "You learned this pattern in Week 2." Right panel generalizes: a generic derived quantity equation Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) with a table showing three columns: "Input," "Partial Derivative ∂Q/∂xᵢ," and "Sensitivity Magnitude |∂Q/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ|." The table has three filled rows with placeholder values showing one input dominating the sensitivity. A gold arrow connects the two panels with the label "Same logic — any model, any derived quantity." Caption: "The power equation was the training problem. Now you apply the same logic to your actual experiment.")
Talking Point: You learned this pattern in Week 2 with the power equations. P equals I-squared times R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error, because current appears squared and the partial derivative amplifies its contribution. That was not a special property of the power equation — it is a property of any derived quantity that depends on measured inputs. For any model Q equals f of x₁, x₂, and so on, the partial derivatives are the sensitivity coefficients, and the product of a large sensitivity coefficient and a large input uncertainty is the signature of a dominant error source. The power equation was the training example. Your Tier 1 canned experiment is the real problem. Right now, you should be taking the governing equations from your experiment — whatever physical model connects your inputs to your derived output — and computing the partial derivatives to see which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget. That calculation should have been in your Phase 1 report. If it was not, it will be.

================================================================================

slide 7: Worked Example — An Unbalanced Wheel (Tier 1 Pool)
(Image: A diagram of a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass attached at radius r from the center. The disk rotates at angular velocity ω, and the centripetal force on the eccentric mass is labeled F_c = m·r·ω². Below the diagram, a partial derivative sensitivity analysis table. Column 1: "Parameter." Column 2: "Partial Derivative of F_c." Column 3: "Value (for m = 0.05 kg, r = 0.08 m, ω = 120 rad/s, typical sensor spec uncertainties)." Rows: m — ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²; r — ∂F_c/∂r = m·ω²; ω — ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. The sensitivity table shows that angular velocity dominates — the factor of 2m·r·ω, evaluated at 120 rad/s, is much larger than the others. A gold box: "Dominant uncertainty source: angular velocity measurement." This example is from the Tier 1 pool and is distinct from any experiment a student in the room is running.)
Talking Point: Let me use an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that is not your assigned experiment — to walk through this analysis concretely. Consider a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass at radius r. The centripetal force is F_c equals m times r times omega squared. Taking partial derivatives: the sensitivity to mass is r times omega squared, to radius is m times omega squared, and to angular velocity is 2 times m times r times omega. At typical lab conditions — 120 radians per second, for example — the angular velocity term is much larger than the others. And there is an additional amplification: angular velocity appears squared in the model, so a 2% uncertainty in omega contributes 4% to the uncertainty in force. This immediately tells you three things: first, angular velocity measurement is the highest-priority sensor selection. Second, if your tachometer has poor precision, improve it before improving the mass or ruler. Third, the most informative test conditions are those where omega variation is detectable and measurable, not just those where force is conveniently large.

================================================================================

slide 8: Four Questions the Model Answers
(Image: A clean four-question framework formatted as a 2x2 grid of boxes. Box 1 (top-left): "Which input dominates the uncertainty budget?" — icon of a ranked bar chart. Box 2 (top-right): "What sensor spec do I need?" — icon of a sensor with a tolerance specification label. Box 3 (bottom-left): "What test conditions are most informative?" — icon of a plot with a region highlighted. Box 4 (bottom-right): "What anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong?" — icon of a plot with a systematic deviation from the expected curve. A gold border surrounds all four boxes with the label: "Read these from the partial derivative structure — before you touch hardware.")
Talking Point: Every physical model, when you apply partial derivative sensitivity analysis, answers four questions that directly inform your experimental design. First: which input dominates the uncertainty budget? The answer tells you which sensor needs the tightest spec. Second: what sensor precision do I need? The answer comes from working backward from the acceptable output uncertainty — what maximum input uncertainty is tolerable given the sensitivity coefficients? Third: what test conditions are most informative? Models are often most sensitive to the parameter of interest — the one you are trying to estimate — at specific values of the independent variable. Testing there maximizes the information you extract. Fourth: what anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong? If the model assumes linearity, the anomalous signature is curvature or hysteresis. If the model ignores a coupling term, the anomalous signature is a dependence on a variable you held constant. Commit to all four answers before you run the experiment.

================================================================================

slide 9: Working Backward — What Precision Do I Need?
(Image: A worked example diagram. Top: "Acceptable output uncertainty goal: δF_c < 5% at 95% CI." Below: the F_c = m·r·ω² equation with partial derivative terms shown. A table computes: "Given δm from a lab scale (±0.1 g), δr from a caliper (±0.5 mm), what δω is acceptable?" Using the quadrature sum requirement and the dominant sensitivity coefficient for ω, the calculation shows that δω must be less than approximately 0.8 rad/s to meet the 5% goal. Below the table: "This means your tachometer or speed controller must have precision better than 0.8 rad/s at 120 rad/s — about 0.67% of full scale." A gold annotation: "Working backward from an output requirement to an input spec is called an accuracy requirement — it is the sensor selection criterion.")
Talking Point: Here is the reverse problem, and it is the one that shows up constantly in actual test design. Suppose I have a requirement: the uncertainty in my centripetal force estimate must be less than 5% at 95% confidence. I can compute, from the partial derivative structure, what that requirement implies for each of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the angular velocity term. Inverting the propagation equation tells me the maximum allowable angular velocity uncertainty — say, 0.8 radians per second at 120 radians per second — which translates to a tachometer precision requirement of less than 0.67% of full scale. Now I can go to an instrument catalog with a specific requirement, not just a vague desire for a "good" tachometer. This is what sensor selection looks like when it is grounded in the model rather than in habit or convenience.

================================================================================

slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative?
(Image: A sensitivity versus independent variable plot. X-axis: "Angular Velocity ω (rad/s)" ranging from 0 to 200. Y-axis: "Sensitivity of F_c to mass uncertainty ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²." The curve rises steeply with ω, labeled "Sensitivity increases as ω²." Three vertical dashed lines mark: "Low ω region — small sensitivity, hard to distinguish F_c from noise." "Mid ω region — moderate sensitivity, informative but not extreme." "High ω region — large sensitivity, best signal-to-noise for model comparison, but hardware constraints apply." A shaded green band marks the "most informative region" around mid-to-high ω. A note: "The model tells you where the comparison is most discriminating — test there.")
Talking Point: The model also tells you where to run the experiment for maximum information. In the centripetal force example, the force depends on omega squared — which means the sensitivity of the comparison between model and data is much higher at large omega. At low rotation rates, the forces are small and hard to measure above the noise floor. At high rotation rates, the forces are large and the comparison between model and data is highly discriminating — small disagreements are detectable. Of course, hardware constraints — bearing loads, motor speed limits, safety margins — set the upper bound. But within those constraints, the model tells you that testing at moderate-to-high omega gives you the most informative comparison. This is not just intuition — it comes directly from the partial derivative structure. Testing at conditions where the model is most sensitive to the quantity of interest is how you design a meaningful experiment rather than one that could agree or disagree for no informative reason.

================================================================================

slide 11: What Anomalous Behavior Would Tell Me the Model Is Wrong?
(Image: Three side-by-side plots, each showing the centripetal force model prediction (dashed line, F_c = m·r·ω²) against possible data patterns. Plot A: "Expected — scatter around the model curve within the uncertainty band." Dots scatter around the curve within gray bounds. Label: "Consistent with measurement noise — model is adequate." Plot B: "Anomalous — nonlinear deviation at high ω." Data follows the model at low ω but falls below it at high ω. Label: "Possible aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass — not in the model. Model is missing a velocity-dependent force." Plot C: "Anomalous — constant offset across all ω." Data runs parallel to the model but consistently below. Label: "Possible systematic bias in mass or radius measurement — calibration issue, not model-form error." Caption: "Anomalous signatures are predictable from model assumptions — commit to them before the experiment.")
Talking Point: From the model, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would tell you the model is wrong. For the centripetal force model, if the data shows scatter within the uncertainty band, the model is adequate for this experiment. But if the data shows a systematic deviation that grows with omega — falling below the model at high rotation rates — that is a signature of an additional velocity-dependent force, like aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass, that the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega values, that is not a model-form error — it is a systematic measurement bias in mass or radius, which is a calibration issue, not a physics issue. These three patterns are distinguishable, and you can predict which ones are physically plausible before running a single trial. That predictive commitment is what I want to see in the Phase 1 report's model comparison section.

================================================================================

slide 12: The Phase 1 Report — Connecting This Lecture to the Deliverable
(Image: A structured outline of the Phase 1 report's required sections, with specific sections highlighted. The full list: (1) Experimental design, (2) AMVF annotation, (3) Measurement record, (4) Multi-sensor comparison, (5) Uncertainty propagation, (6) Model comparison, (7) Experimentally informed model, (8) MATLAB figures, (9) Plain-language summary, (10) Predict-from-specs exercise. Sections 5 and 6 are highlighted in gold with annotations: Section 5 — "Dominant error source ranking: use partial derivative sensitivity analysis — which input contributes most to output uncertainty?" Section 6 — "Model comparison: validation metric + residual analysis — is disagreement random scatter or structured pattern?" A note: "Sections 5 and 6 are where this lecture lives.")
Talking Point: Let me connect this lecture to what you are producing for the Phase 1 report, which is due at the end of this week. Section five — uncertainty propagation — requires dominant error source ranking. That ranking comes from the partial derivative sensitivity analysis you now know how to do: compute the sensitivity coefficients, multiply by the input uncertainties, and rank the contributions. The dominant source is the one with the largest product. Section six — model comparison — requires not just a validation metric but also a classification of the residual structure. Is the disagreement between your data and your model random scatter within the uncertainty band, or is it structured — showing a trend, a curvature, a consistent offset? That classification comes from examining the residuals the way we discussed in Week 3 Lecture 2. Sections five and six are where everything in this lecture appears in your deliverable. Use the analysis, not just the vocabulary.

================================================================================

slide 13: The Model Is the First Instrumentation Document
(Image: A two-column comparison. Left column header: "Intuitive Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "I'll use the sensor we have in the lab. I'll measure everything I can reach. I'll run as many trials as time allows." A red annotation: "Decisions not connected to model structure — no basis for evaluating if they are sufficient." Right column header: "Model-Driven Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "Angular velocity measurement is the dominant source: spec the tachometer first. Test at high omega where comparison is discriminating. Take enough angular velocity readings to reduce the dominant uncertainty to below 1%." A green annotation: "Decisions derived from partial derivative analysis — directly connected to output uncertainty requirement." A gold banner: "The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you touch hardware.")
Talking Point: I want to close with a contrast that I hope makes the central point of this lecture vivid. The left column is how instrumentation decisions get made when they are not connected to the model — you use what's available, measure what's accessible, and run as many trials as time allows. These are not bad choices necessarily, but they have no principled basis. You do not know if they are sufficient. The right column is what model-driven instrumentation design looks like — specific sensor choices with specific precision requirements derived from specific sensitivity coefficients, test conditions selected because the model is most discriminating there, trial count determined by the output uncertainty requirement. The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you write a sensor list. Read it before you design the test matrix. Read it before you finalize your lab setup. If you do, your instrumentation choices become defensible, your comparison becomes meaningful, and your report becomes an engineering argument rather than a data summary.

================================================================================

slide 14: Evaluator Moment — Find the Error in This Ranking
(Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's Phase 1 report excerpt: "Uncertainty propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total uncertainty. This is because our lab scale has the largest absolute uncertainty of all three inputs at ±0.5 kg. Therefore, improving the scale would most reduce total uncertainty in F_c." A bold prompt below: "Is this ranking correct? If not, what is the error in the reasoning?")
Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause.] The ranking is wrong. The student identified the input with the largest absolute uncertainty but forgot to multiply by the sensitivity coefficient. Dominant error source is determined by the product |∂F_c/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity coefficient is orders of magnitude larger than ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω². Even if the tachometer has a smaller absolute uncertainty than the scale, multiplying by the large sensitivity coefficient may still give a larger contribution to total force uncertainty. The correct procedure is to compute all three products and rank them. The student skipped the sensitivity analysis and went straight to comparing raw uncertainties — which is exactly the error the partial derivative method is designed to prevent. If you are nodding right now, you understand why this lecture exists.

================================================================================

slide 15: Closure
The physical modeling branch of the AMVF is not a passive receiver of whatever data a measurement produces — it is an active design space that the mathematical model constrains from the beginning. The partial derivative sensitivity analysis you have practiced since Week 2 is not just a bookkeeping tool for uncertainty propagation — it is a design tool that tells you which sensors matter most, what precision they need, where to run the experiment for maximum information, and what anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure. The Phase 1 report asks you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline — from hypothesis through sensor selection through calibration through comparison — as a single traceable workflow grounded in the model you were given. That traceable workflow is what test engineering looks like. That is what this week asks you to demonstrate.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14).
- This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W4 Lecture 1: "From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test" (CLO 1, 2, 3).
- Combines the blueprint's two options: dominant error source ranking (Option A) and model-to-experiment reading (Option B). Opens with partial derivative sensitivity as the bridge between model structure and measurement design.
- Worked example uses centripetal force for an unbalanced wheel — an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that is distinct from what students in the room would be running (per blueprint instruction). Not a pendulum. Not P=IV (those are already used).
- Four-question framework (slide 8) captures the blueprint's stated scope: which quantity dominates, sensor selection implications, most informative test conditions, anomalous behavior signature.
- Backward propagation (sensor spec from output requirement) is in slide 9 — a practical skill that supports the Phase 1 report's sensor selection rationale section.
- Phase 1 report connection made explicit in slides 2 and 12: slide 2 orients students to all 10 sections as an integrated argument; slide 12 drills into sections 5 and 6 specifically.
- V&V Executor revision (2026-05-19): Added slides 2, 3, 4 as an opening sequence per approved action item [HIGH] W4 L1 — Three critical content gaps. Slides 2–11 (original) renumbered to 5–14. New slides cover: (a) Phase 1 report as 10-section integrated argument; (b) calibration uncertainty propagation 4-step procedure using centripetal force example; (c) predict-from-specs workflow as a labeled 3-step procedure for Section 10 using the supplied lab sensor specifications.
- No E2 or E3 concepts introduced. W3 L2 calibration vs. validation distinction referenced but not re-taught.
- Slide count: 15 (title + 12 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 14 (Evaluator Moment — wrong partial derivative ranking).
- V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 5 talking point — added one-sentence bridge to student's own experiment: "Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor."

readings.title: Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree
## Three Diagnostic Cases and What Each One Tells You About Your Measurement

Two sensors measuring the same physical quantity should agree. When they don't, the disagreement isn't a problem to explain away; it is a diagnostic that tells you something about your measurement that neither sensor could reveal alone.

---

Using multiple sensors to measure the same quantity is standard practice in professional aerospace testing. A single sensor gives you a number; two sensors give you a check. If they agree within their specified uncertainties, you have corroboration: independent evidence that your measurement is not a sensor artifact. If they disagree, you have information. But extracting that information requires knowing which of three fundamentally different cases produced the gap.

Defaulting to "sensor error" as a catch-all explanation when sensors disagree is one of the most common errors in student lab reports, and one of the most professionally costly. Each case has a different cause, a different diagnostic signature, and a different remedy. Confusing them leads to wrong corrections and wrong conclusions.

**Case 1: Systematic bias.** One sensor consistently reads higher or lower than the other across all trials. The gap is approximately constant: it does not grow with the measured value, does not fluctuate randomly with time, and does not disappear when conditions change. This is a **systematic error** (also called a **bias**), and it has a specific physical source: a calibration offset, a manufacturing tolerance that shifts the sensor's output, or an installation condition that introduces a consistent reading error. A [thermocouple](https://www.analog.com/en/resources/design-notes/the-basics-of-thermocouples.html) not fully embedded in the material it is measuring, a [pressure tap](https://www.grc.nasa.gov/www/k-12/airplane/tunpsm.html) with a small leak, or a [strain gauge](https://www.ni.com/en/shop/data-acquisition/sensor-fundamentals/measuring-strain-with-strain-gages.html) bonded with an adhesive layer of inconsistent thickness can all produce a systematic offset. The diagnostic signature is a constant gap in a time-series overlay or a consistent offset in a scatter plot of one sensor's output against the other. The remedy is calibration: compare both sensors to a known reference and determine which, if either, is tracking truth.

![Two side-by-side time-series plots. Left plot: two sensor traces — Sensor A (solid line) and Sensor B (dashed line) — running in parallel across time, with Sensor B consistently offset above Sensor A by a constant gap. A horizontal double-headed arrow labels the gap "Systematic bias: ~0.3 V (constant across all time)." Right plot: the same two sensors with overlapping traces that scatter closely around a common mean value. A shaded band represents the propagated uncertainty interval, and both sensor traces fall within it. Caption reads: "Left: Case 1 — systematic bias. Right: Case 2 — statistical agreement within noise." MATLAB-style plot formatting, Sensor A in black, Sensor B in dashed gold, uncertainty band in light gray.](../images/E1_W4_R1_image1.png)
*This image gives you an immediate visual contrast between a persistent offset and ordinary measurement scatter, which is the key distinction this reading keeps returning to.

**Case 2: Statistical agreement within noise.** Both sensors scatter around the same central value, but neither gives exactly the same reading on repeated measurements. The distributions overlap; the 95% confidence intervals from both sensors include a common range of values. This isn't disagreement at all; it is **random variability**, and the appropriate comparison is not "which sensor is right?" but "do the distributions agree within the propagated uncertainty?" If the confidence intervals overlap, the sensors are statistically consistent. The remedy is not to pick one sensor and discard the other; it is to report the comparison explicitly as evidence of consistency, and to consider averaging the two as a way to reduce the random component of the combined measurement. One important qualifier: averaging reduces the random component only when the two sensors' errors are uncorrelated. If both sensors share a common systematic bias (for example, both are mounted on a surface with the same thermal gradient), averaging will not reduce that component.

**Case 3: Spatial or temporal mismatch.** The two sensors appear to disagree, but they are not actually measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or the same moment in time. This is the most frequently misdiagnosed case. A temperature sensor in the center of a flow field and a temperature sensor at the wall will read differently because the fluid temperature genuinely varies across the cross-section; this is not sensor error, it is spatial structure in the physical quantity being measured. Similarly, two sensors sampling at different rates will produce time-series that appear mismatched because the measurement bandwidth differs. The diagnostic signature is that the disagreement has spatial or temporal structure: it is not random scatter and not a constant offset, but a pattern that maps to a physical gradient or a timing difference. The remedy is experimental redesign: co-locate the sensors or explicitly account for the spatial or temporal difference in your uncertainty analysis.

These three cases map onto a broader taxonomy of error sources that applies to any measurement chain.

**Random error** is the scatter you observe across repeated trials. It is a property of the measurement process, characterized statistically (standard deviation, confidence interval). It cannot be reduced by taking one more careful measurement, but it can be reduced by taking more measurements and averaging. **Systematic bias** is a consistent offset from truth that repeats across trials; it is bounded using the instrument's datasheet specification and propagated through partial derivative error analysis. **Structured error** is the most informative category: a repeatable pattern in the residuals that is neither random scatter nor a constant offset. Structured error appears as a trend: residuals that increase with the independent variable, or that switch sign at a specific condition. This is not measurement noise; it is evidence that the model underlying your measurement or data reduction is missing a physical effect.

![Three horizontal panels stacked vertically, each showing a scatter plot of "Residual (measured minus reference)" on the y-axis versus "Independent variable (arbitrary units)" on the x-axis. Top panel: residuals scattered symmetrically above and below zero within a gray uncertainty band — caption reads "Random error: zero-mean scatter bounded by propagated uncertainty." Middle panel: all residuals displaced above zero by a constant amount, outside the gray band — caption reads "Systematic bias: constant offset from truth, bounded by datasheet spec." Bottom panel: residuals starting near zero on the left and curving upward to the right in a systematic trend — caption reads "Structured error: model-form limitation — a physical effect is missing from the model." Clean MATLAB-style formatting, gold data points, black zero line, gray uncertainty band.](../images/E1_W4_R1_image2.png)
*This image translates the vocabulary of random error, bias, and model-form limitation into residual shapes you can actually recognize on a plot.

In a lab report, these three categories are handled differently. Random error is characterized statistically and reported as a confidence interval. Systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet specification and propagated using partial derivatives. Structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated; *"the residuals show a systematic positive trend above 50 rpm. One plausible explanation is aerodynamic drag that was neglected because the initial model assumed drag would be small; follow-up analysis or testing is needed before treating drag as the confirmed cause"* is the kind of statement that distinguishes an analytical engineer from one who simply fitted a trendline and moved on.

---

The three cases in this reading map directly to the discrepancy taxonomy covered later in Week 5: Case 1 (systematic bias) = Category 1 in the taxonomy; Case 2 (statistical agreement within noise) = Category 2; Case 3 (structured error in residuals) = Category 3. When you reach the Week 5 reading on discrepancy classification, you'll find the same framework applied to model-experiment comparisons; the diagnostic logic carries over exactly.

**The Takeaway:** When two sensors disagree, your first question should be which of the three diagnostic cases applies (systematic bias, statistical agreement, or spatial/temporal mismatch) because each has a different cause, a different remedy, and a different implication for what you can and cannot conclude from your measurement.

readings.summary: A short reference reading covering what to do when two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree — and, more broadly, how to think about multiple sources of uncertainty in the same measurement chain. Three cases are distinguished: (1) one sensor is biased (systematic difference — the gap is consistent across repeated trials), (2) both sensors have random noise but no systematic offset (the distributions overlap but their centers agree), (3) the two sensors are not measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or time (a spatial or temporal mismatch that is not a sensor error at all, but a design flaw). Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy. The reading also covers the taxonomy of error sources students encounter in the Tier 1 experiment: random noise (scatter across repeated trials), systematic bias (consistent offset from truth, bounded by the instrument spec), and structured error (a repeatable pattern that is neither random nor a constant bias — often a sign of model-form limitation or confounding factor). The reading frames how these three categories should be handled differently in a measurement report: random error is characterized statistically, systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet and propagated, and structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated. This reading complements the W4 lecture (dominant error source ranking) and directly supports the Tier 1 Phase 1 report's uncertainty propagation and multi-sensor comparison sections. It also bridges forward to E2 W8's treatment of discrepancy as diagnostic information and the Tier 2 improvement proposal.

Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy. The reading also covers the taxonomy of error sources students encounter in the Tier 1 experiment: random noise (scatter across repeated trials), systematic bias (consistent offset from truth, bounded by the instrument spec), and structured error (a repeatable pattern that is neither random nor a constant bias — often a sign of model-form limitation or confounding factor). The reading frames how these three categories should be handled differently in a measurement report: random error is characterized statistically, systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet and propagated, and structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated. This reading complements the W4 lecture (dominant error source ranking) and directly supports the Tier 1 Phase 1 report's uncertainty propagation and multi-sensor comparison sections. It also bridges forward to E2 W8's treatment of discrepancy as diagnostic information and the Tier 2 improvement proposal.
learning_goals: Read a physical model's equations to identify what must be measured, how precisely, and under what conditions — connecting model structure to experimental design requirements — CLO 1, CLO 3
Rank the contributions of multiple error sources in a measurement chain and articulate where improving one input would most reduce total uncertainty in the derived output — CLO 2

Execute a complete Phase I experimental pipeline — hypothesis, sensor selection, installation, MATLAB-based data collection, uncertainty propagation, and quantitative model comparison — as a single traceable workflow — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3
Write explicit sensor selection rationale connected to accuracy requirements and intended use — CLO 1

Top panel: residuals scattered symmetrically above and below zero within a gray uncertainty band — caption reads "Random error: zero-mean scatter bounded by propagated uncertainty." Middle panel: all residuals displaced above zero by a constant amount, outside the gray band — caption reads "Systematic bias: constant offset from truth, bounded by datasheet spec." Bottom panel: residuals starting near zero on the left and curving upward to the right in a systematic trend — caption reads "Structured error: model-form limitation — a physical effect is missing from the model." Clean MATLAB-style formatting, gold data points, black zero line, gray uncertainty band.](../images/E1_W4_R1_image2.png)

In a lab report, these three categories are handled differently. Random error is characterized statistically and reported as a confidence interval. Systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet specification and propagated using partial derivatives. Structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated; *"the residuals show a systematic positive trend above 50 rpm. One plausible explanation is aerodynamic drag that was neglected because the initial model assumed drag would be small; follow-up analysis or testing is needed before treating drag as the confirmed cause"* is the kind of statement that distinguishes an analytical engineer from one who simply fitted a trendline and moved on.

---

The three cases in this reading map directly to the discrepancy taxonomy covered later in Week 5: Case 1 (systematic bias) = Category 1 in the taxonomy; Case 2 (statistical agreement within noise) = Category 2; Case 3 (structured error in residuals) = Category 3. When you reach the Week 5 reading on discrepancy classification, you'll find the same framework applied to model-experiment comparisons; the diagnostic logic carries over exactly.

**The Takeaway:** When two sensors disagree, your first question should be which of the three diagnostic cases applies (systematic bias, statistical agreement, or spatial/temporal mismatch) because each has a different cause, a different remedy, and a different implication for what you can and cannot conclude from your measurement.

activity_meta.title: From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test
activity_meta.purpose: Establishes the connection between a physical model and the experimental design required to test it — and the connection between that design and the uncertainty budget. The lecture opens with a direct question: given a model equation, what does it tell you about what to measure, how precisely, and under what conditions? The answer lives in the partial derivatives. Each term in an error propagation expression is a sensitivity — it quantifies how much the derived output changes per unit of error in that input. A large sensitivity on a poorly characterized input is the dominant error source; a small sensitivity on a noisy input may be negligible. This is exactly the P=IV lesson from Lab 1, generalized: P=I²R is more sensitive to current than to resistance, so a current-measurement error has greater consequence than the same proportional error in resistance. That logic applies to any model. **Worked example (model-driven design):** Walk through a simple dynamics model from the Tier 1 experiment pool — e.g., an angular velocity expression for an unbalanced wheel or a damped oscillation equation — and use the partial derivatives to identify: (1) which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget, (2) what that implies about sensor selection (which input needs the tightest spec?), (3) what range of the independent variable would make the model comparison most informative (where is the model most sensitive to the parameter being estimated?), and (4) what anomalous behavior would look like if a model assumption breaks down. The key message: the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document for the experiment itself. Students who read their model before they set up the hardware make better instrumentation choices and write more defensible reports.

activity_meta.title: Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree
activity_meta.purpose: A short reference reading covering what to do when two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree — and, more broadly, how to think about multiple sources of uncertainty in the same measurement chain. Three cases are distinguished: (1) one sensor is biased (systematic difference — the gap is consistent across repeated trials), (2) both sensors have random noise but no systematic offset (the distributions overlap but their centers agree), (3) the two sensors are not measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or time (a spatial or temporal mismatch that is not a sensor error at all, but a design flaw). Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy. The reading also covers the taxonomy of error sources students encounter in the Tier 1 experiment: random noise (scatter across repeated trials), systematic bias (consistent offset from truth, bounded by the instrument spec), and structured error (a repeatable pattern that is neither random nor a constant bias — often a sign of model-form limitation or confounding factor). The reading frames how these three categories should be handled differently in a measurement report: random error is characterized statistically, systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet and propagated, and structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated. This reading complements the W4 lecture (dominant error source ranking) and directly supports the Tier 1 Phase 1 report's uncertainty propagation and multi-sensor comparison sections. It also bridges forward to E2 W8's treatment of discrepancy as diagnostic information and the Tier 2 improvement proposal.

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 1 — Final Data Collection + Calibration (Week 3 of 3)
activity_meta.purpose: Final data collection session for Tier 1 Phase 1. Students complete the full pipeline in this session: any remaining data collection, a structured calibration exercise (sensor output compared to a known reference, bias correction computed, calibration uncertainty propagated), and initial MATLAB analysis. The calibration exercise makes the calibration-vs-validation distinction from Week 3 procedurally concrete: calibrating a sensor improves measurement accuracy but does not constitute validation of a model. All data collection for the Phase 1 report must be complete by the end of this session. Groups should be running different canned experiments from the pool, which is what enables the Week 5 cross-prediction exercise. **Predict-from-specs in-lab exercise (required):** Using only the sensor specifications and DAQ configuration already provided in the experiment-specific lab document for the primary sensor identified by the instructional team, students estimate what the uncertainty in the primary derived quantity should be before they use any measured data. Sketch the expected output range as a simple plot with labeled axes and approximate uncertainty bounds. This is done in-lab and carried into Section 10 of the Phase 1 report — it is the first in-course practice of the predict-from-specs workflow that Tier 1 Phase 2 requires.

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 1 — Individual Report
activity_meta.purpose: The primary written deliverable of E1 and the most comprehensive deliverable of Tier 1 Phase 1. See `TIER1_assignment.md` for the full student-facing specification. Required sections: (1) experimental design — hypothesis, variables, control conditions, confounding factors; (2) AMVF annotation — flowchart mapped to actual procedure, not planned procedure; (3) measurement record — all values with units and uncertainty basis; (4) multi-sensor comparison — named validation metric applied numerically, conclusion stated; (5) uncertainty propagation — partial derivatives, dominant error source ranking; (6) model comparison — named validation metric, acceptance criterion, discrepancy classified as measurement uncertainty or model-form limitation; (7) experimentally informed model — at least one specific model update proposed and physically justified; (8) MATLAB figures — all with required captions per the graph presentation framework; (9) plain-language summary (3–5 sentences, for a non-engineer, in the introduction or conclusion); (10) predict-from-specs sub-exercise — primary-sensor uncertainty estimate from supplied lab specifications, with sketch. Graded on technical completeness, argument quality, and communication clarity — the quality of the student's critical analysis of their own work is weighted equally with the technical content. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week Students have now executed Phase I completely and submitted the Phase 1 report. Week 5 asks them to cross the epistemic boundary: instead of analyzing their own experiment, they must construct a predictive model for someone else's experiment — and commit to it before seeing any data. This is Tier 1 Phase 2.

modules.label: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5)
modules.headline: E1 Week 5: Transition 1: Cross-Prediction, Lightning Talks & Peer Assessment
learning_goals: Identify the five elements that make a prediction defensible — system response features, propagated uncertainty, a sketch with committed bounds, a ranked uncertainty source list, and a named acceptance criterion — and produce all five for an unfamiliar experiment — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3

Distinguish between a vague prediction ("it will go up") and a committed prediction ("I expect the measured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for engineering credibility — CLO 3
Construct a predictive model with committed system response features and defined acceptance criterion for an experiment they did not run — CLO 1, CLO 3
Identify dominant uncertainty sources in an unfamiliar experimental setup and incorporate them into a prediction memo — CLO 2
Interpret discrepancy between a prior prediction and measured outcomes using model-form reasoning rather than measurement error attribution — CLO 3
Deliver a concise technical presentation (lightning talk) summarizing a prediction and its comparison to data — CLO 5
Evaluate another group's experiment and provide specific, evidence-based constructive feedback on strengths and weaknesses — CLO 5, CLO 6
modules.summary: Week 5 is a transition, not a content week. Students are assigned an experiment they did not run and must construct a predictive model — with committed hypothesis, dominant uncertainty sources, and a defined acceptance criterion — before any data is released. They then compare their prior prediction to measured outcomes from a peer group and reverse roles. The week culminates in lightning talks where students pres...

lectures.title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1
Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14

================================================================================

slide 1: Title Slide
Title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be direct about what a prediction actually is, why it matters that you commit to it before seeing any data, and what distinguishes a prediction that a test engineer would respect from a guess dressed in engineering vocabulary. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the subject of this lecture. By the end, you will know exactly what the Phase 2 memo requires — and more importantly, you will understand why each element exists.

================================================================================

slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters
(Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The output will probably go up. It should be around 30 watts or so. I think it will match the model pretty well." Red annotations: "No committed value," "No physical reasoning," "No mechanism," "Cannot be evaluated after the fact." Right panel header: "Prediction" — icon of a test engineer at a whiteboard. Text: "Based on F_c = m·r·ω², I predict the centripetal force will reach 28.4 ± 3.2 N at ω = 100 rad/s, with current uncertainty dominant at 11.3% of output. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption: "The distinguishing feature is falsifiability: can the prediction be clearly contradicted by the data?")
Talking Point: A prediction and a guess are not different by degree — they are different by structure. A guess is directional and vague: "it will probably go up," "it should be around 30," "it will match pretty well." None of those statements can be clearly falsified by a measurement. If the value is 38 instead of 30, a person who said "around 30" can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so that a measurement can clearly confirm it, approximately confirm it, or clearly contradict it. The ability for the prediction to be contradicted is not a weakness — it is the entire point. A prediction that cannot be contradicted does not say anything about the physics. If you learn nothing when the prediction is right, you also learn nothing when it is wrong.

================================================================================

slide 3: The Six Elements of a Defensible Prediction
(Image: A vertical numbered list with six labeled items, each in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it increases' but 'it reaches 28.4 N at ω = 100 rad/s.'" Card 2 (green): "Propagated Uncertainty — derived from sensor specs using partial derivatives or Monte Carlo. Stated as estimate ± U at confidence level." Card 3 (gold): "Committed Sketch — a hand-drawn or MATLAB plot of the expected output with labeled axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before data is seen." Card 4 (purple): "Ranked Uncertainty Sources — which input contributes most? In what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Card 6 (orange): "Risk Assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern identified with physical basis. What would 'wrong' look like, and what physical effect would cause it?" Below all six: "All six are required in the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Missing any one makes the prediction incomplete.")
Talking Point: The Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires all six of these elements — and I want to walk through why each one exists. System response features commit you to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you know from Week 2. The committed sketch forces you to visualize what the data should look like before you see it — axes, units, scale, and uncertainty bounds drawn by hand or in MATLAB. The ranked uncertainty sources tell me what you understand about the dominant error in this measurement. The named acceptance criterion commits you to the standard you will use for evaluation — before you see whether your prediction was close or not. And element six: risk assessment. Name at least one specific anomalous data pattern that would indicate the model is wrong — not just "the data might be noisy," but a specific physically-grounded signature: "if the output shows amplitude-dependent behavior, the linear model is missing a nonlinear term." The risk assessment is what connects your prediction to the anomalous data thinking from Week 3. Six elements. All six are required.

================================================================================

slide 4: Element 1 — System Response Features
(Image: A comparison of two prediction statements for the same experiment. Statement A: "The measured temperature will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT with h = 35 W/m²K from the correlation for natural convection from a horizontal surface." Green annotations: "Specific value with units," "Physical mechanism (energy balance) stated," "Parameter source identified (convection correlation)," "Falsifiable: a reading of 110°C would clearly contradict this." Caption: "A system response feature is a committed, specific, mechanism-grounded prediction — not a directional tendency.")
Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach 87 plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius at 25 Watts of heater power, based on the steady-state energy balance with a natural convection coefficient derived from the correlation for a horizontal surface" — that is a system response feature. It commits to a value. It names the physical mechanism behind the prediction. It gives me a basis for evaluating whether the prediction was right or wrong, and more importantly, for understanding what a discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable."

================================================================================

slide 5: Element 2 — Propagated Uncertainty from Sensor Specs
(Image: A three-step workflow diagram. Step 1: "Read the spec sheet — extract sensor accuracy, resolution, and measurement range." A sample spec card shows: "Thermocouple accuracy: ±0.5°C. Data acquisition resolution: ±0.2°C. Combined standard uncertainty: √(0.5² + 0.2²) = 0.54°C." Step 2: "Apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source: uncertainty in convection coefficient h, contributing 78% of total variance)." A gold callout: "This is predict-from-specs — estimating uncertainty before you have data, using only the sensor spec sheet.")
Talking Point: The uncertainty estimate in your prediction is not a guess — it is a calculation from the sensor spec. This is the same predict-from-specs workflow you began in Week 4: read the supplied sensor information, apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation, and report the predicted output uncertainty before you rely on measured data. You are doing exactly the same thing for the Phase 2 memo, but now you have not run the experiment yourself — you are working from the spec sheet included in the experiment packet. The calculation is the same: take the sensor accuracies and resolutions from the spec, propagate them through the governing equation, rank the contributions, and report the dominant source. The fact that you have not seen the physical hardware does not change the calculation. If you can read a spec sheet and apply the propagation formula, you can estimate the uncertainty before the experiment runs.

================================================================================

slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch
(Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a time-series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 60. Y-axis labeled "Temperature (°C)" from 20 to 100. A smooth S-shaped curve rises from ambient temperature to an asymptote around 87°C, labeled "Expected steady-state: 87°C." Two dashed lines parallel to the curve form an uncertainty band labeled "±4°C (propagated from sensor spec)." A horizontal target line at 87°C is labeled "Predicted steady-state." Hand-drawn annotations in a different style: "First 10s: rapid rise." "30-40s: rate slows." "After 50s: near steady-state." "Scatter within this band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and bounds committed in advance.")
Talking Point: The committed sketch is not a formality. It is the most direct test of whether your prediction is concrete. If you cannot draw the expected output — with labeled axes, units, approximate scale, and an uncertainty band — you do not yet have a prediction. You have a direction. The sketch forces you to make a sequence of commitments: what quantity is on each axis, at what scale, over what range. The shape of the curve commits you to the physical mechanism — linear growth, exponential approach, oscillation. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your sketch. If it falls within the band, you have learned something positive. If it falls outside, you have a discrepancy to explain. But none of that analysis is possible if you did not draw the sketch before you saw the data. Draw it first.

================================================================================

slide 7: Elements 4 and 5 — Ranked Sources and Acceptance Criterion
(Image: A two-panel slide. Left panel: "Element 4 — Ranked Uncertainty Sources." A horizontal bar chart showing three inputs, with bars proportional to their contribution to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C (DAQ resolution) — 8%. Annotations: "Input A dominates — improving the convection correlation would most reduce uncertainty." "Inputs B and C are secondary — better sensors would help marginally." Right panel: "Element 5 — Acceptance Criterion." A text box: "Pre-committed criterion: the prediction is considered successful if the measured steady-state temperature falls within the propagated uncertainty interval of 87 ± 4°C. If the measured value falls outside this band, the discrepancy exceeds measurement noise and requires a physical explanation." A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.")
Talking Point: Element four — ranked uncertainty sources — is the output of the sensitivity analysis from this week's lecture. Once you have propagated uncertainty through the governing equation, you know which input contributes the most. State it explicitly, with estimated magnitudes. This is not just bookkeeping — it tells me whether you understand the dominant error in the measurement and whether your prediction accounts for the right sources of uncertainty. Element five — the named acceptance criterion — is the pre-commitment that makes the comparison defensible. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction holds; if it falls outside, the discrepancy requires a physical explanation. Pre-committing to the criterion means you cannot move the goalposts after seeing the result. That is not a bureaucratic rule — it is the logical requirement that makes validation meaningful.

================================================================================

slide 8: The Grading Philosophy — Right Prediction, Wrong Reasoning Scores Lower
(Image: A 2x2 matrix with "Prediction Correctness" on one axis (Right / Wrong) and "Quality of Reasoning" on the other axis (Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal outcome." Top-right "Right prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Suspicious — you may have gotten lucky. Less informative than bottom-left." Bottom-left "Wrong prediction, sound reasoning" — green label "Good — the analysis was correct; the discrepancy teaches you something." Bottom-right "Wrong prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Poor — both the analysis and the outcome fail." A caption: "A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be right from vague intuition.")
Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not the grading standard you may be used to. A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and a complete, committed analysis is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition with no mechanism, no calculation, and no committed uncertainty. The goal of Phase 2 is not to produce a correct prediction. The goal is to demonstrate that you understood the physics of the experiment before you saw the data — well enough to build a specific, mechanistic, falsifiable prediction. If your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons, you have learned something real. If your prediction was right for the wrong reasons — if you got lucky — you have learned nothing durable from the result. This philosophy is not idiosyncratic — it is the professional norm for test engineers, and you should hear it clearly before Phase 2 begins.

================================================================================

slide 9: Worked Example — A Complete Prediction for an Unfamiliar Experiment
(Image: A structured layout walking through a Phase 2 memo structure for a simple heat conduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with temperature sensors at two points along the rod. Headers and one-line summaries for each section: "System Response Features: steady-state temperature at sensor 2 predicted to be 54 ± 5°C based on one-dimensional heat conduction equation Q = k·A·ΔT/L." "Uncertainty: dominant source is thermal conductivity k — literature value uncertainty contributes 68% of total output variance." "Sketch: temperature profile along rod shown as linear gradient with ±5°C band." "Acceptance Criterion: prediction is successful if measured temperature at sensor 2 is within 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod surface fails — radiation or convection losses not included." The layout is clean and reads as a professional memo excerpt.)
Talking Point: Let me walk you through what a complete Phase 2 prediction looks like, using a heat conduction experiment that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I read the theory primer, identify the governing equation — one-dimensional Fourier conduction — and extract the system response feature: steady-state temperature at the second sensor, predicted to be 54 degrees Celsius. I propagate uncertainty using the conductivity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the expected linear temperature gradient along the rod with the uncertainty band. I commit to an acceptance criterion: within 54 plus or minus 5 degrees. And in the risk assessment — element six, which is required for Phase 2 — I identify the anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure: if the measured temperature profile is nonlinear along the rod, the model's assumption of no lateral heat loss is wrong, and the one-dimensional model is inadequate. That is a complete prediction.

================================================================================

slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session
(Image: A structured timeline for today's lab session. Step 1 (30 min): "Receive your Phase 2 experiment packet — theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, original hypothesis from the student who ran Phase 1." Step 2 (45 min): "Extract system response features, propagate uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch." Step 3 (15 min): "Write the acceptance criterion and rank the uncertainty sources." Step 4: "Submit the Prediction Memo before data is released — this is the gate." A red annotation at Step 4: "Data will not be released until the memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received Experiment B's packet; the student who ran Experiment B received Experiment A's packet. You will present both in the Phase 3 lightning talk.")
Talking Point: In today's lab session, you will receive the experiment packet for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that you did not run as your Phase 1. The packet includes the theory primer, sensor specifications, DAQ configuration, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran that experiment. You will not receive the data until after you submit the Prediction Memo. That submission is the gate — data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all six elements of the prediction: extract system response features from the theory primer, propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch, rank the uncertainty sources, write the acceptance criterion, and write the risk assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern with physical basis that would indicate model-form failure. Submit before data is released. That commitment is what makes the subsequent comparison honest. The Phase 3 lightning talk — where you present your cross-prediction results — also includes a graded peer evaluation. A strong peer evaluation names specific evidence and explains the reasoning: not "the uncertainty analysis was incomplete" but "the uncertainty in the derived quantity was not propagated from the sensor spec, which is required by the rubric." The Week 3 reading on comparative analysis gives you the four-part structure; apply it to a live talk the same way you apply it to a written report.

================================================================================

slide 11: Closure
Pre-commitment is not a bureaucratic requirement. It is the logical structure that makes experimental learning honest. If you construct your interpretation of the data after seeing it, you will inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic, falsifiable, with an uncertainty estimate and an acceptance criterion — is your promise to yourself and to the reader that your interpretation of the data was not reverse-engineered from the outcome. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the six components of that promise. In the Phase 2 memo you are writing today, every one of them is required. And the grading philosophy is explicit: sound reasoning in the wrong direction is more valuable than vague intuition in the right one. That is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now.

================================================================================

## Agent Notes
- New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14).
- This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W5 Lecture 1: "What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look" (CLO 1, 2, 3).
- All six elements of the Phase 2 Prediction Memo are explicitly walked through in slides 3–7, per blueprint: system response features (committed values), uncertainty from spec propagation, committed sketch, ranked uncertainty sources, named acceptance criterion, risk assessment. Slide 3 updated from "Five Elements" to "Six Elements" by V&V Executor 2026-05-19 to resolve mismatch with Phase 2 rubric which requires six sections.
- The grading philosophy — wrong prediction from sound reasoning is more valuable than right prediction from vague intuition — is communicated explicitly in slide 8, per the blueprint instruction that this philosophy "must be communicated explicitly in the rubric and in class before Phase 2 begins."
- Worked example (slide 9) uses a heat conduction experiment, distinct from the centripetal force example in W4 L1 and not a pendulum.
- Slide 10 describes the lab session structure, including the "memo as gate" requirement.
- Risk assessment element (anomalous signature) mentioned in slides 9 and 10 — bridges to W3 L2's anomalous data signature content.
- Slide count: 11 (title + 9 content + closure). Within spec (10-12).
- V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 10): Slide 10 talking point — added two sentences orienting students to the Phase 3 peer evaluation rubric: name specific evidence + explained reasoning vs. vague critique. Forward pointer to W3 R2 (comparative analysis reading) as the four-part framework to apply to live talks.
