learning_goals: Identify the five elements that make a prediction defensible — system response features, propagated uncertainty, a sketch with committed bounds, a ranked uncertainty source list, and a named acceptance criterion — and produce all five for an unfamiliar experiment — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3

Distinguish between a vague prediction ("it will go up") and a committed prediction ("I expect the measured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for engineering credibility — CLO 3
Construct a predictive model with committed system response features and defined acceptance criterion for an experiment they did not run — CLO 1, CLO 3
Identify dominant uncertainty sources in an unfamiliar experimental setup and incorporate them into a prediction memo — CLO 2
readings.body_markdown:
# Discrepancy Is Data — Interpreting What the Gap Tells You
## Three Categories of Mismatch, and What Each One Reveals About Your Model

The most instructive moment in any experiment is not when your result matches the prediction. It's when it doesn't, and you're forced to explain why.

---

Every experiment ends with a comparison: measured result on one side, model prediction on the other. When the two agree, confidence builds. When they don't, something more interesting happens: the disagreement carries information that the agreement never could. A result that matches your model tells you the model is not obviously wrong, for this experiment, at this scale, under these conditions. A result that doesn't match tells you something specific about what the model is missing or what the measurement got wrong. But only an engineer who knows how to read the gap can extract that information.

The discipline of discrepancy interpretation begins with classification. Not all gaps between measurement and prediction are the same, and the same physical magnitude of gap can mean fundamentally different things depending on its relationship to the propagated uncertainty bounds and the structure of the residuals.

![Three side-by-side plots, each showing "Measured value" on the y-axis and "Independent variable" on the x-axis, with a model prediction shown as a solid black line and a gray shaded band representing the propagated uncertainty interval. In the left plot (Case 1), data points scatter symmetrically above and below the model line, all falling within the gray uncertainty band — labeled "Case 1: Within uncertainty band." In the center plot (Case 2), data points are systematically displaced above the model line with no systematic trend — the offset is uniform and the uncertainty band does not contain the displaced points — labeled "Case 2: Uniform offset — underestimated uncertainty." In the right plot (Case 3), data points follow a curve that diverges from the straight model line — the gap grows as the independent variable increases — labeled "Case 3: Systematic trend — model-form limitation." Clean MATLAB-style plots, gold data points, black model line, gray uncertainty band.](../images/E1_W5_R1_image1.png)
*This image lets you see the three comparison outcomes at once so the later discussion about agreement, bias, and model-form error has a concrete visual anchor.

**Case 1: The gap falls within the propagated uncertainty band.** The measured result and the model prediction are statistically consistent; the scatter is explained by measurement noise, and no model revision is required by this result alone. This is the resolution you hoped for when you designed the experiment. Note carefully what it does *not* mean: consistency within uncertainty says only that you cannot distinguish the model from noise with this experiment, this instrumentation, and this level of precision. A tighter measurement (fewer sources of random error, a better-characterized sensor) might reveal a real gap that the current uncertainty band was hiding. Consider measuring the natural frequency of a spring-mass system: if the scatter in your repeated frequency measurements is larger than the difference between measured and predicted frequency, the two are statistically consistent. Tighten the measurement, and a real discrepancy may emerge.

**Case 2: The gap is larger than the uncertainty band, but has no systematic structure.** The data points scatter above and below the model, but their distribution is consistently displaced; the mean of the measured values does not fall within the predicted range, even accounting for propagated uncertainty. The residuals show no trend; they are randomly scattered but uniformly offset. This is a signal that your uncertainty analysis is incomplete: a significant error source was omitted from the propagation. The diagnostic question is which source. Review the measurement chain: are there sensor specifications you rough-estimated? An installation effect you assumed was negligible? A calibration offset you did not propagate? Consider a pendulum period measurement where you identified reaction time as the dominant uncertainty source but forgot that the pendulum's initial release angle affects the linearization assumption. That systematic physical effect, omitted from the uncertainty model, produces exactly this pattern: a gap larger than the stated bounds that has no systematic structure.

**Case 3: The gap shows systematic structure.** The residuals are not random; they grow with the independent variable or change sign in a repeatable pattern. This establishes structured inadequacy, not a unique cause. Consider random variation, known bias, setup/installation effects, uncontrolled inputs, data-reduction error, parameter uncertainty, and model-form discrepancy. In a projectile example, missing drag may be the preferred explanation, but timing bias or a camera-calibration defect are competing hypotheses until evidence rules them down. State what evidence supports the preferred cause and what check would distinguish a viable alternative.

![A single residual plot with "Residual (measured minus model)" on the y-axis and "Independent variable" on the x-axis. The plot is divided into three annotated zones. Zone 1 on the far left shows residuals scattered randomly within a horizontal gray uncertainty band around zero — a text annotation reads "Case 1: scatter within bounds." Zone 2 in the middle shows residuals uniformly displaced above the zero line, outside the uncertainty band — a text annotation reads "Case 2: uniform offset — revisit uncertainty sources." Zone 3 on the right shows residuals curving away from zero in a systematic upward trend — a text annotation reads "Case 3: growing trend — model-form limitation." Arrows point from each zone annotation to the corresponding region of the plot. MATLAB-style formatting, gold residuals, gray uncertainty band, black zero line.](../images/E1_W5_R1_image2.png)
*This image shows why residuals are such a strong diagnostic tool: the plot turns a vague sense of mismatch into a pattern you can classify and act on.

The value of this taxonomy isn't just retrospective; it shapes how you design experiments and write predictions before any data is seen. When you commit to a prediction for the Tier 1 Phase 2 Prediction Memo, understanding which category of discrepancy is plausible helps you define your acceptance criterion precisely. If you expect only Case 1 (noise within uncertainty), a tight propagated uncertainty band is your pass/fail gate. If you suspect Case 3 (model-form error), your acceptance criterion should explicitly describe what systematic structure in the residuals would constitute evidence of a model-form failure, and your risk assessment should name the physical effect you think the model might be missing.

This taxonomy carries directly into E2, where residual plots are formalized as a required analytical tool and where the consequences of misclassifying a Case 3 gap as Case 1 (ignoring systematic model-form error as "just noise") become more consequential as the models grow more complex.

---

The verbal decision rule is simple to apply in practice: residuals within the uncertainty band → Case 1, no model revision required; mean outside the band but no trend in the residuals → Case 2, find the missing uncertainty source; consistent trend in the residuals → Case 3, find the missing physics. Apply this rule before reaching for a statistical test or a more complex analysis. The qualitative pattern is usually diagnostic enough to identify the category and point toward the appropriate response.

**The Takeaway:** Discrepancy between prediction and measurement isn't a grade penalty; it's an opportunity to identify which part of your model or measurement chain failed. The answer depends entirely on whether the gap is within your uncertainty bounds (Case 1), uniformly displaced beyond them (Case 2), or systematically structured (Case 3), and each category points to a different corrective action.

assessments.title: ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment

variants.text:
# ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment
## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis

**Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification
**Weeks:** 2–5
**CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5
**Structure:** Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4)
**Assessment:** Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) +
Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4)

---

## What This Assignment Is

The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all
three phases. The physical experiments differ: you'll be assigned a specific experiment
for each phase. But the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are
identical for all students, regardless of which experiment they're assigned.

Throughout all phases, use the course uncertainty convention in `E1_W2_R3_reading.md`:
put inputs on a standard-uncertainty basis before propagation and label every reported or
plotted interval by type, method, and coverage.

Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional,
and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive.

---

## Learning Goals

By the end of this assignment and its associated activities, you should be able to:

1. Execute an assigned experiment, record the relevant measurements in the required engineering format, and state a defensible uncertainty basis for each primary quantity.
2. Compare two sensor types or measurement methods for the same physical quantity and argue, with a named validation metric, whether the results are consistent enough for the intended use.
3. Build a blind prediction from theory, sensor specifications, and DAQ information before seeing data, then state what agreement threshold and anomalous pattern would matter.
4. Interpret discrepancies between prediction and measurement without post-hoc guesswork, using the comparison to identify model limitations, uncertainty drivers, and at least one experimentally informed improvement.
5. Synthesize evidence across two related experiments in a short technical presentation and explain what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why.

---

## Why Three Phases?

Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1.
It's the natural order. It's also a good starting point, but that's only the beginning.

A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it on top of a prediction.
Before a test ever runs, a test engineer asks: *what should the data look like?* They build
a model of the experiment itself, accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, and the
signal chain, then commit to a prediction. That prediction isn't decoration. It's used to
design the experiment. It determines whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of
interest, anticipates failure modes, and defines in advance what a meaningful result looks
like. The ability to watch data come in during a live test, immediately know whether it
matches expectation, and act on that in real time is one of the most important skills a
test engineer develops.

This course will get there. The full experimental design workflow, where your prediction
shapes the design of the test, is the focus of E3. For now, in E1 and E2, you are building
the foundational mindset: the habit of pre-committing to a prediction before you see the data.

This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see
a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model *before* you saw
the data, you can't dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is
real. You have to explain it.

That is Phase 2.

Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one experiment. Your partner
ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data: an
experiment you never ran, using only the setup description and sensor specs. This is
deliberate. The goal is not to mimic the full test engineer workflow, but to develop the
prediction-first mindset by working through it in the most challenging form: predicting an
experiment you have no direct experience with.

Think of Phase 3 as reverse-engineering the experiment. You are reconstructing what the data
should look like from the physics and the instrument chain alone, without the shortcut of
having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset, building a forward model
before you have the answer, is exactly what you'll need when you design your own experiment
from scratch in E3.

The three-phase structure is not the actual workflow of a test engineer. It is a scaffold for
developing the mindset of one.

---

## Assignment Overview

### Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual)

**What you are assigned:** One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool.
**What you do:** Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a
predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement.
**What you produce:** A Phase 1 Individual Report.
**When:** Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates).

### Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind)

**What you are assigned:** A *different* experiment from the Tier 1 pool: one you did
not run in Phase 1.
**What you do:** Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup
description for this unfamiliar experiment, predict what the data should look like.
You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware.
**What you produce:** A Phase 2 Prediction Memo, submitted before data is released.
**When:** Week 4 (prediction submitted; data released after the due date).

### Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4)

**Who you work with:** You'll be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment.
They ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment
as their Phase 2.
**What you do:** Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what
the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments.
**What you produce:** A combined oral presentation (lightning talk). You and your partner
present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4
presentation.
**When:** Week 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions).

---

## Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions

### The assignment in one sentence

Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare
your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model.

### Before the lab session and during the lab session

Complete items 1–4 before you arrive; items 5–8 are in-lab steps. Items 1–4 aren't graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable. They're required inputs to your Phase 1 report, and students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab.

1. **Read the experiment-specific lab document** for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document.
2. **Write a hypothesis**: a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]."
3. **Sketch an AMVF diagram** for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect. You'll refine this after the lab.
4. **Identify your independent and dependent variables.** Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor.
5. **Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods** to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis.
6. **Record your measurements** using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound).
7. **Use MATLAB** for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used.
8. **Note anomalies in real time.** If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately: when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence.

### Phase 1 report — required sections

**1. Experimental design**
Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least
one confounding factor identified and addressed.

**2. AMVF annotation**
Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one)
to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan.

**3. Theory — confirm the model and understand it**
The experiment-specific lab document provides a theoretical model for the physical phenomenon
you are measuring. You are not expected to re-derive it from scratch, but you are expected
to demonstrate that you understand it well enough to use it.

This means identifying the physical principle(s) the model applies and confirming that the
dimensions are consistent. Name at least one simplifying assumption the model makes, and
describe the condition under which that assumption would break down. Then show the working
equation with your specific experimental values substituted in. This is the prediction your
model makes for your particular setup. If you cannot do this, you are not ready to run the
analysis in the sections that follow.

**4. Measurement record**
Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each.
Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table.

**5. Multi-sensor comparison**
Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a
named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold,
and the conclusion numerically, not just visually.

**6. Uncertainty propagation**
For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation
using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error
sources. Show your work.

**7. Model comparison**
Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a
named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance
criterion. Interpret any discrepancy: is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model
form limitations, or both?

**8. Experimentally informed model**
Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be
updated or refined. This isn't about accepting or rejecting the model. It's about using
the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what
physical reasoning supports that change.

**9. MATLAB figures**
All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty
bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the
graph presentation framework from E1 W2.

**10. Plain-language summary**
A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer.
This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix.

**11. Predict-from-specs**
Using only the sensor specifications and DAQ configuration provided in your experiment-specific lab document for the primary sensor identified by the instructional team, estimate what the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity should be before you use any measured data.
Propagate the uncertainty from the stated spec-sheet parameters.
Sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and approximate uncertainty bounds.
This workflow is the conceptual foundation for Phase 2, where you will apply the same predict-from-specs procedure to a complete experiment you have never run.

### Phase 1 report — format and submission

- Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections.
- Format: LaTeX. Submit a single `.zip` archive containing your LaTeX source (`.tex` file(s) and any figure files) and the compiled PDF. Figures should be embedded in the compiled PDF.
- See **E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX** if you haven't used LaTeX before.
- Submission: See Canvas for due date.

---

## Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions

### The assignment in one sentence

Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you've never
run: predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit
it before you see any real data.

### What "reverse" means

In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it.
In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work
backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not
just of the physical phenomenon. You are essentially reverse-engineering the experiment.

Note that in Phase 2 you are predicting the output of a single, specified sensor: the one
described in your experiment packet. Phase 1 required you to use two sensors and compare
them; Phase 2 is a prediction exercise, and your packet will identify which sensor and
configuration to base your analysis on. Use only that sensor's specifications for your
uncertainty propagation.

This requires you to think about:
- What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup?
- What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs?
- How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity?
- What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs?
- Where are the likely failure modes, and what could produce data that looks anomalous?

A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate
whether an experiment is worth running and whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of
interest. It's also how you determine whether you'll be able to detect anomalies in real
time during the test.

### What you receive for Phase 2

You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment.
The theory primer in that packet is the same document the students who ran the experiment received.
It contains:
- The theory primer for the experiment
- The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent)
- The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments)
- A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions
- The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab

You do **not** receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the
student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet.

### Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections

**1. Predicted system response**
What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key
features. For example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s during the first 5 s, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions.

**2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity**
Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet.
Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result
as: *[quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units].*

**3. Predicted plot sketch**
Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs.
time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included.
Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction.

**4. Dominant uncertainty sources**
List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution.
Justify the ranking using your propagation results.

**5. Acceptance criterion**
Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of
your predicted value, you will consider the prediction reasonably supported. State this threshold
before you see the data.

**6. Risk assessment**
Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data, not because of a faulty
experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Use the Week 3
anomalous-data-signature framework here: name what “wrong” would look like physically, not a
generic equipment-failure scenario. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you
would investigate first.

**Submission:** The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release
date. Once submitted, the version you turn in is frozen for the blind comparison and is the
version that will be graded as your original commitment. After data is released, you are
encouraged to keep iterating on your thinking, but preserve the original memo and track how
your interpretation changes so the evolution of your reasoning remains traceable.

### Grading philosophy for Phase 2

**A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning
and committed uncertainty analysis, is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be
right but was derived from vague intuition.**

The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This
isn't arbitrary. It's the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a
wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy and demonstrates a deeper
attempt at understanding. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing durable from the result.

This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have
questions about it, ask before you submit.

---

## Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions

### The pairing

You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your
Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2.
This means:
- You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A
- Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A
- You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B
- Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B

Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared.

### The presentation structure

Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately
8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time).

**Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1)**
Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what
their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student.

**Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment)**
Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment: what they
expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then what the actual data showed.
Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student.

**Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly)**
The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will
likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together
tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither
experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you
change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair.

*One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make
that the other experiment’s model also makes, and where do those assumptions diverge?
The most interesting synthesis often lives in that gap.*

### Group of 4 (if applicable)

If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked
on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly. Each pair presents Segments
1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together.
This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments.

The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section
before Week 4.

### Peer evaluation

Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for each presentation.
Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations.
A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness
with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation. Vague
evaluations ("good job, maybe add more data") receive no credit.

**See the Tier 1 student rubric in this assignment package for the presentation rubric and peer evaluation criteria.**

---

## Assignment Timeline Summary

| Phase | Activity | Format | When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-lab | Hypothesis, AMVF sketch, variable identification | Bring to lab | Before W2 lab session |
| Phase 1 | Run experiment, analyze data | Lab sessions | W2–W4 |
| Phase 1 | Individual report | Written, Canvas | Due W4 (see Canvas) |
| Phase 2 | Receive experiment packet | — | W4 release date |
| Phase 2 | Prediction memo (submitted before data release) | Written, Canvas | W4 deadline (before data release) |
| Phase 3 | Paired presentation (lightning talk) | In-person, in class | W5 |
| Phase 3 | Peer evaluations | Written, submitted same day | W5 |

---

## Resources

- E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
- E1 Week 3 Lecture: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics

assessments.body_markdown:
# ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment
## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis

**Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification
**Weeks:** 2–5
**CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5
**Structure:** Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4)
**Assessment:** Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) +
Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4)

---

## What This Assignment Is

The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all
three phases. The physical experiments differ: you'll be assigned a specific experiment
for each phase. But the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are
identical for all students, regardless of which experiment they're assigned.

Throughout all phases, use the course uncertainty convention in `E1_W2_R3_reading.md`:
put inputs on a standard-uncertainty basis before propagation and label every reported or
plotted interval by type, method, and coverage.

Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional,
and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive.

---

## Learning Goals

By the end of this assignment and its associated activities, you should be able to:

1. Execute an assigned experiment, record the relevant measurements in the required engineering format, and state a defensible uncertainty basis for each primary quantity.
2. Compare two sensor types or measurement methods for the same physical quantity and argue, with a named validation metric, whether the results are consistent enough for the intended use.
3. Build a blind prediction from theory, sensor specifications, and DAQ information before seeing data, then state what agreement threshold and anomalous pattern would matter.
4. Interpret discrepancies between prediction and measurement without post-hoc guesswork, using the comparison to identify model limitations, uncertainty drivers, and at least one experimentally informed improvement.
5. Synthesize evidence across two related experiments in a short technical presentation and explain what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why.

---

## Why Three Phases?

Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1.
It's the natural order. It's also a good starting point, but that's only the beginning.

A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it on top of a prediction.
Before a test ever runs, a test engineer asks: *what should the data look like?* They build
a model of the experiment itself, accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, and the
signal chain, then commit to a prediction. That prediction isn't decoration. It's used to
design the experiment. It determines whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of
interest, anticipates failure modes, and defines in advance what a meaningful result looks
like. The ability to watch data come in during a live test, immediately know whether it
matches expectation, and act on that in real time is one of the most important skills a
test engineer develops.

This course will get there. The full experimental design workflow, where your prediction
shapes the design of the test, is the focus of E3. For now, in E1 and E2, you are building
the foundational mindset: the habit of pre-committing to a prediction before you see the data.

This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see
a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model *before* you saw
the data, you can't dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is
real. You have to explain it.

That is Phase 2.

Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one experiment. Your partner
ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data: an
experiment you never ran, using only the setup description and sensor specs. This is
deliberate. The goal is not to mimic the full test engineer workflow, but to develop the
prediction-first mindset by working through it in the most challenging form: predicting an
experiment you have no direct experience with.

Think of Phase 3 as reverse-engineering the experiment. You are reconstructing what the data
should look like from the physics and the instrument chain alone, without the shortcut of
having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset, building a forward model
before you have the answer, is exactly what you'll need when you design your own experiment
from scratch in E3.

The three-phase structure is not the actual workflow of a test engineer. It is a scaffold for
developing the mindset of one.

---

## Assignment Overview

### Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual)

**What you are assigned:** One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool.
**What you do:** Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a
predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement.
**What you produce:** A Phase 1 Individual Report.
**When:** Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates).

### Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind)

**What you are assigned:** A *different* experiment from the Tier 1 pool: one you did
not run in Phase 1.
**What you do:** Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup
description for this unfamiliar experiment, predict what the data should look like.
You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware.
**What you produce:** A Phase 2 Prediction Memo, submitted before data is released.
**When:** Week 4 (prediction submitted; data released after the due date).

### Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4)

**Who you work with:** You'll be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment.
They ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment
as their Phase 2.
**What you do:** Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what
the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments.
**What you produce:** A combined oral presentation (lightning talk). You and your partner
present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4
presentation.
**When:** Week 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions).

---

## Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions

### The assignment in one sentence

Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare
your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model.

### Before the lab session and during the lab session

Complete items 1–4 before you arrive; items 5–8 are in-lab steps. Items 1–4 aren't graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable. They're required inputs to your Phase 1 report, and students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab.

1. **Read the experiment-specific lab document** for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document.
2. **Write a hypothesis**: a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]."
3. **Sketch an AMVF diagram** for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect. You'll refine this after the lab.
4. **Identify your independent and dependent variables.** Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor.
5. **Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods** to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis.
6. **Record your measurements** using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound).
7. **Use MATLAB** for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used.
8. **Note anomalies in real time.** If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately: when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence.

### Phase 1 report — required sections

**1. Experimental design**
Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least
one confounding factor identified and addressed.

**2. AMVF annotation**
Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one)
to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan.

**3. Theory — confirm the model and understand it**
The experiment-specific lab document provides a theoretical model for the physical phenomenon
you are measuring. You are not expected to re-derive it from scratch, but you are expected
to demonstrate that you understand it well enough to use it.

This means identifying the physical principle(s) the model applies and confirming that the
dimensions are consistent. Name at least one simplifying assumption the model makes, and
describe the condition under which that assumption would break down. Then show the working
equation with your specific experimental values substituted in. This is the prediction your
model makes for your particular setup. If you cannot do this, you are not ready to run the
analysis in the sections that follow.

**4. Measurement record**
Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each.
Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table.

**5. Multi-sensor comparison**
Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a
named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold,
and the conclusion numerically, not just visually.

**6. Uncertainty propagation**
For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation
using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error
sources. Show your work.

**7. Model comparison**
Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a
named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance
criterion. Interpret any discrepancy: is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model
form limitations, or both?

**8. Experimentally informed model**
Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be
updated or refined. This isn't about accepting or rejecting the model. It's about using
the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what
physical reasoning supports that change.

**9. MATLAB figures**
All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty
bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the
graph presentation framework from E1 W2.

**10. Plain-language summary**
A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer.
This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix.

**11. Predict-from-specs**
Using only the sensor specifications and DAQ configuration provided in your experiment-specific lab document for the primary sensor identified by the instructional team, estimate what the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity should be before you use any measured data.
Propagate the uncertainty from the stated spec-sheet parameters.
Sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and approximate uncertainty bounds.
This workflow is the conceptual foundation for Phase 2, where you will apply the same predict-from-specs procedure to a complete experiment you have never run.

### Phase 1 report — format and submission

- Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections.
- Format: LaTeX. Submit a single `.zip` archive containing your LaTeX source (`.tex` file(s) and any figure files) and the compiled PDF. Figures should be embedded in the compiled PDF.
- See **E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX** if you haven't used LaTeX before.
- Submission: See Canvas for due date.

---

## Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions

### The assignment in one sentence

Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you've never
run: predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit
it before you see any real data.

### What "reverse" means

In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it.
In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work
backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not
just of the physical phenomenon. You are essentially reverse-engineering the experiment.

Note that in Phase 2 you are predicting the output of a single, specified sensor: the one
described in your experiment packet. Phase 1 required you to use two sensors and compare
them; Phase 2 is a prediction exercise, and your packet will identify which sensor and
configuration to base your analysis on. Use only that sensor's specifications for your
uncertainty propagation.

This requires you to think about:
- What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup?
- What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs?
- How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity?
- What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs?
- Where are the likely failure modes, and what could produce data that looks anomalous?

A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate
whether an experiment is worth running and whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of
interest. It's also how you determine whether you'll be able to detect anomalies in real
time during the test.

### What you receive for Phase 2

You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment.
The theory primer in that packet is the same document the students who ran the experiment received.
It contains:
- The theory primer for the experiment
- The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent)
- The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments)
- A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions
- The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab

You do **not** receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the
student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet.

### Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections

**1. Predicted system response**
What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key
features. For example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s during the first 5 s, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions.

**2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity**
Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet.
Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result
as: *[quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units].*

**3. Predicted plot sketch**
Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs.
time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included.
Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction.

**4. Dominant uncertainty sources**
List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution.
Justify the ranking using your propagation results.

**5. Acceptance criterion**
Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of
your predicted value, you will consider the prediction reasonably supported. State this threshold
before you see the data.

**6. Risk assessment**
Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data, not because of a faulty
experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Use the Week 3
anomalous-data-signature framework here: name what “wrong” would look like physically, not a
generic equipment-failure scenario. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you
would investigate first.

**Submission:** The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release
date. Once submitted, the version you turn in is frozen for the blind comparison and is the
version that will be graded as your original commitment. After data is released, you are
encouraged to keep iterating on your thinking, but preserve the original memo and track how
your interpretation changes so the evolution of your reasoning remains traceable.

### Grading philosophy for Phase 2

**A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning
and committed uncertainty analysis, is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be
right but was derived from vague intuition.**

The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This
isn't arbitrary. It's the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a
wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy and demonstrates a deeper
attempt at understanding. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing durable from the result.

This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have
questions about it, ask before you submit.

---

## Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions

### The pairing

You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your
Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2.
This means:
- You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A
- Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A
- You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B
- Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B

Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared.

### The presentation structure

Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately
8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time).

**Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1)**
Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what
their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student.

**Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment)**
Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment: what they
expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then what the actual data showed.
Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student.

**Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly)**
The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will
likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together
tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither
experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you
change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair.

*One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make
that the other experiment’s model also makes, and where do those assumptions diverge?
The most interesting synthesis often lives in that gap.*

### Group of 4 (if applicable)

If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked
on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly. Each pair presents Segments
1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together.
This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments.

The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section
before Week 4.

### Peer evaluation

Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for each presentation.
Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations.
A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness
with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation. Vague
evaluations ("good job, maybe add more data") receive no credit.

**See the Tier 1 student rubric in this assignment package for the presentation rubric and peer evaluation criteria.**

---

## Assignment Timeline Summary

| Phase | Activity | Format | When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-lab | Hypothesis, AMVF sketch, variable identification | Bring to lab | Before W2 lab session |
| Phase 1 | Run experiment, analyze data | Lab sessions | W2–W4 |
| Phase 1 | Individual report | Written, Canvas | Due W4 (see Canvas) |
| Phase 2 | Receive experiment packet | — | W4 release date |
| Phase 2 | Prediction memo (submitted before data release) | Written, Canvas | W4 deadline (before data release) |
| Phase 3 | Paired presentation (lightning talk) | In-person, in class | W5 |
| Phase 3 | Peer evaluations | Written, submitted same day | W5 |

---

## Resources

- E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
- E1 Week 3 Lecture: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics
- E1 Week 3 Reading: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework
- E1 Week 4 Lab: Full Phase I Pipeline (this is Phase 1 of the Tier 1 assignment)
- E1 Week 5 Discussion: Lightning Talks & Peer Assessment
- E0 Supplemental: Technical Writing Basics and Presentation Best Practices
- E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice
- E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX
- Experiment-specific lab document (provided separately for your assigned experiment)
- Coleman & Steele, 4th ed. — reference for uncertainty propagation

## Student Package Note

Use this assignment together with the **Tier 1 student rubric** included in the student-facing assignment package. The rubric gives the pass criteria and evidence standards for the written report, prediction memo, presentation, and peer evaluations.

---

experiment packet contents*

variants.text:
# Tier 1 Lab Assignment — Frequently Asked Questions

## 1. Is Tier 1 one lab, or several connected deliverables?

Tier 1 is one assignment sequence with three connected phases.
Phase 1 is your own experiment and report.
Phase 2 is your blind prediction for a different experiment.
Phase 3 is the paired comparison and synthesis presentation.
The phases overlap in time, so do not wait to finish one completely before reading the next.

## 2. Do I use the same experiment in all three phases?

No.
In Phase 1, you run one assigned experiment from the Tier 1 pool.
In Phase 2, you receive a different experiment packet and predict that experiment before seeing any data.
In Phase 3, you compare your Phase 2 prediction to the real data from that second experiment, and your partner does the same for yours.

## 3. What exactly do I need before I arrive for the Phase 1 lab session?

Bring the Phase 1 pre-lab work listed in the assignment:
- your one-sentence hypothesis
- your initial AMVF sketch
- your independent and dependent variables
- at least one control condition and one possible confounding factor

These are not graded as a separate pre-lab submission, but they are required inputs to the Phase 1 report.
Students who arrive without them usually lose time during the lab.

## 4. Do I really need two sensors or two measurement methods in Phase 1?

Yes.
That comparison is required, not optional.
Tier 1 is designed to make you compare independent ways of measuring the same quantity, then argue numerically whether they agree.
A visual overlay by itself is not enough; your report must apply a named validation metric and state the threshold and conclusion clearly.

## 5. What is the predict-from-specs task in Phase 1?

Phase 1 includes one predict-from-specs exercise.
Use the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ information for the primary sensor identified in your experiment-specific lab materials.
Before relying on measured data, propagate those specification-based uncertainties forward to estimate the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity, then sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and uncertainty bounds.
This is practice for the larger Phase 2 prediction workflow.

## 6. What is in the Phase 2 Experiment Packet?

The packet includes:
- the theory primer for the experiment
- the sensor specifications
- the DAQ configuration
- the physical setup description and nominal operating conditions
- the pre-lab hypothesis submitted by the student who ran that experiment

It does **not** include the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report.
Your prediction must come entirely from the packet.

## 7. What does “blind” mean in Phase 2?

It means you commit to the prediction before you see the data.
That commitment is the point of the exercise.
If the comparison is made after the data is already known, the prediction loses its evidentiary value.

## 8. If my prediction memo is frozen, am I allowed to change my mind later?

You may continue refining your thinking after the data is released, but the version you submit before data release is the original commitment used for the blind comparison.
Keep that original memo intact.
If your interpretation evolves later, track the change clearly so the evolution of your reasoning is traceable.

## 9. Is Phase 2 graded on whether my prediction is correct?

No.
Phase 2 is graded on the quality of your reasoning, not on raw prediction accuracy.
A prediction built from sound physical reasoning, explicit uncertainty analysis, and a clear acceptance criterion is more valuable than a lucky right answer with vague logic behind it.
The memo should show how you thought, not just what number you guessed.

## 10. What counts as a good risk assessment in Phase 2?

A good risk assessment names what “wrong” would look like physically.
It should identify an anomalous data pattern that would suggest a model limitation or physics edge case, not a generic equipment failure.
Use the Week 3 anomalous-data-signature framework here.
For example, a useful risk statement might name hysteresis, a systematic trend, or a shape change in the response that the model cannot explain.

## 11. What should the Phase 2 prediction memo include?

The memo must include all six required elements:
- predicted system response
- predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity
- predicted plot sketch with units and uncertainty bounds
- dominant uncertainty sources
- acceptance criterion
- risk assessment

If one of these is missing, the prediction is incomplete.

## 12. How strict is the acceptance criterion requirement?

It must be numerical and stated before data release.
“Close to the prediction” is not enough.
You need to define what counts as adequate agreement before you compare the prediction to the actual data.

## 13. What happens in Phase 3?

Phase 3 is the paired comparison and synthesis presentation.
You and your partner present:
- your own Phase 1 result
- your own Phase 2 prediction of the other experiment
- a joint synthesis across both experiments

If logistics allow, two pairs may be combined into a group of 4 for the presentation, but the core comparison structure stays the same.

## 14. Are peer evaluations really graded?

Yes.
Peer evaluation is a required part of Phase 3.
A strong peer evaluation names a specific strength, a specific weakness, and a concrete improvement recommendation, each supported by evidence from the presentation.
Generic praise or vague criticism does not satisfy the requirement.

## 15. Where should I look if I want the exact pass criteria?

Use `TIER1_rubric_student.md` alongside the assignment.
The assignment tells you what to produce.
The student rubric tells you what a passing response looks like in each phase.
You should read both before you begin data collection.

---

*ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 FAQ | Companion to `TIER1_assignment.md` and `TIER1_rubric_student.md`*
*Evergreen — structure, expectations, and grading logic are stable across experiment assignments*

assessments.body_markdown:
# Tier 1 Lab Assignment — Frequently Asked Questions

## 1. Is Tier 1 one lab, or several connected deliverables?

Tier 1 is one assignment sequence with three connected phases.
Phase 1 is your own experiment and report.
Phase 2 is your blind prediction for a different experiment.
Phase 3 is the paired comparison and synthesis presentation.
The phases overlap in time, so do not wait to finish one completely before reading the next.

## 2. Do I use the same experiment in all three phases?

No.
In Phase 1, you run one assigned experiment from the Tier 1 pool.
In Phase 2, you receive a different experiment packet and predict that experiment before seeing any data.
In Phase 3, you compare your Phase 2 prediction to the real data from that second experiment, and your partner does the same for yours.

## 3. What exactly do I need before I arrive for the Phase 1 lab session?

Bring the Phase 1 pre-lab work listed in the assignment:
- your one-sentence hypothesis
- your initial AMVF sketch
- your independent and dependent variables
- at least one control condition and one possible confounding factor

These are not graded as a separate pre-lab submission, but they are required inputs to the Phase 1 report.
Students who arrive without them usually lose time during the lab.

## 4. Do I really need two sensors or two measurement methods in Phase 1?

Yes.
That comparison is required, not optional.
Tier 1 is designed to make you compare independent ways of measuring the same quantity, then argue numerically whether they agree.
A visual overlay by itself is not enough; your report must apply a named validation metric and state the threshold and conclusion clearly.

## 5. What is the predict-from-specs task in Phase 1?

Phase 1 includes one predict-from-specs exercise.
Use the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ information for the primary sensor identified in your experiment-specific lab materials.
Before relying on measured data, propagate those specification-based uncertainties forward to estimate the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity, then sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and uncertainty bounds.
This is practice for the larger Phase 2 prediction workflow.

## 6. What is in the Phase 2 Experiment Packet?

The packet includes:
- the theory primer for the experiment
- the sensor specifications
- the DAQ configuration
- the physical setup description and nominal operating conditions
- the pre-lab hypothesis submitted by the student who ran that experiment

It does **not** include the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report.
Your prediction must come entirely from the packet.

## 7. What does “blind” mean in Phase 2?

It means you commit to the prediction before you see the data.
That commitment is the point of the exercise.
If the comparison is made after the data is already known, the prediction loses its evidentiary value.

## 8. If my prediction memo is frozen, am I allowed to change my mind later?

You may continue refining your thinking after the data is released, but the version you submit before data release is the original commitment used for the blind comparison.
Keep that original memo intact.
If your interpretation evolves later, track the change clearly so the evolution of your reasoning is traceable.

## 9. Is Phase 2 graded on whether my prediction is correct?

No.
Phase 2 is graded on the quality of your reasoning, not on raw prediction accuracy.
A prediction built from sound physical reasoning, explicit uncertainty analysis, and a clear acceptance criterion is more valuable than a lucky right answer with vague logic behind it.
The memo should show how you thought, not just what number you guessed.

## 10. What counts as a good risk assessment in Phase 2?

A good risk assessment names what “wrong” would look like physically.
It should identify an anomalous data pattern that would suggest a model limitation or physics edge case, not a generic equipment failure.
Use the Week 3 anomalous-data-signature framework here.
For example, a useful risk statement might name hysteresis, a systematic trend, or a shape change in the response that the model cannot explain.

## 11. What should the Phase 2 prediction memo include?

The memo must include all six required elements:
- predicted system response
- predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity
- predicted plot sketch with units and uncertainty bounds
- dominant uncertainty sources
- acceptance criterion
- risk assessment

If one of these is missing, the prediction is incomplete.

## 12. How strict is the acceptance criterion requirement?

It must be numerical and stated before data release.
“Close to the prediction” is not enough.
You need to define what counts as adequate agreement before you compare the prediction to the actual data.

## 13. What happens in Phase 3?

Phase 3 is the paired comparison and synthesis presentation.
You and your partner present:
- your own Phase 1 result
- your own Phase 2 prediction of the other experiment
- a joint synthesis across both experiments

If logistics allow, two pairs may be combined into a group of 4 for the presentation, but the core comparison structure stays the same.

## 14. Are peer evaluations really graded?

Yes.
Peer evaluation is a required part of Phase 3.
A strong peer evaluation names a specific strength, a specific weakness, and a concrete improvement recommendation, each supported by evidence from the presentation.
Generic praise or vague criticism does not satisfy the requirement.

## 15. Where should I look if I want the exact pass criteria?

Use `TIER1_rubric_student.md` alongside the assignment.
The assignment tells you what to produce.
The student rubric tells you what a passing response looks like in each phase.
You should read both before you begin data collection.

---

*ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 FAQ | Companion to `TIER1_assignment.md` and `TIER1_rubric_student.md`*
*Evergreen — structure, expectations, and grading logic are stable across experiment assignments*

activity_meta.title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look
activity_meta.purpose: Directly primes students for the Tier 1 Phase 2 prediction memo, which they will begin in this week's lab session. The lecture opens with the distinction that separates a prediction from a guess: a prediction is falsifiable, committed in writing before data is seen, and derived from reasoning that can be evaluated independently of whether the prediction turned out to be correct. The lecture walks through the five elements that every defensible prediction must contain — (1) system response features with committed specific values or ranges (not vague directional claims), (2) an uncertainty estimate derived from the available sensor specs using the propagation methods from E1 W2, (3) a sketch of the expected output with axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before any data is seen, (4) a ranked list of dominant uncertainty sources with estimated magnitudes, (5) a named acceptance criterion stated as a numerical threshold. Each element is illustrated using a worked example from an experiment the class has not used — the instructor walks through how to extract a prediction from theory and specs alone, without touching hardware. **The grading philosophy:** This lecture must communicate the Phase 2 grading standard explicitly: a prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and complete committed analysis is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The goal of pre-commitment is not to be correct — it is to be honest about what you knew before you saw the data, so the discrepancy teaches you something. This philosophy echoes through E2 and E3: it is the professional norm for test engineers, and students should hear it named clearly before Phase 2 begins.

activity_meta.title: The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough
activity_meta.purpose: The instructor walks through a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that no student in the room ran as their Phase 1. This is not a polished demonstration — it is a transparent modeling exercise, done in real time, with the instructor narrating every decision. The sequence mirrors exactly what students must do in Phase 2 and Phase 3: read the theory primer and sensor specs → identify the primary output and its expected range → apply partial derivative propagation to estimate uncertainty → sketch the expected time-series or distribution with committed bounds → define an acceptance criterion → then reveal the actual data and compare. The comparison includes explicit narration of what the discrepancy reveals: is the gap within the propagated uncertainty band (consistent with measurement noise), or does the residual show a systematic trend (evidence of a model-form limitation)? The instructor names which category applies and explains the physical interpretation. **Bridge to E2:** This lecture closes E1 by making explicit what E2 will demand. In E1, students ran their own experiments and separately predicted someone else's. In E2, prediction before exposure is not an exercise — it is the required gate before any hardware access. The canned experiments in E2 are more complex, the models have more parameters, and the consequences of a vague pre-commitment are more visible. What students practiced in Tier 1 Phase 2 is the same skill, now applied under higher stakes. The instructor should close this lecture — and E1 — with the question that opens E2: *what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?*

activity_meta.title: Discrepancy Is Data — Interpreting What the Gap Tells You
activity_meta.purpose: A short reading that frames discrepancy between a committed prediction and measured data not as failure but as information. Three categories of discrepancy are described and distinguished: (1) the gap falls within the propagated uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise, no model revision required; (2) the gap is larger than the uncertainty band but has no systematic structure — suggests the uncertainty was underestimated, prompting a review of which error sources were omitted; (3) the gap shows a systematic trend (residuals increase with the independent variable, or have a consistent sign) — evidence of a model-form limitation, where the model is missing a physical effect. The reading gives brief examples of each: a bounce ball trajectory where air resistance is ignored produces a systematically optimistic prediction at long times (category 3); a pendulum period measurement with an over-optimistic stopwatch uncertainty bound produces a gap that looks large but may simply reflect an underestimated error source (category 2); and a spring-mass natural frequency estimate where scatter is within the uncertainty band (category 1). Students are expected to apply this taxonomy in both the Tier 1 Phase 2 comparison and the Phase 3 synthesis segment. This reading bridges directly to E2 W8, where the same taxonomy is formalized using residual plots and applied to the more complex Tier 2 canned experiments.

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction Exercise
activity_meta.purpose: Each student is assigned a canned experiment from the Tier 1 pool that they did not run in Phase 1. They receive the Phase 2 Experiment Packet (theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran it) — but not the data. Before data is released, they develop a predictive model, specify system response features and expected trends, identify dominant uncertainty sources, sketch the expected output, and define an acceptance criterion. This commitment is submitted as the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Data is then released, and students compare their prediction to the measured outcomes. The pairing is complementary: the student who receives Experiment B's packet ran Experiment A as their Phase 1, and the student who ran Experiment B is now receiving Experiment A's packet. This sets up the Phase 3 combined presentation.

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 2 — Prediction Memo (submitted before data release)
activity_meta.purpose: The pre-data-release commitment document. See `TIER1_assignment.md` for the full student-facing specification. Required sections: (1) predicted system response — specific values and ranges, expected trend shape committed in writing; (2) predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — partial derivatives applied to the packet's sensor specs, result stated as estimate ± uncertainty; (3) predicted plot sketch — hand-drawn or MATLAB, with uncertainty bounds, labeled axes; (4) dominant uncertainty sources ranked by contribution; (5) acceptance criterion defined as a numerical threshold before data is seen; (6) risk assessment — at least one anomalous data pattern identified that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. Graded on quality of reasoning and completeness of commitment — not on whether the prediction turned out to be correct. Right prediction from wrong reasoning scores lower than wrong prediction from sound reasoning — this grading philosophy must be communicated explicitly in the rubric and in class before Phase 2 begins.

activity_meta.title: Tier 1 Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis Presentation (Lightning Talk)
activity_meta.purpose: Each paired group delivers a combined lightning talk presenting both the forward analysis and the reverse prediction, with a joint synthesis conclusion. Audience members complete structured written peer evaluation forms. Students receive grades both for the quality of their own presentation and for the quality of their written peer evaluations — the critique grade is weighted equally to the production grade. This establishes the peer assessment culture that recurs in E2 Week 10 and E3 Week 14. A class-wide debrief closes E1 with the question that opens E2: *if you had to predict before measuring in every experiment, what would you need to know before touching hardware?* ## 3. Bridge to Next Week The cross-prediction exercise made prediction-before-measurement feel real, and the exploratory example lecture made the complete workflow visible. E2 begins by formalizing this requirement — students must now develop predictive models for complex aerospace systems before any experiment begins, and the modeling decisions are less obvious and less forgiving than in E1.

Define intended use as the primary framing constraint on all predictive modeling decisions — CLO 3

Articulate how experimental results can be used to inform and update a predictive model — not merely validate it — as the stated purpose of every E2 canned experiment — CLO 3
