lectures.summary: Introduces the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem and aliasing as the fundamental constraints on any digital measurement system. Covers signal bandwidth as the property of the physical phenomenon being measured (not a property of the sensor), the aliasing mechanism — how undersampled signals fold back into the measured spectrum — and the practical consequences for aerospace measurements where signal content may span many orders of frequency. Students work through examples to develop intuition for when sampling rate matters critically.
lectures.purpose: Introduces the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem and aliasing as the fundamental constraints on any digital measurement system. Covers signal bandwidth as the property of the physical phenomenon being measured (not a property of the sensor), the aliasing mechanism — how undersampled signals fold back into the measured spectrum — and the practical consequences for aerospace measurements where signal content may span many orders of frequency. Students work through examples to develop intuition for when sampling rate matters critically.
learning_goals: Apply the Nyquist sampling theorem to identify the minimum sampling rate required for a given signal bandwidth, and predict the consequences of aliasing when the theorem is violated — CLO 4
Explain the purpose and design rationale of an anti-aliasing filter, and identify appropriate filter cutoff frequency relative to signal bandwidth and sampling rate — CLO 4
Identify and describe at least three common DAQ configuration choices — channel configuration, resolution, sample rate — and articulate the tradeoff each choice imposes — CLO 4
Visualize a measured signal in both time and frequency domain using MATLAB and identify signal bandwidth from the frequency-domain representation — CLO 4
lectures.body_markdown:
slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Nyquist Sampling, Aliasing & Signal Bandwidth
Talking Point: Before you can run a credible measurement on a complex aerospace system, you need to understand what you are asking the digital measurement system to do. A sensor converts a physical quantity into an analog electrical signal — a continuous stream of information about the world. A data acquisition system takes discrete snapshots of that signal at a fixed rate. Everything about whether those snapshots faithfully represent the original signal depends on one thing: how fast you sample relative to how fast the signal changes. That relationship is governed by the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, and when it is violated, the result is not noise — it is a plausible-looking signal that is completely wrong. That is the subject of this lecture.

slide 2: What Signal Bandwidth Actually Is
**Signal bandwidth** — the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information in the signal — is a property of the physical phenomenon, not the sensor or DAQ.
(Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: a time-domain signal showing a complex waveform — a mix of a slow sinusoidal component and a faster oscillation superimposed on it. The signal is continuous, plotted as a smooth analog curve, with a label "Measured physical phenomenon — continuous in time." Right panel: the same signal's frequency-domain representation (FFT magnitude spectrum) plotted as a bar chart, with two visible peaks labeled "Low-frequency component: 10 Hz" and "High-frequency component: 340 Hz." A bracket spans from 0 to 340 Hz and is labeled "Signal bandwidth: 340 Hz — this is a property of the phenomenon, not the sensor." The visual contrast between the time domain (messy, complex) and the frequency domain (clean, peaks visible) is the main teaching point of this slide.)
Talking Point: Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring — it is not a property of your sensor or your DAQ system. It describes the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information about the signal. A structural vibration signal from an aerospace component might have a dominant resonance at 10 hertz and a higher-frequency mode at 340 hertz. The signal bandwidth of that measurement extends to 340 hertz — regardless of what sample rate you choose. Why does this matter? Because if you choose a sample rate without knowing the signal bandwidth, you are making a claim about the signal's frequency content without evidence. The claim you are making is: "I believe this signal has no content above half my sample rate." If that claim is wrong, your data is not merely imprecise — it is silently corrupted in a way that looks real.

slide 3: The Sampling Theorem — The Fundamental Constraint
(Image: A diagram showing a continuous analog signal (smooth sinusoidal curve) with two sets of sample points overlaid. The top set of samples is spaced closely — labeled "Sample rate = 10× signal frequency." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a dashed curve through the sample points, closely tracks the original. The bottom set of samples is spaced too widely — labeled "Sample rate = 0.8× signal frequency (undersampled)." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a red dashed curve, produces a much lower-frequency sinusoid that does not match the original. A boxed statement in the center of the slide reads: "Nyquist Criterion: sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal." The original signal and the alias are clearly color-coded — original in gold, alias in red.)
Talking Point: The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem tells us the minimum sample rate required to faithfully represent a signal. The requirement is simple: your sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal you are trying to measure. That frequency — half the sample rate — is called the Nyquist frequency, and it is the boundary above which information is lost. Note that I said "greater than twice" — not "equal to twice." In practice, engineers apply a margin above the theoretical minimum because real signals do not have perfectly sharp cutoffs. The practical rule is that your sample rate should be at least five to ten times the highest frequency of interest, depending on how well you can characterize the signal's frequency content. Choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment: you are claiming that the signal has no content above the Nyquist frequency, and if that claim is wrong, you will not know it from the data alone.

slide 4: What Aliasing Actually Looks Like — The Stroboscopic Wheel
(Image: A three-frame stroboscopic effect illustration. Frame 1: A wheel with a single marker dot, spinning at 10 revolutions per second, is illuminated by a strobe light flashing at 12 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly forward — dotted arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow forward." Frame 2: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 9 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly backward — arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow backward." Frame 3: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 10 Hz. The wheel appears to stop — labeled "Apparent motion: stationary." Below the three frames: "None of these stroboscopic observations are noise. They are coherent, plausible, and completely wrong." A caption reads: "This is aliasing. The observed frequency is the difference between the signal and the strobe — it is not in the original signal at all.")
Talking Point: The most important thing I want you to understand about aliasing is that it does not produce obvious noise — it produces a coherent, plausible-looking signal at a different frequency. The stroboscopic wheel is the perfect illustration. When a wheel spinning at ten revolutions per second is illuminated by a strobe flashing at twelve hertz, it appears to spin slowly forward at two revolutions per second. Your DAQ is the strobe — it observes the voltage signal at fixed intervals. If those intervals are too far apart relative to how fast the signal changes, the reconstructed waveform is as wrong as the backward-spinning wheel. The wheel is not actually slowing down — your measurement system is creating a false low-frequency signal out of the difference between the true signal and your sample rate. This is aliasing. The alias frequency is the difference between the true signal frequency and the sampling frequency, and it falls inside your measurement bandwidth — which is exactly why it is dangerous. You cannot look at an aliased signal and know it is wrong by visual inspection. It looks like a real, physical signal, because digitally, it is one.

slide 5: Why Aliasing Is Invisible — The Silent Corruption Problem
(Image: A side-by-side comparison. Left: a real physical vibration signal — a clean sinusoid at 380 Hz, shown in the frequency domain as a single spectral peak. Right: the same signal as captured by a DAQ system sampling at 500 Hz — the 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 - 380 = 120 Hz, appearing as a spectral peak at 120 Hz. Both frequency-domain plots are labeled with axes (frequency in Hz, magnitude in arbitrary units). The caption at the bottom reads: "The 120 Hz alias peak looks real. There is no visual marker in the data that indicates corruption." An annotation on the right plot reads: "You would report this as a 120 Hz structural mode — and you would be wrong.")
Talking Point: Here is the scenario I want you to sit with. You are running a structural vibration test on an aerospace component. You expect significant frequency content up to about 300 hertz. You set your sample rate at 500 hertz, which gives you a Nyquist frequency of 250 hertz. You are reasonably satisfied. What you do not know is that there is a structural mode at 380 hertz — well above your Nyquist frequency. That 380 hertz signal aliases into your measurement at 500 minus 380, which is 120 hertz. You now have a perfectly clean, well-resolved spectral peak at 120 hertz that you did not expect. You write it up as a structural resonance you found, present it to your team, and potentially design a control system to avoid exciting it. None of that is real. There is no 120 hertz mode. The mode at 380 hertz is real, and it is invisible to your measurement. This is why aliasing is a silent corruption problem — it does not announce itself. The only protection is choosing a sufficient sample rate before you collect data.

slide 6: Anti-Aliasing Filters — The Structural Solution
(Image: A block diagram of the signal processing chain between a physical sensor and a digital data record. The chain has five stages from left to right: "Physical sensor" → "Amplifier/Conditioning" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." The anti-aliasing filter stage is highlighted with a gold border. An annotation shows the filter's cutoff frequency labeled "fc < fs/2" (cutoff frequency must be below the Nyquist frequency). A second annotation on the ADC stage reads: "Once digitized, aliased content cannot be removed — information is permanently lost." A dashed vertical line between the anti-aliasing filter and the ADC is labeled "This line is the point of no return — aliasing must be prevented before digitization.")
Talking Point: The structural solution to aliasing is the anti-aliasing filter: a low-pass filter placed in the signal conditioning chain before the analog-to-digital converter, designed to attenuate frequency content above the Nyquist frequency before it can be aliased into the digital record. The critical principle here is timing. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain — before the ADC — because once the signal has been digitized, the alias is already mixed into the data. A digital filter applied after digitization cannot un-alias a signal, because the aliased content and the real content at the same frequency are indistinguishable in the digital record. The information that was lost when the signal was undersampled is permanently gone. This is why the design of the anti-aliasing filter must be decided before you set up your DAQ configuration — it is not a post-processing step, it is a pre-measurement design decision.

slide 7: Choosing the Sample Rate — The Engineering Decision
(Image: A horizontal decision diagram with three components. Component 1: "Characterize signal bandwidth" — shown as a frequency spectrum with the highest expected frequency labeled fmax. Component 2: "Apply Nyquist margin" — shown as the formula "fs ≥ 5 × fmax (practical engineering margin)" with a note "Theoretical minimum: fs > 2 × fmax." A footnote beneath Component 2 reads: "The 5–10× rule accounts for the anti-aliasing filter's transition band — it is not a universal constant. Lower margins may be acceptable with a steeper filter; wider margins may be needed when signal bandwidth is uncertain." Component 3: "Verify against DAQ hardware limits" — shown as a hardware specification block with maximum sample rate and resolution. The three components are connected by arrows. Below the diagram: "Sample rate selection is a modeling commitment — you are claiming no energy above fs/2. Document your reasoning." The diagram uses a clean flowchart style with the course color scheme.)
Talking Point: In practice, how do you choose a sample rate? Start by characterizing the signal bandwidth — identify the highest frequency that contains information relevant to your measurement. For structural modes, this might come from a preliminary analysis of the natural frequencies using the geometry and material properties. For aerodynamic measurements, it might come from the expected turbulent fluctuation frequencies at your operating conditions. Once you have an estimate of the maximum frequency of interest, apply a practical margin: use a sample rate at least five to ten times that frequency, not just two times. The factor-of-two Nyquist minimum leaves almost no margin for filter roll-off, hardware imperfections, or unexpected high-frequency content. Then verify that your chosen sample rate is achievable with your DAQ hardware given the number of channels and the resolution you need. Document this decision in your report — not just what sample rate you used, but why you chose it based on the expected signal bandwidth.

slide 8: Identifying Signal Bandwidth from Real Data — MATLAB in the Loop
(Image: Two MATLAB figure panels. Left panel: a time-domain signal from a structural vibration measurement — a decaying oscillation with visible high-frequency content superimposed. The x-axis is labeled "Time (s)," y-axis "Acceleration (m/s²)." Right panel: the frequency-domain FFT of the same signal, with the x-axis labeled "Frequency (Hz)" and y-axis "Magnitude (m/s²)." Two spectral peaks are labeled: "Primary mode: 47 Hz" and "Secondary mode: 312 Hz." A vertical dashed red line is labeled "Nyquist frequency for this measurement: 500 Hz (sample rate = 1000 Hz)." The bandwidth bracket extends from 0 to 312 Hz, with the note: "312 Hz << 500 Hz — adequate margin." The figure style matches MATLAB default with course-consistent axis labels.)
Talking Point: In the Week 7 lab session, you are going to do exactly this — use MATLAB to visualize the signal from your Tier 2 canned experiment in both the time domain and the frequency domain. The time-domain plot tells you a lot about the character of the signal: is it a steady oscillation, a decaying transient, a step response? But the frequency-domain FFT is what tells you about bandwidth. You are looking for the highest frequency at which there is meaningful spectral energy — not noise floor, but actual signal content. That frequency is the lower bound on your Nyquist frequency, and half of it is the minimum cutoff frequency for your anti-aliasing filter. In this example, the primary structural mode is at 47 hertz and the secondary mode is at 312 hertz. If the sample rate were 600 hertz instead of 1000, the secondary mode at 312 hertz would be right against the Nyquist limit with almost no margin — any filter imperfection would potentially alias secondary mode content into the measurement. The 1000 hertz sample rate gives you comfortable margin. Seeing this in your own data, for your own experiment, is what makes the Nyquist criterion real rather than abstract.

slide 9: What Aliasing Looks Like in Aerospace Data — A Worked Example
(Image: A simulated MATLAB FFT plot showing two scenarios side by side. Left: "Adequately sampled (fs = 2000 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz, 420 Hz, and 780 Hz, all clearly resolved within the 0–1000 Hz bandwidth. Right: "Undersampled (fs = 800 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz and 420 Hz, but the 780 Hz component has aliased to 800 - 780 = 20 Hz, appearing as a prominent low-frequency peak. The 20 Hz alias peak is labeled in red: "780 Hz signal aliased to 20 Hz — reported as a structural mode if not recognized." Vertical dashed lines show the Nyquist frequency for each case: 1000 Hz on the left and 400 Hz on the right. A banner across the top reads: "Same physical signal — two entirely different frequency-domain representations based solely on sample rate choice.")
Talking Point: Let me make aliasing numerically concrete. Here is a physical signal with three real components at 80, 420, and 780 hertz. On the left, sampled at 2000 hertz — all three components are cleanly resolved in the FFT. On the right, the same physical signal sampled at 800 hertz. The Nyquist frequency is now 400 hertz. The 80-hertz and 420-hertz components are there — but the 420-hertz component is already very close to the Nyquist limit and may be attenuated by the anti-aliasing filter if it is too aggressive. And the 780-hertz component aliases to 800 minus 780, which is 20 hertz. That 20-hertz peak is prominent in the undersampled FFT. If you did not know the truth, you would report it as a 20-hertz structural mode. You might even try to correlate it to some physical mechanism at that frequency. This is not a rare failure mode — it is exactly the kind of error that gets made when sample rates are chosen without first characterizing the signal bandwidth.

slide 10: Connecting to the Tier 2 Lab — What You Are Evaluating
(Image: A structured checklist layout with four evaluation criteria. Each criterion is in its own row with an evaluation question on the left and a corresponding action on the right. Row 1: "Does the sample rate satisfy Nyquist with adequate margin?" → "Check: fs ≥ 5 × fmax based on FFT analysis." Row 2: "Is the anti-aliasing filter cutoff appropriate?" → "Check: fc < fs/2 and fc does not attenuate signal content of interest." Row 3: "Are there unexpected peaks in the FFT?" → "Investigate: could any be aliased content from above the Nyquist frequency?" Row 4: "Would a different sample rate or filter choice change the results?" → "Propose: document in the DAQ configuration section and in the improvement proposal." The checklist is designed to look like a structured analysis protocol — students can use it directly in the W7 lab session and in the Tier 2 group report Section 6.)
Talking Point: This is what you will do with the tools from this lecture in the Week 7 lab session. You are going to examine the DAQ configuration for your Tier 2 canned experiment — the actual sample rate and filtering choices that were made — and evaluate whether those choices are defensible given the signal content you measure. First: compute the Nyquist frequency for the chosen sample rate and verify that it provides adequate margin above the signal bandwidth you measure in the FFT. Second: locate the anti-aliasing filter specification in the DAQ documentation and verify that the cutoff is below the Nyquist frequency without attenuating signal content of interest. Third: look for unexpected peaks in the FFT — anything that might be aliased content from above the Nyquist frequency. And fourth: identify at least one DAQ configuration improvement that would increase measurement quality, and document it. This analysis feeds directly into the DAQ configuration justification section of your group report and into your improvement proposal — and it is grounded in the theory we covered today.

slide 11: Closure
The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem is not an abstract mathematical curiosity — it is the fundamental constraint on every digital measurement you will make in an aerospace career. Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon, not the sensor, and choosing a sample rate without characterizing the bandwidth is making an untested claim about what the signal contains. Aliasing does not look like noise — it looks like a real signal at a plausible frequency, and the only protection against it is adequate sample rate and proper anti-aliasing filtering, both chosen before data collection begins. In the Week 7 lab, you will see this in real data from your Tier 2 experiment — the FFT analysis is not just a visualization exercise, it is the diagnostic tool for evaluating whether the sampling choices in your experiment are defensible. In Lecture 2 this week, we go one level deeper: the signal conditioning chain between the sensor and the DAQ, and how filtering, gain, and noise source management complete the picture of what it means to capture a signal faithfully.

## Agent Notes
- Content is grounded in the W7 L1 blueprint purpose statement: Nyquist theorem, aliasing mechanism, signal bandwidth as a property of the phenomenon, practical consequences.
- The stroboscopic wheel analogy (slide 4) is drawn from the W7 R1 reading description — using it in the lecture creates conceptual continuity with the companion reading.
- Anti-aliasing filter is introduced at the end of this lecture (slide 6) at awareness level; full signal conditioning chain is the subject of W7 L2.
- Slide 10 connects directly to the W7 lab activity and to the Tier 2 report DAQ configuration section.
- MATLAB FFT workflow (slide 8) previews the lab activity without duplicating it — students see the tool, then apply it.
- No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 4 primary treatment per blueprint.

lectures.title: Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain
Talking Point: In the last lecture, we established that choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment — you are claiming that the physical signal has no meaningful content above the Nyquist frequency. But the sample rate is only part of the story. Between the physical sensor and the DAQ digitizer, the signal passes through a conditioning chain: it gets amplified, filtered, and modified in ways that can improve the measurement — or corrupt it — depending on the choices made. This lecture covers that chain: what each stage does, why it is there, and the most important design decisions you need to make and document before any data is collected.

slide 2: The Signal Conditioning Chain — An Overview
(Image: A horizontal block diagram of the complete signal conditioning chain from physical phenomenon to digital data record. Five blocks connected by arrows, left to right: "Physical Sensor / Transducer" → "Signal Amplifier (Gain + Offset)" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." Each block has a small icon below it: the sensor has a waveform, the amplifier has a gain triangle symbol, the filter has a frequency roll-off curve, the ADC has a staircase digitization symbol, and the data record has a MATLAB plot icon. A bracket below the first three blocks is labeled "Analog domain — these stages happen before digitization." A bracket below the last two blocks is labeled "Digital domain — once here, analog information is locked in." The color of the analog domain bracket is gold; the digital domain bracket is a lighter gray.)
Talking Point: Let me give you the full picture of what happens between a physical event and a number in a MATLAB variable. The sensor transduces the physical quantity into an electrical signal — typically a voltage or current proportional to what is being measured. That signal then passes through an amplifier, which increases the signal level to a range that the ADC can resolve effectively. It then passes through the anti-aliasing filter, which removes frequency content above the Nyquist frequency. Then the ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at the chosen rate and converts each sample to a digital number. Every design decision in the analog domain — how much gain, what filter cutoff, what filter order — affects the quality of the digital record. And critically: none of these effects can be undone after digitization. If you amplified the wrong band, you cannot de-amplify it. If you filtered with the wrong cutoff, you cannot unfilter. The measurement is fixed the moment it crosses into the digital domain.

slide 3: Gain and Offset — Why Amplification Is a Design Decision
(Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: "Under-amplified signal" — a small-amplitude sensor output waveform (about 10% of the ADC input range) plotted on a voltage axis labeled 0 to 10 V. The waveform only occupies 0 to 1 V of the range. A label reads: "Signal uses only 10% of ADC range — poor resolution, high quantization error contribution." Right panel: "Properly amplified signal" — the same waveform amplified to occupy 60–80% of the ADC input range. A label reads: "Signal occupies 70% of range — resolution optimized." Below both panels, a caption: "Gain selection directly controls how much of the ADC's resolution is used to represent the signal. An under-amplified signal wastes bits." A small inset shows the ADC quantization staircase to reinforce the concept.)
Talking Point: The purpose of the amplifier stage is to match the sensor's output range to the ADC's input range — to use as much of the ADC's dynamic range as possible without clipping. An ADC converts an analog voltage to a digital integer within its input range. If your sensor output spans 0.1 volts but your ADC input range is 0 to 10 volts, you are using only one percent of the available digital counts to represent the entire signal. Every bit that you are not using is measurement precision that you are throwing away. Proper gain selection puts the signal in the operating region where the ADC provides meaningful resolution. This is directly related to quantization error — the uncertainty introduced by the discrete nature of the ADC output. Quantization error is typically estimated as plus-or-minus half of one least-significant-bit, and its contribution to the total measurement uncertainty is smaller when the signal occupies more of the ADC range.

slide 4: Noise Sources in the Signal Chain — What You Are Fighting
(Image: A layered diagram of the signal conditioning chain with noise sources identified at each stage. The sensor block has an arrow labeled "Sensor noise: thermal noise (Johnson noise), sensitivity drift." The signal cable between sensor and amplifier has a lightning bolt icon labeled "Electromagnetic interference (EMI): coupling from nearby power lines, motors, RF sources." The amplifier block has an arrow labeled "Amplifier noise: thermal noise, bias current." A ground symbol between components is labeled "Ground loops: current flowing through shared ground path produces measurable voltage offset." Each noise source has an estimated contribution in parentheses: "sensor thermal noise: typically μV level," "EMI: can be mV level," "amplifier noise: typically nV to μV," "ground loops: can be mV to hundreds of mV." The visual is a chain diagram with noise injected at each stage — cumulative noise accumulates through the chain.)
Talking Point: Noise enters the signal chain at every stage, and understanding where it comes from is the first step toward controlling it. The sensor itself introduces thermal noise — a fundamental quantum-mechanical effect that is proportional to temperature and resistance. The cable between the sensor and the amplifier can pick up electromagnetic interference from nearby power lines, motors, or radio-frequency sources. The amplifier adds its own noise, and if the circuit does not have a clean, single-point ground reference, current flowing through the shared ground path can add a DC offset or a 60-hertz hum to the signal. Ground loops are one of the most common causes of unexplained noise in experimental measurements, and they are almost entirely preventable with careful grounding practice. A ground loop typically shows up as a 60 Hz peak in the FFT — a regular spike at the power line frequency that doesn't move regardless of how the physical system is loaded or excited. The takeaway is that noise is not a fixed property of your measurement system — it depends on how the system is assembled and set up, and improving signal quality sometimes requires changing the setup, not the hardware.

slide 5: The Anti-Aliasing Filter — Design Rationale and Cutoff Selection
(Image: A frequency-domain plot showing the amplitude response of a low-pass filter on the y-axis (in dB, from 0 to −60 dB) and frequency on the x-axis (from 0 to twice the sampling frequency). The filter has a flat passband extending from 0 to the cutoff frequency fc, a transition band from fc to the Nyquist frequency fs/2, and a stopband attenuating above fs/2. Three vertical dashed lines mark: fc (filter cutoff), fmax (highest frequency of signal interest), and fs/2 (Nyquist frequency). The relationships between these three frequencies are labeled: "fc must be above fmax — do not attenuate signal content," "fc must be below fs/2 — all content above Nyquist must be attenuated before ADC," "Transition band determines filter order — steeper rolloff requires higher-order filter." A note reads: "There is always a tradeoff: narrower transition band = higher filter order = more phase distortion.")
Talking Point: The anti-aliasing filter's job is precise: it must attenuate all frequency content above the Nyquist frequency sufficiently that no alias can contaminate the measurement, while passing all signal content of interest without attenuation. The cutoff frequency sits in the transition zone between "signal content I want" and "content that would alias." This means the cutoff must be above the highest frequency of interest — otherwise you are filtering out signal — and it must be below the Nyquist frequency — otherwise you are not completing the anti-aliasing job. The width of the transition band is controlled by the filter order: a higher-order filter has a steeper rolloff, which gives you more separation between the passband and the stopband for a given sampling rate. But there is a tradeoff: higher-order filters introduce more phase distortion in the passband, which can affect the timing relationships in the signal. This is not a deep filter design course — but you need to be able to read a filter specification and understand whether the cutoff is in the right place relative to your signal bandwidth and sample rate.

slide 6: Analog vs. Digital Filtering — The Critical Distinction
(Image: A two-column comparison table. Column header left: "Analog filtering (pre-ADC)." Column header right: "Digital filtering (post-ADC)." Row 1: "When it happens" | "Before digitization — in the signal conditioning chain" | "After digitization — in MATLAB or DSP hardware." Row 2 [BOLD/HIGHLIGHTED]: "Can prevent aliasing?" | "YES — that is its primary purpose" | "NO — aliased content is already mixed into the digital data; it cannot be separated." Row 3: "Introduces phase delay?" | "Yes, order-dependent" | "Yes, filter-type-dependent; can be zero-phase with filtfilt in MATLAB." Row 4: "Can introduce artifacts?" | "Rarely, if properly designed" | "Yes — ringing near sharp transitions, edge effects, passband ripple." Row 5: "Primary use" | "Anti-aliasing before ADC; noise rejection" | "Post-processing: smoothing, band selection, noise removal from already-captured signal." A bold annotation at the bottom reads: "Anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain. This is not a preference — it is a physical requirement." The 'Can prevent aliasing?' row is visually emphasized — bold text or highlighted background — to distinguish it from the other rows.)
Talking Point: You do not need to design filters — you need to know which domain handles which problem. The single most important row in this table is the one about aliasing prevention. I want to be direct about the most important practical implication of this table. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain, before the analog-to-digital converter, because once a signal has been aliased into the digital record, no digital filter can separate it from the legitimate signal content at the same frequency. Digital filters — including MATLAB's filtfilt and butter functions — are powerful post-processing tools, and they have real uses: smoothing noisy data, extracting a specific frequency band from a captured record, or removing a known noise frequency. But they are applied to data that has already been digitized, which means they cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Additionally, digital filters introduce their own artifacts: zero-phase filters like filtfilt perform the filtering twice in opposite directions, which removes phase delay but doubles the transient effects at the edges of the data record. Finite impulse response filters can introduce ringing near sharp transitions. You do not need to design filters from scratch in this course. You do need to know that the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision, and it must be documented in any credible experimental report.

slide 7: Documentation Requirements — Why Signal Conditioning Is a Required Report Section
(Image: A structured documentation template showing required entries for a DAQ configuration justification section. Six rows: Row 1 "Sample rate selected" — value and Nyquist frequency derived. Row 2 "Signal bandwidth estimate" — how estimated (from model, from literature, from preliminary measurement). Row 3 "Anti-aliasing filter" — type, cutoff frequency, order, vendor and model if applicable. Row 4 "Gain setting" — value and basis (ADC input range vs. expected sensor output range). Row 5 "Noise sources identified" — at least two named, estimated magnitude, mitigation applied. Row 6 "Known limitations" — any configuration choice that could affect data quality, with rationale for why it was accepted. The template is formatted as a simple table with a header "DAQ Configuration Justification — [Experiment Name]" and a date field. The visual communicates that this is a formal required document, not a checkbox.)
Talking Point: Every one of the choices we discussed today — sample rate, gain, filter cutoff, filter order, grounding configuration — is a required documentation item in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report. Not because documentation is bureaucratic, but because these choices affect whether your data is trustworthy, and a reader who wants to evaluate your validation claim needs to know that you made these choices deliberately and can explain them. The DAQ configuration section is not a settings list — it is an argument. You are arguing that the choices you made are appropriate for the signal you are trying to capture, given the bandwidth of the physical phenomenon, the Nyquist criterion, the range of the sensor output, and the noise environment of the measurement. If you cannot justify each choice, you cannot defend your data, and you cannot make a credible validation claim. This documentation habit is exactly what distinguishes a well-run experimental campaign from a black-box measurement.

slide 8: Worked Example — Evaluating a Sensor's Signal Conditioning Setup
(Image: A one-page instrument specification excerpt mockup showing four key parameters: "Sensor output range: 0 to 50 mV (full scale)," "Noise floor: 5 μV RMS," "Recommended gain: 100 (amplified output: 0 to 5 V)," "Anti-aliasing filter: 2nd-order Butterworth, −3 dB at 1 kHz (−3 dB means the filter has reduced the signal amplitude to approximately 71% of its original value at this frequency)." Below the spec excerpt, a MATLAB figure shows the frequency-domain FFT of the amplified sensor output, with a spectral peak at 240 Hz labeled "Primary signal component" and a flat noise floor. A vertical dashed line at 1000 Hz is labeled "Filter cutoff," and a vertical dashed line at 500 Hz (half the cutoff, assuming a 1000 Hz sample rate) is labeled "Nyquist frequency." An annotation reads: "Signal bandwidth (240 Hz) << Nyquist (500 Hz) — adequate margin. But: filter cutoff (1000 Hz) is above Nyquist (500 Hz) — the filter is NOT providing anti-aliasing protection at this sample rate." The problem is highlighted in red.)
Talking Point: Let me walk through a real evaluation using actual instrument specifications. The sensor has a full-scale output range of 0 to 50 millivolts, a noise floor of 5 microvolts RMS, a recommended gain of 100 — which brings the output to 0 to 5 volts, matching a standard ADC input range — and a built-in second-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter with a negative-3-dB cutoff at 1 kilohertz. If you are sampling at 1000 hertz, the Nyquist frequency is 500 hertz. The filter cutoff is at 1 kilohertz — which is above the Nyquist frequency. That means the anti-aliasing filter is not actually providing aliasing protection at this sample rate. Any content between 500 and 1000 hertz would alias into the measurement, and the filter is not attenuating it. The fix is straightforward: either increase the sample rate to at least 2 kilohertz so the Nyquist frequency is above the filter cutoff, or add a supplementary analog filter with a lower cutoff. This is exactly the kind of configuration evaluation you will do in the Week 7 lab — reading the instrument documentation, checking whether the configuration is self-consistent, and identifying where it is not.

slide 9: Connecting to the Improvement Proposal — What You Are Looking For
(Image: A two-column illustration. Left column header: "What to look for in the existing configuration." Three items listed: "Is the sample rate high enough given the signal bandwidth?" | "Is the anti-aliasing filter cutoff appropriate?" | "Are there identifiable noise sources that could be reduced?" Right column header: "How to frame the improvement proposal." Three items listed: "Propose a specific sample rate with Nyquist margin calculation." | "Propose a specific filter cutoff and order with justification." | "Identify specific noise mitigation strategies (shielded cable, differential measurement, single-point ground)." A connecting arrow between the two columns is labeled "The improvement proposal is grounded in this analysis — not a generic 'use better equipment' suggestion." At the bottom: "Improvement proposal category (b): DAQ/signal conditioning — this is the CLO 4 graded deliverable in the Tier 2 report.")
Talking Point: Section 6 of your Tier 2 group report asks you to propose improvements to the experiment, and category (b) is specifically DAQ and signal conditioning. This is the CLO 4 graded deliverable of E2, and the standard is higher than "use a better sensor." A credible improvement proposal is grounded in your data and your analysis. If you found from the FFT that the signal has significant energy at frequencies above the current Nyquist frequency, you propose a specific increased sample rate with the Nyquist margin calculation shown. If you found that the anti-aliasing filter cutoff is poorly positioned relative to the sample rate, you propose a specific alternative cutoff with the filter order and the rationale. If you identified a ground loop in the noise floor, you propose differential measurement or cable shielding with an explanation of why the current configuration produces the noise. The improvement proposal is where the theory you learned this week gets applied to the specific experiment you ran. Here is what a fully quantified improvement argument looks like: the FFT reveals a structural mode at 380 Hz. Current sample rate is 500 Hz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 250 Hz — below the 380 Hz mode. The 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 − 380 = 120 Hz and appears as a false spectral peak. Proposed improvement: increase sample rate to 2 kHz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 1 kHz — adequate margin above a potential 780 Hz secondary mode. That is the level of quantification the rubric awards for improvement proposal excellence.

slide 10: The Signal Conditioning Choices Are Modeling Decisions
(Image: A diagram showing the concept of signal conditioning as a modeling commitment. The center of the diagram has a bold statement: "Every signal conditioning choice encodes an assumption about the signal." Three examples branch from the center: "Sample rate choice" → "Assumes: no meaningful signal energy above fs/2." "Filter cutoff choice" → "Assumes: no signal content between fc and fs/2 that matters for the intended use." "Gain choice" → "Assumes: maximum signal amplitude will not exceed ADC input range." Each assumption has a consequence box attached: "Violated sample rate assumption → aliasing." "Violated filter cutoff assumption → attenuated signal content OR aliased content not removed." "Violated gain assumption → clipping — signal is permanently distorted." The visual style uses a spider diagram or concept map layout, with the central statement in a large gold circle and the branches radiating outward.)
Talking Point: I want to close this lecture with the unifying frame. Every signal conditioning choice you make is a modeling decision — it encodes an assumption about the signal you are trying to capture. Choosing a sample rate of 2 kilohertz means you are claiming the signal has no meaningful energy above 1 kilohertz. Choosing an anti-aliasing filter cutoff of 800 hertz means you are claiming that any signal content between 800 hertz and 1 kilohertz can be discarded without affecting your measurement quality for the intended use. Choosing a gain of 100 means you are claiming the maximum signal amplitude will not exceed 5 volts after amplification. Each of these assumptions can be wrong, and when they are, the consequences range from reduced precision to completely corrupted data — and the corrupted data will not always be obviously wrong. The only protection is making these decisions based on analysis of the expected signal, documenting the reasoning, and being prepared to defend each choice when your measurement is scrutinized. That is what makes a measurement credible, and that is what the DAQ configuration section of your report is for.

slide 11: Closure
Signal conditioning is not a black box that happens before your MATLAB workspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the physical signal you are trying to capture. Amplification matches sensor output to ADC dynamic range and controls quantization error contribution. The anti-aliasing filter must be placed in the analog domain before the ADC, with a cutoff below the Nyquist frequency, because aliased content cannot be removed after digitization. Noise enters the chain at every stage — from the sensor, from the cable, from the amplifier, from the ground configuration — and identifying and documenting known noise sources is a required part of your experimental report. Analog and digital filtering serve different purposes and must not be confused: digital filters cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Every one of these choices belongs in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report, argued from the signal bandwidth and the intended use of the measurement. The Week 7 lab will give you the data and the documentation to perform that analysis for your own experiment.

## Agent Notes
- Content is grounded in the W7 L2 blueprint purpose statement: signal conditioning chain, amplification, anti-aliasing filter design rationale, noise sources, analog vs. digital filtering contrast.
- The analog vs. digital filtering contrast (slide 6) is specifically required by the blueprint: "an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles."
- Slide 10 frames all signal conditioning choices as modeling commitments — connecting this lecture to the AMVF spine and the E2 prediction discipline.
- The worked example in slide 8 uses the sensor spec evaluation format that students will apply in the W7 lab.
- Slide 9 connects directly to the Tier 2 improvement proposal category (b), per blueprint note about the lab feeding into the group report.
- No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 4 primary treatment.

lectures.summary: Covers the signal conditioning chain between a physical sensor and a DAQ digitizer: amplification (gain and offset), filtering (low-pass anti-aliasing filter — purpose, order, cutoff selection), and noise sources (thermal noise, electromagnetic interference, ground loops). The design rationale for anti-aliasing filter cutoff selection is emphasized: the cutoff must be below the Nyquist frequency of the chosen sampling rate, but not so aggressive that it attenuates signal content of interest. Students see that signal conditioning choices are not technical defaults — they encode assumptions about signal structure and acceptable data quality. **Analog vs. digital filtering contrast (5–7 min segment):** This lecture should include an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles. The key message: anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain before the ADC — digital filters applied post-digitization cannot retroactively correct aliasing because the information is already lost. Additionally, digital filters introduce phase delay, passband ripple, and potential edge-case artifacts (e.g., ringing near sharp transitions) that are non-obvious. Students do not need to know how to design analog or digital filters — this is awareness-level content. The takeaway: signal conditioning choices must be made before sampling, not after, and the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision that must be documented in any credible experimental report.

learning_goals: Apply the Nyquist sampling theorem to identify the minimum sampling rate required for a given signal bandwidth, and predict the consequences of aliasing when the theorem is violated — CLO 4
Explain the purpose and design rationale of an anti-aliasing filter, and identify appropriate filter cutoff frequency relative to signal bandwidth and sampling rate — CLO 4
Identify and describe at least three common DAQ configuration choices — channel configuration, resolution, sample rate — and articulate the tradeoff each choice imposes — CLO 4
Visualize a measured signal in both time and frequency domain using MATLAB and identify signal bandwidth from the frequency-domain representation — CLO 4
lectures.body_markdown:
slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain
Talking Point: In the last lecture, we established that choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment — you are claiming that the physical signal has no meaningful content above the Nyquist frequency. But the sample rate is only part of the story. Between the physical sensor and the DAQ digitizer, the signal passes through a conditioning chain: it gets amplified, filtered, and modified in ways that can improve the measurement — or corrupt it — depending on the choices made. This lecture covers that chain: what each stage does, why it is there, and the most important design decisions you need to make and document before any data is collected.

slide 2: The Signal Conditioning Chain — An Overview
(Image: A horizontal block diagram of the complete signal conditioning chain from physical phenomenon to digital data record. Five blocks connected by arrows, left to right: "Physical Sensor / Transducer" → "Signal Amplifier (Gain + Offset)" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." Each block has a small icon below it: the sensor has a waveform, the amplifier has a gain triangle symbol, the filter has a frequency roll-off curve, the ADC has a staircase digitization symbol, and the data record has a MATLAB plot icon. A bracket below the first three blocks is labeled "Analog domain — these stages happen before digitization." A bracket below the last two blocks is labeled "Digital domain — once here, analog information is locked in." The color of the analog domain bracket is gold; the digital domain bracket is a lighter gray.)
Talking Point: Let me give you the full picture of what happens between a physical event and a number in a MATLAB variable. The sensor transduces the physical quantity into an electrical signal — typically a voltage or current proportional to what is being measured. That signal then passes through an amplifier, which increases the signal level to a range that the ADC can resolve effectively. It then passes through the anti-aliasing filter, which removes frequency content above the Nyquist frequency. Then the ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at the chosen rate and converts each sample to a digital number. Every design decision in the analog domain — how much gain, what filter cutoff, what filter order — affects the quality of the digital record. And critically: none of these effects can be undone after digitization. If you amplified the wrong band, you cannot de-amplify it. If you filtered with the wrong cutoff, you cannot unfilter. The measurement is fixed the moment it crosses into the digital domain.

slide 3: Gain and Offset — Why Amplification Is a Design Decision
(Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: "Under-amplified signal" — a small-amplitude sensor output waveform (about 10% of the ADC input range) plotted on a voltage axis labeled 0 to 10 V. The waveform only occupies 0 to 1 V of the range. A label reads: "Signal uses only 10% of ADC range — poor resolution, high quantization error contribution." Right panel: "Properly amplified signal" — the same waveform amplified to occupy 60–80% of the ADC input range. A label reads: "Signal occupies 70% of range — resolution optimized." Below both panels, a caption: "Gain selection directly controls how much of the ADC's resolution is used to represent the signal. An under-amplified signal wastes bits." A small inset shows the ADC quantization staircase to reinforce the concept.)
Talking Point: The purpose of the amplifier stage is to match the sensor's output range to the ADC's input range — to use as much of the ADC's dynamic range as possible without clipping. An ADC converts an analog voltage to a digital integer within its input range. If your sensor output spans 0.1 volts but your ADC input range is 0 to 10 volts, you are using only one percent of the available digital counts to represent the entire signal. Every bit that you are not using is measurement precision that you are throwing away. Proper gain selection puts the signal in the operating region where the ADC provides meaningful resolution. This is directly related to quantization error — the uncertainty introduced by the discrete nature of the ADC output. Quantization error is typically estimated as plus-or-minus half of one least-significant-bit, and its contribution to the total measurement uncertainty is smaller when the signal occupies more of the ADC range.

slide 4: Noise Sources in the Signal Chain — What You Are Fighting
(Image: A layered diagram of the signal conditioning chain with noise sources identified at each stage. The sensor block has an arrow labeled "Sensor noise: thermal noise (Johnson noise), sensitivity drift." The signal cable between sensor and amplifier has a lightning bolt icon labeled "Electromagnetic interference (EMI): coupling from nearby power lines, motors, RF sources." The amplifier block has an arrow labeled "Amplifier noise: thermal noise, bias current." A ground symbol between components is labeled "Ground loops: current flowing through shared ground path produces measurable voltage offset." Each noise source has an estimated contribution in parentheses: "sensor thermal noise: typically μV level," "EMI: can be mV level," "amplifier noise: typically nV to μV," "ground loops: can be mV to hundreds of mV." The visual is a chain diagram with noise injected at each stage — cumulative noise accumulates through the chain.)
Talking Point: Noise enters the signal chain at every stage, and understanding where it comes from is the first step toward controlling it. The sensor itself introduces thermal noise — a fundamental quantum-mechanical effect that is proportional to temperature and resistance. The cable between the sensor and the amplifier can pick up electromagnetic interference from nearby power lines, motors, or radio-frequency sources. The amplifier adds its own noise, and if the circuit does not have a clean, single-point ground reference, current flowing through the shared ground path can add a DC offset or a 60-hertz hum to the signal. Ground loops are one of the most common causes of unexplained noise in experimental measurements, and they are almost entirely preventable with careful grounding practice. A ground loop typically shows up as a 60 Hz peak in the FFT — a regular spike at the power line frequency that doesn't move regardless of how the physical system is loaded or excited. The takeaway is that noise is not a fixed property of your measurement system — it depends on how the system is assembled and set up, and improving signal quality sometimes requires changing the setup, not the hardware.

slide 5: The Anti-Aliasing Filter — Design Rationale and Cutoff Selection
(Image: A frequency-domain plot showing the amplitude response of a low-pass filter on the y-axis (in dB, from 0 to −60 dB) and frequency on the x-axis (from 0 to twice the sampling frequency). The filter has a flat passband extending from 0 to the cutoff frequency fc, a transition band from fc to the Nyquist frequency fs/2, and a stopband attenuating above fs/2. Three vertical dashed lines mark: fc (filter cutoff), fmax (highest frequency of signal interest), and fs/2 (Nyquist frequency). The relationships between these three frequencies are labeled: "fc must be above fmax — do not attenuate signal content," "fc must be below fs/2 — all content above Nyquist must be attenuated before ADC," "Transition band determines filter order — steeper rolloff requires higher-order filter." A note reads: "There is always a tradeoff: narrower transition band = higher filter order = more phase distortion.")
Talking Point: The anti-aliasing filter's job is precise: it must attenuate all frequency content above the Nyquist frequency sufficiently that no alias can contaminate the measurement, while passing all signal content of interest without attenuation. The cutoff frequency sits in the transition zone between "signal content I want" and "content that would alias." This means the cutoff must be above the highest frequency of interest — otherwise you are filtering out signal — and it must be below the Nyquist frequency — otherwise you are not completing the anti-aliasing job. The width of the transition band is controlled by the filter order: a higher-order filter has a steeper rolloff, which gives you more separation between the passband and the stopband for a given sampling rate. But there is a tradeoff: higher-order filters introduce more phase distortion in the passband, which can affect the timing relationships in the signal. This is not a deep filter design course — but you need to be able to read a filter specification and understand whether the cutoff is in the right place relative to your signal bandwidth and sample rate.

slide 6: Analog vs. Digital Filtering — The Critical Distinction
(Image: A two-column comparison table. Column header left: "Analog filtering (pre-ADC)." Column header right: "Digital filtering (post-ADC)." Row 1: "When it happens" | "Before digitization — in the signal conditioning chain" | "After digitization — in MATLAB or DSP hardware." Row 2 [BOLD/HIGHLIGHTED]: "Can prevent aliasing?" | "YES — that is its primary purpose" | "NO — aliased content is already mixed into the digital data; it cannot be separated." Row 3: "Introduces phase delay?" | "Yes, order-dependent" | "Yes, filter-type-dependent; can be zero-phase with filtfilt in MATLAB." Row 4: "Can introduce artifacts?" | "Rarely, if properly designed" | "Yes — ringing near sharp transitions, edge effects, passband ripple." Row 5: "Primary use" | "Anti-aliasing before ADC; noise rejection" | "Post-processing: smoothing, band selection, noise removal from already-captured signal." A bold annotation at the bottom reads: "Anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain. This is not a preference — it is a physical requirement." The 'Can prevent aliasing?' row is visually emphasized — bold text or highlighted background — to distinguish it from the other rows.)
Talking Point: You do not need to design filters — you need to know which domain handles which problem. The single most important row in this table is the one about aliasing prevention. I want to be direct about the most important practical implication of this table. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain, before the analog-to-digital converter, because once a signal has been aliased into the digital record, no digital filter can separate it from the legitimate signal content at the same frequency. Digital filters — including MATLAB's filtfilt and butter functions — are powerful post-processing tools, and they have real uses: smoothing noisy data, extracting a specific frequency band from a captured record, or removing a known noise frequency. But they are applied to data that has already been digitized, which means they cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Additionally, digital filters introduce their own artifacts: zero-phase filters like filtfilt perform the filtering twice in opposite directions, which removes phase delay but doubles the transient effects at the edges of the data record. Finite impulse response filters can introduce ringing near sharp transitions. You do not need to design filters from scratch in this course. You do need to know that the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision, and it must be documented in any credible experimental report.

slide 7: Documentation Requirements — Why Signal Conditioning Is a Required Report Section
(Image: A structured documentation template showing required entries for a DAQ configuration justification section. Six rows: Row 1 "Sample rate selected" — value and Nyquist frequency derived. Row 2 "Signal bandwidth estimate" — how estimated (from model, from literature, from preliminary measurement). Row 3 "Anti-aliasing filter" — type, cutoff frequency, order, vendor and model if applicable. Row 4 "Gain setting" — value and basis (ADC input range vs. expected sensor output range). Row 5 "Noise sources identified" — at least two named, estimated magnitude, mitigation applied. Row 6 "Known limitations" — any configuration choice that could affect data quality, with rationale for why it was accepted. The template is formatted as a simple table with a header "DAQ Configuration Justification — [Experiment Name]" and a date field. The visual communicates that this is a formal required document, not a checkbox.)
Talking Point: Every one of the choices we discussed today — sample rate, gain, filter cutoff, filter order, grounding configuration — is a required documentation item in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report. Not because documentation is bureaucratic, but because these choices affect whether your data is trustworthy, and a reader who wants to evaluate your validation claim needs to know that you made these choices deliberately and can explain them. The DAQ configuration section is not a settings list — it is an argument. You are arguing that the choices you made are appropriate for the signal you are trying to capture, given the bandwidth of the physical phenomenon, the Nyquist criterion, the range of the sensor output, and the noise environment of the measurement. If you cannot justify each choice, you cannot defend your data, and you cannot make a credible validation claim. This documentation habit is exactly what distinguishes a well-run experimental campaign from a black-box measurement.

slide 11: Closure
Signal conditioning is not a black box that happens before your MATLAB workspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the physical signal you are trying to capture. Amplification matches sensor output to ADC dynamic range and controls quantization error contribution. The anti-aliasing filter must be placed in the analog domain before the ADC, with a cutoff below the Nyquist frequency, because aliased content cannot be removed after digitization. Noise enters the chain at every stage — from the sensor, from the cable, from the amplifier, from the ground configuration — and identifying and documenting known noise sources is a required part of your experimental report. Analog and digital filtering serve different purposes and must not be confused: digital filters cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Every one of these choices belongs in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report, argued from the signal bandwidth and the intended use of the measurement. The Week 7 lab will give you the data and the documentation to perform that analysis for your own experiment.

## Agent Notes
- Content is grounded in the W7 L2 blueprint purpose statement: signal conditioning chain, amplification, anti-aliasing filter design rationale, noise sources, analog vs. digital filtering contrast.
- The analog vs. digital filtering contrast (slide 6) is specifically required by the blueprint: "an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles."
- Slide 10 frames all signal conditioning choices as modeling commitments — connecting this lecture to the AMVF spine and the E2 prediction discipline.
- The worked example in slide 8 uses the sensor spec evaluation format that students will apply in the W7 lab.
- Slide 9 connects directly to the Tier 2 improvement proposal category (b), per blueprint note about the lab feeding into the group report.
- No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 4 primary treatment.

readings.title: Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement
## Why the fastest thing you can't see can still destroy your data

Watch a helicopter's rotor blades in a film and you will sometimes see them appear to rotate backward — or hover perfectly still at full throttle. Nothing is wrong with the blades. Something is wrong with the camera.

A film camera captures still frames at a fixed rate — typically 24 per second. If the blades complete almost exactly one full rotation between each frame, they appear stationary in the footage. If they complete slightly more than a full rotation, they appear to spin slowly backward. The blades are moving at full speed. The camera, sampling the scene too slowly relative to the rotation rate, produces an artifact that looks like real motion. This optical illusion is not just a curiosity. It is a precise physical analogy for one of the most consequential pitfalls in digital data acquisition: **aliasing**.

---

Every time you ran data collection in E1, someone had already chosen the sample rate for you. In E2, that decision is yours to justify. Every digital measurement system — every DAQ board, every microcontroller analog input, every data logger — works the same way the film camera does. It takes samples of a continuous physical signal at discrete moments in time. Between samples, it knows nothing. The physical signal could be doing anything, and the measurement system will never know.

The theorem formalizes the helicopter illusion precisely. The same artifact appears in accelerometer data from a vibration test when sample rate is insufficient: the 380 Hz structural mode appears in your data file as a 120 Hz signal, and there is nothing in the data that tells you it is wrong. Here is the formal statement: the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem** establishes the fundamental limit: to faithfully capture a signal that contains frequency content up to some maximum frequency *f_max*, you must sample at a rate of at least *2f_max*. This threshold — twice the highest frequency in the signal — is called the **Nyquist rate**. Sample faster than this and you can reconstruct the original signal. Sample slower, and you cannot.

What happens when you sample slower than the Nyquist rate? The same thing that happened to the helicopter blades. High-frequency content in the signal does not disappear — it folds back into the measured spectrum and appears as a false low-frequency signal. A vibration at 500 Hz, sampled at 600 samples per second, will appear in your data as a 100 Hz oscillation. That 100 Hz component is not in the physical system. It is an artifact of the sampling process. And critically: it looks exactly like a real signal. There is no asterisk next to it, no error flag, no warning in the data file. It is a ghost that wears the costume of truth.

![A three-panel diagram illustrating aliasing. Panel 1 shows a smooth, high-frequency sinusoidal signal (the true physical signal, labeled "True signal: 500 Hz") plotted as a continuous curve. Vertical dashed lines mark the sampling instants — spaced too far apart to capture the full waveform. Panel 2 shows the same sampling instants with open circles on the true signal curve — the sampled values. Panel 3 shows those sampled values connected by a smooth curve, producing an apparent low-frequency sinusoid labeled "Aliased result: 100 Hz." All three panels are stacked vertically, aligned on the same time axis, showing that the same sampling instants produce both the true signal and the aliased artifact depending on how they are connected. Clean, black-and-white engineering diagram style with axis labels and frequency annotations.](../images/E2_W7_R1_image1.png)
*This image lets you watch a high-frequency signal turn into a believable but false low-frequency story, which is exactly why aliasing is so dangerous in practice.

---

Here is the part that trips up experienced engineers: **signal bandwidth** is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring, not a property of your sensor or your DAQ system. If you are measuring acoustic vibrations in a rocket nozzle, the signal bandwidth is determined by the dynamics of the combustion process — not by your microphone, not by your DAQ board, and not by whatever sampling rate you happened to inherit from a previous project. Before you choose a sampling rate, you must ask: what is the highest frequency my signal of interest actually contains?

This sounds simple. In practice it requires genuine knowledge of the physics. A structural vibration test on a beam might have fundamental modes below 100 Hz and essentially no relevant content above 1 kHz — a 2,500 samples-per-second rate would be conservative and appropriate. A combustion pressure transient in a rocket engine test might contain critical content up to 50 kHz — that same 2,500 samples-per-second rate would alias catastrophically, producing clean-looking data that is entirely fictitious.

When you commit to a sampling rate, you are making a claim. You are stating, before you collect any data, that your signal contains no frequency content above half your chosen sampling rate. If that claim is wrong — if the signal has energy at higher frequencies that you didn't anticipate — the data is silently corrupted. No error message. No obvious artifact. Just a dataset that reflects a physical reality that doesn't exist.

**Anti-aliasing filters** exist to enforce this claim in hardware. By attenuating signal content above the Nyquist frequency before the signal reaches the analog-to-digital converter, they prevent high-frequency content from folding into the measured spectrum. They are not optional safeguards — they are the mechanism that makes the Nyquist guarantee meaningful. Their design (specifically, where to set the cutoff frequency relative to the signal bandwidth and the sampling rate) is one of the topics the W7 lectures will address directly.

![A frequency-domain illustration of anti-aliasing. The horizontal axis is labeled "Frequency (Hz)" with three vertical reference lines: "Signal bandwidth (f_max)," "Filter cutoff (f_c)," and "Nyquist frequency (f_s / 2)." The signal spectrum is shown as a shaded region from 0 to f_max. The anti-aliasing filter response is shown as a curve that is flat (1.0) from 0 Hz to f_c, then rolls off sharply toward zero as frequency approaches f_s / 2. Beyond f_s / 2, a dashed region is labeled "Content here aliases." The diagram shows that the filter cutoff must be placed above the signal bandwidth (so signal content isn't lost) but below the Nyquist frequency (so aliased content can't enter). The zone between f_c and f_s / 2 is labeled "Transition band — filter must attenuate here." Black and gold color scheme, clean engineering diagram style.](../images/E2_W7_R1_image2.png)
*This image turns the sampling rule into a design picture so you can reason about filter placement and Nyquist protection before touching hardware.

---

This reading stops here, before filter design, before DAQ configuration choices, before MATLAB analysis. Those topics are covered in the companion reading (R2) and in the Week 7 lectures. The job of this reading is narrower: ensure that before you walk into class, you understand *why* these choices matter.

Sampling rate is not a technical setting you accept as a lab default. It is a modeling commitment. It encodes assumptions about the physical world that, if wrong, produce data that looks real and is wrong. When your Tier 2 canned experiment uses a specific sampling rate, you should be able to defend that choice on physical grounds — and identify what you would be missing if the rate were too low.

---

**The Takeaway:** Aliasing is not an obscure artifact of bad equipment — it is a fundamental consequence of digital sampling that is invisible in the data itself, and the only defense is understanding the physics of your signal well enough to make the Nyquist commitment confidently before you collect the first data point.
