readings.title: DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
# DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference
## Every configuration choice is a claim about your signal. Treat it that way.

A data acquisition system handed to you with factory defaults is not a neutral tool. It is a collection of someone else's assumptions about what you are measuring.

**Quick-Reference: DAQ Configuration Decisions**

| Choice | Key Question | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Channel configuration | Is the signal source floating or subject to ground loop noise? | Default differential; use single-ended only for large signals with shared ground and short cables |
| Input range | What is the expected signal amplitude? | Set to the smallest range that comfortably brackets the signal; do not accept ±10 V defaults for millivolt signals |
| Sample rate | What is the signal bandwidth, and what anti-aliasing margin is required? | Sample at 5× to 10× the signal bandwidth; document the Nyquist argument explicitly |
| Anti-aliasing filter cutoff | What is the transition band of the filter relative to the chosen sample rate? | Set cutoff at ≤ 0.4 × sample rate; document relationship to signal bandwidth |
| Triggering | Does the event onset need to be captured, or is the signal slowly varying? | Hardware trigger for transient events; software trigger acceptable for quasi-static signals |

Understanding those assumptions (channel configuration, resolution, sample rate, triggering) is not optional bookkeeping. Each choice encodes a constraint on what the system can faithfully represent, and each constraint contributes its own error source to your measurement. This reading is a reference document. Use it when setting up your Tier 2 experiment and again when drafting the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. Unlike the companion reading (R1), which builds physical intuition for aliasing and sampling, this reading is deliberately dense: it is meant to be consulted, not just read once.

---

## Channel Configuration: Single-Ended vs. Differential

Most DAQ systems offer two channel configuration modes, and the difference matters whenever noise or ground potential is a concern.

**Single-ended configuration** measures voltage between the signal line and a shared system ground. It uses twice as many channels for a given number of signals compared to differential, which makes it attractive when channel count is a constraint. The vulnerability: any noise voltage that appears between the sensor's ground reference and the DAQ system's ground reference is measured as signal. In environments with multiple power supplies, motors, or long cable runs, this ground-referencing error, called a **ground loop**, can swamp a low-level sensor signal.

**Differential configuration** measures voltage between two dedicated signal lines, rejecting any voltage that appears equally on both (called **common-mode rejection**). Differential inputs are the right choice whenever your signal source is floating, or whenever the signal amplitude is small enough that ground loop noise is a significant fraction of the signal magnitude. A **floating signal source** has no connection to the local measurement ground: battery-powered sensors and isolated transducers are common examples, and their reference point may drift relative to the DAQ ground. Most precision measurement applications — strain gauges, thermocouples, load cells — use differential configuration for this reason.

The practical decision: single-ended is acceptable when the signal is large (volts, not millivolts), cable runs are short, and all equipment shares a common ground. In any other case, default to differential.

---

## Resolution: Quantization Error and Its Contribution to Uncertainty

Every analog-to-digital converter (**ADC**) maps a continuous voltage range onto a finite number of discrete digital levels. A 12-bit ADC divides its input range into 2¹² = 4,096 discrete levels. A 16-bit ADC divides the same range into 65,536 levels. The voltage difference between adjacent levels is the **quantization step size** — also called the **least significant bit (LSB)** voltage.

For a ±10 V input range on a 12-bit ADC, the quantization step size is 20 V / 4,096 ≈ 4.9 mV. That is the smallest voltage change the system can detect. Any signal change smaller than one LSB is invisible — it either rounds up to the next level or rounds down. The resulting error is **quantization error**, and it contributes to the total measurement uncertainty.

For most well-designed experiments, quantization error is dominated by other error sources (sensor noise, thermal drift, amplifier noise). But there are important exceptions. If you are measuring a slowly varying, low-amplitude signal (structural creep, thermal expansion, very low-level acoustic vibration) and your input range is set far wider than the signal amplitude, the effective resolution shrinks dramatically. A signal that occupies only 1% of the ADC input range is being measured with roughly 100 times worse effective resolution than the ADC spec implies.

The practical decision: set the input range to the smallest value that comfortably brackets your expected signal. Do not accept ±10 V defaults when your signal is ±100 mV.

![A diagram illustrating quantization for two different ADC input range settings. On the left, a ±10V input range is divided into discrete levels, with a small signal occupying only the bottom fraction of the range — the signal is shown as a small-amplitude sine wave that spans only a few quantization levels. On the right, the input range is set to ±200 mV to match the signal amplitude, and the same signal now spans many quantization levels. Both panels show the step-staircase digitized output overlaid on the smooth true signal. A caption reads: "Resolution is a function of both the ADC bit depth and the ratio of signal amplitude to input range." Engineering diagram style, clean black and white with annotation labels.](../images/E2_W7_R2_image1.png)
*This image shows that DAQ resolution is not only a datasheet number; your range choice directly decides how much of that resolution the signal actually gets to use.

---

## Sample Rate: Nyquist Argument Plus Engineering Margin

The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem (covered in R1) establishes a lower bound: sample at least twice the highest frequency in your signal. In practice, that lower bound is a starting point, not a design target.

A signal sampled at exactly twice its highest frequency requires a perfect, infinitely sharp anti-aliasing filter — which does not exist in hardware. Real anti-aliasing filters have a transition band: a frequency range over which the attenuation rolls off gradually from passband to stopband. To accommodate this transition band, the practical rule is to sample at a rate **5× to 10× the signal bandwidth**, not 2×. This margin relaxes the filter requirements and provides buffer against uncertainty in the exact upper frequency of the signal.

When documenting your sample rate choice in the Tier 2 report, the required argument is: (1) the estimated signal bandwidth (from physical reasoning, not from the data), (2) the anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency, (3) the chosen sampling rate and the margin it provides over the Nyquist minimum, and (4) any known limitations if the margin is tight.

---

## Triggering and Synchronization

Many aerospace experiments involve transient events — a structure loaded to failure, a motor spun up to operating speed, a thermal cycle initiated. In these cases, data collection must be synchronized to the event.

**Software triggering** starts acquisition when a command is issued. The delay between the command and the first sample is on the order of milliseconds — acceptable for slow-varying signals, problematic for transient events. **Hardware triggering** starts acquisition when an analog voltage crosses a threshold or a digital line goes high, with sub-microsecond response. For any experiment where the timing of data collection relative to the physical event matters, hardware triggering is required.

**Pre-trigger buffers** allow the DAQ system to capture data that occurred before the trigger event — the system records continuously, and the trigger selects the retention window. This is essential for capturing the onset of a transient before the threshold condition is detected.

For example: if you want to capture the onset of a structural impact, hardware triggering on a load cell threshold ensures data collection starts the moment the load exceeds a set value, not a few milliseconds later when the computer catches up. For slowly varying signals (thermal measurements, quasi-static loading), software triggering is usually sufficient.

---

## A Worked Example: The Bit-Rollover Artifact

This example requires knowing that some sensors count events as unsigned integers (always positive, values 0 to 65,535) while many DAQ systems record data as signed integers (positive and negative). When a sensor wraps from its maximum count back to zero, a signed integer recording sees a large negative jump.

Consider a counter-based displacement sensor that outputs a count value from 0 to 65,535 (a 16-bit unsigned integer) representing a full rotation. The sensor counts up as the shaft rotates in one direction. If the shaft reverses just past the 65,535 count, the next reading is 0 — a rollover. When that value is recorded by a DAQ system that stores the output as a signed 16-bit integer, the jump from 65,535 to 0 appears in the data as a sudden, large negative-going step.

In a time-series plot, this step looks like a real physical event — a rapid reversal, a discontinuity, an impulse. It is not. It is a sensor counter rollover recorded by a DAQ system that was not configured to handle unsigned output. Only knowledge of the sensor's output format and awareness of the DAQ configuration reveals the artifact for what it is. Uncertainty analysis cannot identify this type of artifact — it looks like a physical reading that is within sensor range. The defense is documentation: reading the DAQ configuration against the sensor datasheet before collecting data, not after.

![A time-series plot showing sensor output versus time. The signal generally trends upward smoothly, then abruptly drops from near-maximum to near-zero at one time instant, then continues rising. The drop is labeled "Apparent reversal event." A callout box notes: "Sensor counter rollover: 65535 → 0, recorded as negative step in signed integer format. Physical shaft motion was continuous." The x-axis is labeled "Time (s)" and the y-axis "Sensor output (counts)." The smooth expected trajectory (without the rollover) is shown as a dashed continuation curve through the drop. Clear engineering plot style with annotation.](../images/E2_W7_R2_image2.png)
*This image helps you see how a data-system artifact can masquerade as a physical event if you do not understand how the sensor and acquisition chain encode the signal.

---

## Documentation Requirement

Every DAQ configuration choice must be documented in your Tier 2 report with justification, not just a list of settings. The required entries for each choice:

| Choice | What to document |
|---|---|
| Channel configuration | Single-ended or differential; reason |
| Input range | Value; why this range was chosen for the signal amplitude |
| Sample rate | Value; Nyquist argument; margin above minimum |
| Anti-aliasing filter cutoff | Value; relationship to signal bandwidth and sample rate |
| Triggering | Type; reason; pre-trigger buffer if applicable |
| Resolution | ADC bit depth; quantization step size; assessment of whether it limits the measurement |

A DAQ configuration section that lists settings without justification does not demonstrate that the choices were made defensibly. The goal is to show that you understood what each setting controls and chose it on physical grounds.

---

**The Takeaway:** DAQ configuration is not a setup task to complete before the real work begins. It is part of the measurement design, and every undocumented default is an undocumented assumption about your signal that may or may not be valid for your experiment.

| Channel configuration | Is the signal source floating or subject to ground loop noise? | Default differential; use single-ended only for large signals with shared ground and short cables |
| Input range | What is the expected signal amplitude? | Set to the smallest range that comfortably brackets the signal; do not accept ±10 V defaults for millivolt signals |
| Sample rate | What is the signal bandwidth, and what anti-aliasing margin is required? | Sample at 5× to 10× the signal bandwidth; document the Nyquist argument explicitly |
| Anti-aliasing filter cutoff | What is the transition band of the filter relative to the chosen sample rate? | Set cutoff at ≤ 0.4 × sample rate; document relationship to signal bandwidth |
| Triggering | Does the event onset need to be captured, or is the signal slowly varying? | Hardware trigger for transient events; software trigger acceptable for quasi-static signals |

Understanding those assumptions (channel configuration, resolution, sample rate, triggering) is not optional bookkeeping. Each choice encodes a constraint on what the system can faithfully represent, and each constraint contributes its own error source to your measurement. This reading is a reference document. Use it when setting up your Tier 2 experiment and again when drafting the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. Unlike the companion reading (R1), which builds physical intuition for aliasing and sampling, this reading is deliberately dense: it is meant to be consulted, not just read once.

---

## Channel Configuration: Single-Ended vs. Differential

Most DAQ systems offer two channel configuration modes, and the difference matters whenever noise or ground potential is a concern.

**Single-ended configuration** measures voltage between the signal line and a shared system ground. It uses twice as many channels for a given number of signals compared to differential, which makes it attractive when channel count is a constraint. The vulnerability: any noise voltage that appears between the sensor's ground reference and the DAQ system's ground reference is measured as signal. In environments with multiple power supplies, motors, or long cable runs, this ground-referencing error, called a **ground loop**, can swamp a low-level sensor signal.

**Differential configuration** measures voltage between two dedicated signal lines, rejecting any voltage that appears equally on both (called **common-mode rejection**). Differential inputs are the right choice whenever your signal source is floating, or whenever the signal amplitude is small enough that ground loop noise is a significant fraction of the signal magnitude. A **floating signal source** has no connection to the local measurement ground: battery-powered sensors and isolated transducers are common examples, and their reference point may drift relative to the DAQ ground. Most precision measurement applications — strain gauges, thermocouples, load cells — use differential configuration for this reason.

The practical decision: single-ended is acceptable when the signal is large (volts, not millivolts), cable runs are short, and all equipment shares a common ground. In any other case, default to differential.

---

## Resolution: Quantization Error and Its Contribution to Uncertainty

Every analog-to-digital converter (**ADC**) maps a continuous voltage range onto a finite number of discrete digital levels. A 12-bit ADC divides its input range into 2¹² = 4,096 discrete levels. A 16-bit ADC divides the same range into 65,536 levels. The voltage difference between adjacent levels is the **quantization step size** — also called the **least significant bit (LSB)** voltage.

For a ±10 V input range on a 12-bit ADC, the quantization step size is 20 V / 4,096 ≈ 4.9 mV. That is the smallest voltage change the system can detect. Any signal change smaller than one LSB is invisible — it either rounds up to the next level or rounds down. The resulting error is **quantization error**, and it contributes to the total measurement uncertainty.

For most well-designed experiments, quantization error is dominated by other error sources (sensor noise, thermal drift, amplifier noise). But there are important exceptions. If you are measuring a slowly varying, low-amplitude signal (structural creep, thermal expansion, very low-level acoustic vibration) and your input range is set far wider than the signal amplitude, the effective resolution shrinks dramatically. A signal that occupies only 1% of the ADC input range is being measured with roughly 100 times worse effective resolution than the ADC spec implies.

The practical decision: set the input range to the smallest value that comfortably brackets your expected signal. Do not accept ±10 V defaults when your signal is ±100 mV.

![A diagram illustrating quantization for two different ADC input range settings. On the left, a ±10V input range is divided into discrete levels, with a small signal occupying only the bottom fraction of the range — the signal is shown as a small-amplitude sine wave that spans only a few quantization levels. On the right, the input range is set to ±200 mV to match the signal amplitude, and the same signal now spans many quantization levels. Both panels show the step-staircase digitized output overlaid on the smooth true signal. A caption reads: "Resolution is a function of both the ADC bit depth and the ratio of signal amplitude to input range." Engineering diagram style, clean black and white with annotation labels.](../images/E2_W7_R2_image1.png)
*This image shows that DAQ resolution is not only a datasheet number; your range choice directly decides how much of that resolution the signal actually gets to use.

---

## Sample Rate: Nyquist Argument Plus Engineering Margin

The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem (covered in R1) establishes a lower bound: sample at least twice the highest frequency in your signal. In practice, that lower bound is a starting point, not a design target.

A signal sampled at exactly twice its highest frequency requires a perfect, infinitely sharp anti-aliasing filter — which does not exist in hardware. Real anti-aliasing filters have a transition band: a frequency range over which the attenuation rolls off gradually from passband to stopband. To accommodate this transition band, the practical rule is to sample at a rate **5× to 10× the signal bandwidth**, not 2×. This margin relaxes the filter requirements and provides buffer against uncertainty in the exact upper frequency of the signal.

When documenting your sample rate choice in the Tier 2 report, the required argument is: (1) the estimated signal bandwidth (from physical reasoning, not from the data), (2) the anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency, (3) the chosen sampling rate and the margin it provides over the Nyquist minimum, and (4) any known limitations if the margin is tight.

---

## Triggering and Synchronization

Many aerospace experiments involve transient events — a structure loaded to failure, a motor spun up to operating speed, a thermal cycle initiated. In these cases, data collection must be synchronized to the event.

**Software triggering** starts acquisition when a command is issued. The delay between the command and the first sample is on the order of milliseconds — acceptable for slow-varying signals, problematic for transient events. **Hardware triggering** starts acquisition when an analog voltage crosses a threshold or a digital line goes high, with sub-microsecond response. For any experiment where the timing of data collection relative to the physical event matters, hardware triggering is required.

**Pre-trigger buffers** allow the DAQ system to capture data that occurred before the trigger event — the system records continuously, and the trigger selects the retention window. This is essential for capturing the onset of a transient before the threshold condition is detected.

For example: if you want to capture the onset of a structural impact, hardware triggering on a load cell threshold ensures data collection starts the moment the load exceeds a set value, not a few milliseconds later when the computer catches up. For slowly varying signals (thermal measurements, quasi-static loading), software triggering is usually sufficient.

---

## A Worked Example: The Bit-Rollover Artifact

This example requires knowing that some sensors count events as unsigned integers (always positive, values 0 to 65,535) while many DAQ systems record data as signed integers (positive and negative). When a sensor wraps from its maximum count back to zero, a signed integer recording sees a large negative jump.

Consider a counter-based displacement sensor that outputs a count value from 0 to 65,535 (a 16-bit unsigned integer) representing a full rotation. The sensor counts up as the shaft rotates in one direction. If the shaft reverses just past the 65,535 count, the next reading is 0 — a rollover. When that value is recorded by a DAQ system that stores the output as a signed 16-bit integer, the jump from 65,535 to 0 appears in the data as a sudden, large negative-going step.

In a time-series plot, this step looks like a real physical event — a rapid reversal, a discontinuity, an impulse. It is not. It is a sensor counter rollover recorded by a DAQ system that was not configured to handle unsigned output. Only knowledge of the sensor's output format and awareness of the DAQ configuration reveals the artifact for what it is. Uncertainty analysis cannot identify this type of artifact — it looks like a physical reading that is within sensor range. The defense is documentation: reading the DAQ configuration against the sensor datasheet before collecting data, not after.

![A time-series plot showing sensor output versus time. The signal generally trends upward smoothly, then abruptly drops from near-maximum to near-zero at one time instant, then continues rising. The drop is labeled "Apparent reversal event." A callout box notes: "Sensor counter rollover: 65535 → 0, recorded as negative step in signed integer format. Physical shaft motion was continuous." The x-axis is labeled "Time (s)" and the y-axis "Sensor output (counts)." The smooth expected trajectory (without the rollover) is shown as a dashed continuation curve through the drop. Clear engineering plot style with annotation.](../images/E2_W7_R2_image2.png)
*This image helps you see how a data-system artifact can masquerade as a physical event if you do not understand how the sensor and acquisition chain encode the signal.

---

## Documentation Requirement

Every DAQ configuration choice must be documented in your Tier 2 report with justification, not just a list of settings. The required entries for each choice:

| Choice | What to document |
|---|---|
| Channel configuration | Single-ended or differential; reason |
| Input range | Value; why this range was chosen for the signal amplitude |
| Sample rate | Value; Nyquist argument; margin above minimum |
| Anti-aliasing filter cutoff | Value; relationship to signal bandwidth and sample rate |
| Triggering | Type; reason; pre-trigger buffer if applicable |
| Resolution | ADC bit depth; quantization step size; assessment of whether it limits the measurement |

A DAQ configuration section that lists settings without justification does not demonstrate that the choices were made defensibly. The goal is to show that you understood what each setting controls and chose it on physical grounds.

---

**The Takeaway:** DAQ configuration is not a setup task to complete before the real work begins. It is part of the measurement design, and every undocumented default is an undocumented assumption about your signal that may or may not be valid for your experiment.

activity_meta.title: Nyquist Sampling, Aliasing & Signal Bandwidth
activity_meta.purpose: Introduces the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem and aliasing as the fundamental constraints on any digital measurement system. Covers signal bandwidth as the property of the physical phenomenon being measured (not a property of the sensor), the aliasing mechanism — how undersampled signals fold back into the measured spectrum — and the practical consequences for aerospace measurements where signal content may span many orders of frequency. Students work through examples to develop intuition for when sampling rate matters critically.

activity_meta.title: Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain
activity_meta.purpose: Covers the signal conditioning chain between a physical sensor and a DAQ digitizer: amplification (gain and offset), filtering (low-pass anti-aliasing filter — purpose, order, cutoff selection), and noise sources (thermal noise, electromagnetic interference, ground loops). The design rationale for anti-aliasing filter cutoff selection is emphasized: the cutoff must be below the Nyquist frequency of the chosen sampling rate, but not so aggressive that it attenuates signal content of interest. Students see that signal conditioning choices are not technical defaults — they encode assumptions about signal structure and acceptable data quality. **Analog vs. digital filtering contrast (5–7 min segment):** This lecture should include an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles. The key message: anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain before the ADC — digital filters applied post-digitization cannot retroactively correct aliasing because the information is already lost. Additionally, digital filters introduce phase delay, passband ripple, and potential edge-case artifacts (e.g., ringing near sharp transitions) that are non-obvious. Students do not need to know how to design analog or digital filters — this is awareness-level content. The takeaway: signal conditioning choices must be made before sampling, not after, and the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision that must be documented in any credible experimental report.

activity_meta.title: Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement
activity_meta.purpose: Intuition-building pre-class read for W7's two lectures. Approaches Nyquist and aliasing from a physical intuition angle: what does it actually mean for a signal to be "undersampled," and why does the resulting artifact look like a real signal rather than obvious noise? Uses the stroboscopic wheel and helicopter blade visual analogies to make aliasing concrete before any mathematics appears. Covers signal bandwidth as a property of the physical phenomenon (not the sensor), and frames the sampling rate decision as a modeling commitment — you are claiming, before you sample, that your signal has no content above half your sampling rate. If that claim is wrong, the data is silently corrupted. The reading stops before filter design and DAQ configuration — those are covered in the companion reading (R2) and lectures. Its job is to ensure students arrive at the W7 lectures already understanding *why* these choices matter, so lecture time can address *how* to make them defensibly.

activity_meta.title: DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference
activity_meta.purpose: Reference-style companion to the W7 signal conditioning lecture, designed to be read alongside or immediately after class. Covers the DAQ configuration choices that experimentalists must document and justify: single-ended vs. differential channel configuration (ground loop implications), resolution (quantization error and its contribution to uncertainty), sample rate selection (Nyquist argument plus margin), triggering and synchronization, and practical setup documentation. The central message is that DAQ configuration is a written, justifiable design decision — not a lab default to accept without inspection. Students are expected to reference this reading when completing the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. **Bit rollover example:** Includes a concrete walkthrough of a bit-rollover or counter-overflow artifact that produces a plausible-looking oscillatory signal in the recorded data — where only uncertainty bounds and physical reasoning allow the artifact to be identified as non-physical. This is the key illustration for why DAQ configuration choices introduce their own error sources and why uncertainty analysis is the mechanism for distinguishing physical behavior from instrumentation artifacts. Unlike R1, this reading is reference-dense rather than narrative — students should expect to return to it when drafting their report.

activity_meta.title: Signal Examination & DAQ Configuration Analysis
activity_meta.purpose: Students examine the sensor suite and DAQ configuration documentation for their Tier 2 canned experiment. Using MATLAB, they visualize the measured signal in both time domain and frequency domain (using FFT), identify signal bandwidth from the spectrum, evaluate whether the current sampling rate satisfies the Nyquist criterion with adequate margin, and assess whether the anti-aliasing filter cutoff is appropriate. They propose and justify any configuration changes that would improve data quality. This analysis feeds directly into the DAQ configuration section of the Tier 2 group report and into the improvement proposal (Section 6, category b of `TIER2_assignment.md`). ## 3. Bridge to Next Week Students now understand how to select and justify sampling and filtering choices. Week 8 returns to Canned Experiment 1: data is released, and groups must compare their committed prediction to measured outcomes, interpret the discrepancy structurally, and begin the full Tier 2 analysis that culminates in the group report.

modules.label: E2 Week 3 (Course Week 8)
modules.headline: E2 Week 8: Model Adequacy, Discrepancy & Calibration Revisited
learning_goals: Interpret model-experiment discrepancy as diagnostic information about model-form limitations rather than experimental failure — CLO 3
Distinguish between parametric uncertainty (imprecise knowledge of model parameters), model-form error (structural inadequacy of the model), and measurement error — CLO 2, CLO 3
Inspect residuals structurally — identifying systematic trends vs. random scatter — and articulate what each pattern implies about model adequacy — CLO 3
Perform a calibration status review with reference to instrument documentation and distinguish calibration activities from validation activities — CLO 1, CLO 3
Use experimental results to propose specific improvements to a predictive model — identifying what model parameters or assumptions the data reveals are inaccurate or incomplete — CLO 3
Begin the Tier 2 group report analysis sections using the released Canned Experiment 1 data — CLO 5
modules.summary: Phase I taught students to ask whether model and experiment agree. Phase II asks a harder question: when they disagree, what does that tell you about the model? Week 8 introduces model-form error, sensitivity to boundary condition assumptions, and the distinction between discrepancy as failure and discrepancy as diagnostic information. Students learn to interpret residuals structurally — not as noise to explain aw...

lectures.title: Model-Form Error & Discrepancy as Diagnostic Information

variants.label: Markdown
variants.text:
slide 1: Title Slide
Title: Model-Form Error & Discrepancy as Diagnostic Information
Talking Point: This lecture addresses what I consider the hardest conceptual shift of Phase II, and I want to state it plainly at the start. When the data comes back from your Tier 2 experiment and it does not match your prediction perfectly — and it will not — your first instinct is going to be to explain the discrepancy away. To say the sensors drifted, or something went wrong with the setup, or there was noise. That instinct is wrong. Discrepancy is not failure. Discrepancy is data about the model, and the structure of the discrepancy tells you something specific and true about what the model is missing. That is the subject of this lecture: learning to read the gap between prediction and measurement as information rather than embarrassment.

slide 2: Three Types of Discrepancy — A Taxonomy That Matters
**Residual** = predicted value − measured value at a single data point. A collection of residuals across all data points is the diagnostic fingerprint of your model's limitations.
(Image: A three-row table with clear visual differentiation. Column headers: "Type," "What it looks like in residuals," "Physical interpretation." Row 1 — "Parametric uncertainty": residuals are randomly scattered around zero with no trend, all within the uncertainty band. Accompanying mini-plot shows scatter plot of residuals vs. independent variable — points distributed randomly, bounded by ±uncertainty band. Label: "Model form is adequate — wrong parameter values." Row 2 — "Model-form error": residuals show a systematic trend — increasing with the independent variable, or consistently positive in one range and negative in another. Mini-plot shows a clear slope or curve in the residuals. Label: "Model is structurally missing a physical effect." Row 3 — "Measurement error": residuals are randomly scattered but exceed the uncertainty band consistently in one direction. Mini-plot shows scatter above the band. Label: "Systematic bias in measurement, not in the model." The three rows are color-coded: Row 1 in gold, Row 2 in orange-red, Row 3 in gray. The visual distinction between random scatter and systematic trend is the central teaching point.)
Talking Point: Residual structure is a diagnostic clue, not a one-to-one label. Use seven candidate classes: random measurement variation; known measurement bias; setup or installation effect; uncontrolled or confounding input; data-reduction error; parameter uncertainty; and model-form discrepancy. A structured trend establishes inadequacy in the combined model–experiment system. It may support a model-form explanation, but setup, input, reduction, sensor, and parameter hypotheses must be considered. State a preferred explanation only after citing evidence, and name at least one competing hypothesis and a check that could distinguish them.

slide 3: Residuals as the Diagnostic Signal
(Image: Four residual plots in a two-by-two grid, each showing residuals (y-axis: prediction minus measurement) vs. an independent variable (x-axis). Plot 1: "Random scatter — within uncertainty band." Residuals distributed randomly, all bounded by dashed ±σ lines. Caption: "Consistent with measurement noise — model may be adequate." Plot 2: "Systematic linear trend." Residuals increase linearly with the independent variable — clear slope. Caption: "Model missing a first-order physical effect." Plot 3: "Nonlinear systematic pattern." Residuals curve upward at both ends and are near zero at the center. Caption: "Model misses nonlinearity or boundary effect." Plot 4: "Constant bias." Residuals are randomly scattered but consistently displaced above zero beyond the uncertainty band. Caption: "Systematic measurement error — not a model problem." All four plots use the same axis scales for direct comparison. The dashed uncertainty band is present in all four plots as a reference.)
Talking Point: These four residual plots are the interpretive toolkit for your Tier 2 data analysis. When you plot your residuals — prediction minus measurement, for every data point — the shape of that plot is the primary diagnostic signal. Random scatter within the uncertainty band is good news for the model: it means the structure of the physics is probably right, and the scatter you see is consistent with the measurement precision you quantified in the prediction document. A systematic linear trend means the model is missing a physical effect that scales with the independent variable — if you are testing a structural beam and the residuals increase with applied load, the model may be missing compliance or geometric nonlinearity. A curved pattern — near zero at intermediate conditions, large at the extremes — is a classic signature of a small-deflection or small-angle assumption breaking down at the limits of the operating range. And a constant bias means you have a systematic measurement error: a sensor that needs recalibration, or a zero-offset that was never corrected. Every one of these patterns has a specific physical interpretation, and your job in the Tier 2 analysis is to name which one you see and explain what it implies.

slide 4: Parametric Uncertainty — When the Form Is Right but the Numbers Are Wrong
(Image: A side-by-side comparison of two model predictions vs. the same experimental data. Left panel: "Original prediction" — a dashed prediction curve that consistently overestimates the measured data by a roughly constant multiplicative factor. The residuals at the bottom are flat (no trend) but consistently negative — indicating the model overpredicts everywhere. Right panel: "Revised prediction after parameter correction" — the prediction curve after the stiffness parameter is corrected using the data as a reference; the revised curve passes through the data scatter within uncertainty bounds. A caption reads: "Parametric uncertainty diagnosis: systematic bias with no trend → correct the parameter, not the model structure." The two panels have the same axis scales, with measured data shown as scatter points in both.)
Talking Point: When the residuals are flat — no trend, consistently negative or positive by a roughly constant amount — you have a parametric error. The model's structure is correct: it can produce the right shape of response, but it is using the wrong numerical value for one of the input parameters. In E2, the most common source of this pattern is an input parameter that you estimated from a textbook or datasheet that turns out to be different from the actual hardware — a material stiffness that has manufacturing variation, a mass that was estimated from a nominal drawing rather than measured directly, an aerodynamic coefficient that is sensitive to surface finish. The diagnostic step is to ask: which parameter, if adjusted by a physically plausible amount, would close the gap between the model and the data? If a small adjustment to one parameter brings the curves into agreement, that is strong evidence that the model form is correct and the parameter is the issue. This distinction matters because the fix for a parametric error — better parameter characterization — is completely different from the fix for a model-form error.

slide 5: Model-Form Error — When the Structure Is Wrong
(Image: A single graph showing a physical model prediction curve (linear, monotonically increasing) and experimental data scatter (which follows the prediction at low values of the independent variable but deviates nonlinearly at high values — the data bends upward, suggesting stiffening behavior not in the model). A residual plot below the main graph shows the characteristic systematic trend: residuals near zero at low independent variable values, growing positive at high values. An annotation points to the residual trend: "Systematic positive trend above x = 3 N — model missing geometric stiffening." A second annotation at the top: "This gap is not noise. The uncertainty band is shown. The trend is outside it." The uncertainty band (dashed lines) is present in both the main plot and the residual plot for reference.)
Talking Point: Model-form error is the most informative type of discrepancy because it is telling you something true about the physics that your model does not capture. When the residuals show a systematic trend — they increase with the independent variable, or they are consistently of one sign in one operating regime and the opposite sign in another — the model cannot produce that pattern through any choice of parameters. The model is structurally incomplete. In the example here, the model predicts a linear response and the data agrees at small values, but at larger values the data stiffens in a way the linear model cannot follow. The residuals grow systematically. This is a textbook signature of geometric nonlinearity: the linear beam model assumes small displacements, and at large displacements the geometry changes enough to increase effective stiffness. The model is not wrong in the absolute sense — it is inadequate beyond the small-displacement regime, for the intended use of predicting behavior at large loads. This is exactly the kind of diagnostic information that makes a model more useful: you now know the regime in which the linear model is adequate and the regime in which a geometric nonlinearity term is required. The same pattern appears in aerospace contexts: measured drag coefficient shows a positive trend with Reynolds number that the model does not predict — this is a model-form error indicating that the laminar-to-turbulent transition is not captured in the model's drag expression. Different domain, same diagnostic logic.

slide 6: Boundary Condition Realism — The Assumption You Did Not Know You Were Making
(Image: A two-panel illustration comparing two structural configurations. Left panel: "Idealized model: fully fixed boundary condition." Shows a cantilever beam with the left end drawn as a rigid, built-in wall, with the label "Model assumption: zero rotation and zero displacement at support." Right panel: "Real experimental setup: imperfect fixity." Shows the same beam but the support has a small angular deviation — the label reads "Actual boundary: slight rotation compliance — the support is not perfectly rigid." A comparison of residuals from each assumption is shown below as two short residual plots: the rigid assumption produces residuals with a systematic trend, while a flexible-support model reduces the trend substantially. Caption: "Boundary condition assumptions are often the dominant source of model-form error in structural experiments.")
Talking Point: One of the most common sources of model-form error in structural and mechanical experiments is not the governing equation itself — it is the boundary conditions. When you build a model, you make assumptions about how the system is supported, loaded, and constrained. In a textbook, boundaries are perfect: a fixed support is perfectly rigid, a pinned joint has zero friction, a load is applied at a mathematical point. In a real experiment, none of these idealizations are exactly true. A support that is intended to be fully fixed may have slight rotational compliance. A joint that is supposed to be pinned may have friction that introduces a restoring moment. These deviations from the ideal boundary condition appear in the residuals as systematic trends — typically largest near the boundary, decreasing with distance from it. When you see this pattern, the diagnostic question is: which of my boundary condition assumptions is least realistic? If a small adjustment to the boundary stiffness or the load application point closes the residual trend, that is the culprit. Naming this in your Tier 2 analysis — identifying which assumption was violated and what it implies for the model — is the highest-quality type of discrepancy analysis.

slide 7: Measurement Error vs. Model Error — A Case for Uncertainty Bounds
(Image: A comparison of two scenarios, presented as two MATLAB scatter plots side by side. Left: "Scenario A — discrepancy within uncertainty bounds." Measured data scatter plotted around a model prediction line. Horizontal dashed uncertainty lines at ±σ. All data points within the band. Caption: "No grounds to claim model failure — discrepancy is consistent with measurement precision." Right: "Scenario B — systematic bias beyond uncertainty bounds." Same model prediction line, but measured data is consistently below it, outside the uncertainty band at nearly every point. Caption: "Systematic bias: not random scatter. Source investigation required." Below the two panels, a question prompt: "Is the cause of the bias in the model, the measurement, or both? How would you distinguish?" A dotted arrow between the panels labeled "The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis — it defines what random scatter looks like.")
Talking Point: The uncertainty band you computed in your prediction document is not just a formality — it is your null hypothesis. It answers the question: what would the residuals look like if the only differences between prediction and data were due to measurement noise at the level you estimated? If the residuals are within the band, you cannot claim a meaningful disagreement — the data is consistent with your model within the precision of your measurement. If the residuals are consistently outside the band, something is wrong in a non-random way. The question then is: is the systematic bias coming from the model or from the measurement? To distinguish them, you need to ask whether the bias is constant across different operating conditions, different sensors, or different setups. If you repeat the measurement with a different sensor type or a different calibration reference and the bias shifts, it is a measurement error. If the bias is consistent regardless of how you measure, it is more likely a model error. This is exactly why the calibration status review — which we will cover in Lecture 2 today — is a required section of the Tier 2 group report: it is the systematic investigation of whether your measurement has a known bias source.

slide 8: Using Discrepancy to Improve the Model — The Experimentally Informed Model
(Image: A four-stage circular process diagram. Stage 1 (top): "Predictive model — physical equations, committed prediction." Arrow to Stage 2 (right): "Experiment — data collected." Arrow to Stage 3 (bottom): "Discrepancy analysis — classify residual pattern as parametric, model-form, or measurement." Arrow to Stage 4 (left): "Model revision proposal — specific update to the model based on what the discrepancy revealed." Arrow back to Stage 1 labeled "Updated model — inform future predictions." The diagram is rendered in the course color scheme with a clean circular layout. A gold star icon appears on the Stage 4 box with the label: "This is the required deliverable in the Tier 2 report — not just 'the model was close enough'.")
Talking Point: Returning to the experimentally informed model concept that was introduced at the start of E2: the goal of running a canned experiment in Phase II is not to validate the model in a binary sense and move on. The goal is to use the discrepancy analysis to propose a specific improvement to the model. Stage 1 is your committed prediction — you built a model, derived a prediction, and committed to it. Stage 2 is the experiment — you collected data. Stage 3 is the discrepancy analysis — you classified the residual pattern as parametric uncertainty, model-form error, or measurement error, and you named the physical cause. Stage 4 is the model revision proposal: based on what the residual pattern revealed, what specific change to the model — a different boundary condition assumption, an added nonlinear term, a corrected parameter value — would reduce the discrepancy? This is what the Tier 2 report asks you to provide. Not "the model was close enough" and not "there was some error in the experiment." A specific, physically justified proposal for how the model could be improved based on what the data told you.

slide 9: Applying the Taxonomy to Your Tier 2 Data
(Image: A structured worksheet-style layout. Three decision steps arranged vertically. Step 1: "Plot residuals — prediction minus measurement — versus the independent variable." Sub-question: "Are the residuals random or systematic?" Two branches: "Random → Step 2." "Systematic → Model-form error or boundary condition issue → name the physical effect." Step 2 (from "Random"): "Compare residual magnitude to uncertainty band." Sub-question: "Are residuals within the band?" Two branches: "Yes → Parametric uncertainty or adequate agreement." "No, consistently biased → Systematic measurement or parameter error." Step 3: "If constant bias beyond band — perform calibration status check." "Was the sensor within calibration interval? Was a zero offset correction applied?" A footer note reads: "This three-step process is the required analysis structure for Section 3A of the Tier 2 group report.")
Talking Point: Start with the residual plot and decide whether the pattern is random or structured. Then work through the seven candidate classes rather than jumping from "trend" to "model-form error." Check calibration and known bias, setup and installation, uncontrolled inputs, data-reduction code, parameter values, and finally whether the model form can reproduce the observed pattern. Your report must support the preferred explanation and name at least one viable alternative plus a discriminating check.

slide 10: A Model That Disagrees Informatively Is More Valuable Than One That Agrees Approximately
(Image: A two-row comparison. Row 1 header: "Case A — approximate agreement, no diagnostic information." Shows a prediction curve overlaid on data scatter that roughly matches everywhere, residuals small and without trend. A note reads: "Conclusion: 'Model is close enough.' What was learned: nothing specific." Row 2 header: "Case B — structured disagreement, specific diagnostic." Shows a prediction curve that clearly overestimates at high values, with residuals showing a systematic trend. A note reads: "Conclusion: 'Model overestimates stiffening at large deflections — geometric nonlinearity not captured.' What was learned: the regime of model adequacy, a specific model improvement target, and the physical mechanism responsible." A banner at the bottom: "Case B is more scientifically valuable even though the model is 'less accurate.' The structure of the disagreement is the information.")
Talking Point: I want to leave you with a reframing that I think is genuinely important for how you approach engineering work. A model that agrees within uncertainty bounds has demonstrated adequacy — that is a success. A structured disagreement is a different kind of success: it reveals specifically where the model breaks down and why. Both outcomes have value; only one points toward model improvement. Approximate agreement everywhere means you learned that the model is not obviously wrong — you have limited information about where it breaks down, under what conditions, or why. Structured disagreement means you have learned something specific: the model is missing geometric stiffening above a certain deflection, or the aerodynamic drag coefficient is approximately 30% higher than the textbook value at these Reynolds numbers, or the support compliance reduces the apparent natural frequency by 15% compared to the fixed-end assumption. These are specific, actionable findings that make the model more useful for its intended purpose. The test engineer who delivers a report saying "the model is close enough" has done less valuable work than the one who delivers a report saying "the model is adequate within this operating regime, and here is the specific modification required to extend its validity." That is what Phase II is teaching you to do.

slide 11: Closure
Discrepancy between a model and experimental data is not embarrassment — it is information, and the structure of the discrepancy is the diagnostic signal. Parametric uncertainty produces random scatter within the uncertainty band and calls for better parameter characterization. Model-form error produces systematic trends in the residuals and calls for identifying the missing physical effect. Systematic measurement error produces consistent bias beyond the uncertainty band and calls for a calibration status review. The uncertainty band you computed in your prediction document is not a formality — it is your null hypothesis, defining what random scatter looks like. A model that disagrees in a structured way is telling you something true about the physics, and a test engineer who can read that structure and translate it into a specific model improvement proposal is delivering value that cannot be replaced by running more trials or using a better sensor. In Lecture 2 this week, we will go deeper on calibration — not just as a procedure, but as a source of uncertainty that propagates into your measurement outcome and must be documented to support a credible validation claim.

## Agent Notes
- Content is grounded entirely in the W8 L1 blueprint purpose statement: model-form error, parametric uncertainty, boundary condition realism, systematic residual interpretation.
- The three-type taxonomy (parametric / model-form / measurement) is introduced in slide 2 and applied throughout — this is the required analytical vocabulary for Section 3A of the Tier 2 report.
- The experimentally informed model concept (slide 8) is a required E2 deliverable orientation per instructor_clarification_notes.md item 5.
- Slide 10 explicitly frames structured disagreement as more valuable than approximate agreement — echoing the lecture narrative spine from Conceptual_Arc.md.
- Connects forward to W8 L2 (calibration status review) in closure slide.
- No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 3 primary treatment.

lectures.summary: Establishes the hardest conceptual shift of E2: discrepancy between model and experiment is not failure to be explained away — it is data about the model. Covers parametric uncertainty (you know the form is right but the parameters are imprecise), model-form error (the functional form itself is inadequate), and boundary condition realism (assumptions about system boundaries or inputs that were wrong). Students work through examples where systematic trends in residuals reveal which type of inadequacy is present. The framing: a model that disagrees with data in a structured way is more informative than one that agrees approximately everywhere.
